## Thread: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement — 7 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2003-10](2003-10.md#t-395a4eaf9f)._

#### ↳ Re: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-01T06:09:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t6Iob.10649$Px2.4368@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227>`

```
What compiler do you mean by "VS-COBOL"
  - OS/VS COBOL
  - VS COBOL II

or some other IBM compiler?

OS/VS COBOL programs are (and can only BE) AMODE(24) and RMODE(24).  If you
try and change these (via a Link-edit override) you will get ABENDs at
run-time.

 VS COBOL II *does* support AMODE(any) and RMODE((31) programs - but these
are determined by various compiler options such a RES, RENT, and DATA.

Both of those compilers are "long out of support" by IBM.

The current IBM compiler *does* include an RMODE compiler option - but ALL
of its code is AMODE(31)

   ***

I think you need to "dig further" into exactly what you are after to get a
"good reply"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <usrr@mac.kat>
- **Date:** 2003-11-01T19:57:55+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<011120031957556611%usrr@mac.kat>`
- **References:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227> <t6Iob.10649$Px2.4368@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Lets clear up some confusion on AMODE 31/24.

Firstly AMODE 31 just means that the program is capable of accepting 31
bit addresses for it's variables. It does not a-priori mean that the
program will in fact use 31 bit addresses at run time.

THAT is accomplished by the Language Environment settings for the
program. By default, these will probably be set to allocate storage
above the line for AMODE 31 and below the line for AMODE 24.

This can be changed at runtime:
Specify the following parms in the JCL parameter field;
   ,PARM='STACK(,,BELOW),BELOWHEAP(8K,4K,FREE)/pgm parms'

If the COBOL program is the first program executed (as it must be for
the above to work) then this will force the entire enclave to obtain
storage below the line for all Working-Storage and Local-Storage
assignments.

Also you don't have to run the entire program with below the line
storage. You can leave the defaults as the are and use Language
Environment calls to Allocate a HEAP below the line. Then after you
allocate some storage from this heap and assign it to a Linkage Section
dataname, you can then move the appropriate information into this area
and call the assembler routine using this below the line storage.

All of this is explained in great detail in the Language Environment
manuals, which I would have thought that any mainframe programmer would
have read cover to cover.

In article <t6Iob.10649$Px2.4368@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> What compiler do you mean by "VS-COBOL"
>   - OS/VS COBOL
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> "good reply"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-01T21:25:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bxVob.706$qh2.269@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227> <t6Iob.10649$Px2.4368@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <011120031957556611%usrr@mac.kat>`

```
Sorry - but your description of AMODE (vs RMODE) is confusing at best.

AMODE is a linkage editor (or binder) attribute.  You can "mark" a program
as AMODE(31) - even if it is not programmed correctly to handle such data.
Furthermore, AMODE (and RMODE) both PRE-date LE; they were introduced with
MVS/XA.

For a program with CORRECTLY set AMODE and RMODE attributes, the following
is true:

1) AMODE(24) - the program only (correctly) understands 24-bit addresses
(for data)
2) AMODE(31) - the program "understands" any 24- or 31-bit address (but not
"above the bar 64-bit addresses)
3) RMODE(24) - the program's INSTRUCTIONS must be loaded below the 16M line
4) RMODE(ANY) - the program's instructions may be loaded anywhere (below the
2G bar) and will execute correctly

  ***

OS/VS COBOL programs *must* be AMODE(24) and RMODE(24) to run - regardless
of whether using the original (now unsupported) OS/VS COBOL run-time or the
LE run-time (and link-edit) libraries

VS COBOL II - programs could have almost any valid combination of AMODE and
RMODE

(currently supported) Enterprise COBOL programs are ALWAYS AMODE(31) which
means that they "understand" 31-bit data addresses - regardless of any
compiler options that are set.  The RMODE for both RENT (compiler option,
not link-edit option) and NORENT programs may be either RMODE(ANY) *or*
RMODE(24) - and this is set by the (relatively new) RMODE compiler option.

It is "valid" to change a link-edit option DOWNWARD (e.g. RMODE(ANY) ->
RMODE(24)   or AMODE(31) -> AMODE(24) ) but not vice versa.  There are even
times that you might want to do this.

If you compile any VS COBOL II or later RENT program with DATA(24), that
means that its working-storage is allocated below the 16M line.  For COBOL
compilers that have LOCAL-STORAGE, where that is allocated is determined by
the LE STACK compiler option.  All enterprise COBOL programs (regardless of
compiler options) can understand LINKAGE section items above the 16M line
(but below the 2G bar).

For LE references on "restrictions" on LE (not linkage-editor/binder
restrictions), see:

  ALL31

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/2.3.4

  HEAP

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/2.3.24

  STACK

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/2.3.52

For a discussion of when/how to override AMODE/RMODE  Linkage Editor
options, see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igymg202/APPENDIX1.10

"Joseph Katnic" <usrr@mac.kat> wrote in message
news:011120031957556611%usrr@mac.kat...
> Lets clear up some confusion on AMODE 31/24.
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> > OS/VS COBOL programs are (and can only BE) AMODE(24) and RMODE(24).  If
you
> > try and change these (via a Link-edit override) you will get ABENDs at
> > run-time.
> >
> >  VS COBOL II *does* support AMODE(any) and RMODE((31) programs - but
these
> > are determined by various compiler options such a RES, RENT, and DATA.
> >
> > Both of those compilers are "long out of support" by IBM.
> >
> > The current IBM compiler *does* include an RMODE compiler option - but
ALL
> > of its code is AMODE(31)
> >
> >    ***
> >
> > I think you need to "dig further" into exactly what you are after to get
a
> > "good reply"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <usrr@mac.kat>
- **Date:** 2003-11-02T20:41:50+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<021120032041508126%usrr@mac.kat>`
- **References:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227> <t6Iob.10649$Px2.4368@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <011120031957556611%usrr@mac.kat> <bxVob.706$qh2.269@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Which all this gobbledegook reduces to :
1. Compile all your programs to run with the biggest address space
AMODE(31). The RMODE is only used to determine where the program will
be loaded. The Current Supported Compiler will automatically give you
RMODE(ANY). The defaults are AMODE(31) RMODE(ANY).
2. Allocate storage below the line for any routines that need it.
This can be accomplished by LE runtime options or via programmer
control using the LE call interface.

## Thread: A simulation problem - help anyone? — 7 msgs

### A simulation problem - help anyone?

- **From:** "Easy" <nu@nu.nu>
- **Date:** 2003-11-01T11:26:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vq7pbdj4anqm7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Anyone want to take a crack at this problem?

At a carnival game, the mark pays $1 to roll four balls at five holes for
scores of 5, 4, 3, 2, 1.  The odds of hitting the 5-hole is 1/15;  2/15 for
the 4-hole; 3/15 for the 3-hole; 4/15 for the 2-hole; and 5/15 for the
1-hole.  A score of 13-15 wins a Bitty Bear; and a score of 16-20 wins a Big
Bear.  If Bitty Bears cost $4 and Big Bears cost $8, for every dollar the
carney spends, what will be the take?
```

In article <3FA306AE.C4486765@mb.sympatico.ca>,
 Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> jce wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

I think the point of all the newer verbs (evaluate, perform, etc) is 
that goto is no longer needed to produce the constructs in question.

When you decide you want a bowl of chicken soup, do you start by raising 
your own chicken from an egg?  Or do you use the kind from your local 
market?

Rolling your own with GOTO is certainly possible, even possible to do 
right and well.  But it introduces a very ugly opportunith for things to 
be done wrong and ill in future maintenance and/or by lazy or unskilled 
initial programmers.

Evaluate does not come with that baggage.

I suppose what I am saying is that it takes EFFORT to do GOTO correctly.

It takse EFFORT to do EVALUATE incorrectly.
```

My stand on all these arguments comes from having done maintenance on everything under the

other is that the program has to work!); and : "easy-to-follow" is not necessarily the same

##### ↳ ↳ Re: need help with Norcom's GUIscreenio and Netexpress

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-02T21:39:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gosaqvgbp7t96h9aa70mqaf3hgqqqgm1rh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <3FA56184.F97959CA@shaw.ca>`

```
On Sun, 02 Nov 2003 19:58:49 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>You might *just* have more luck if you sign up for M/F's Answer Exchange (a freebie)
>and ask your question under N/E there - go to microfocus.com and search for Answer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>what you have also tried as alternatives.
----
Jimmy,

Your suggestion regarding the Answer Exchange is a good one; I'll add
that to our documentation.  Thanks.

I think we have this resolved, although I haven't yet heard back from
Charlie.  A large percentage of our users (dating back to 1999) run
Micro Focus compilers, so it's just a getting-started problem.

Just for some background, the only compiler-specific issue involved
with GUI ScreenIO is the CALL interface to our runtime DLL.  We
created it so that it can be called using the standard Windows system
conventions.

Rather than provide a separate object library for every COBOL
compiler, we chose to provide the source code for an intermediate
COBOL module that is coded so as to call our runtime DLL using the
conventions it requires.  This amounts to all of five or six lines of
code.  You just compile the version for your own compiler, and you're
off to the races.  

The usual problem is that a new user made an error decommenting the
correct code for his compiler in the interface module, which is
generally found/fixed in short order.  

The other common problem is that the integrated development needs to
be told about our object library so that it does the LINK correctly
(just as would be required for any other package), and it is sometimes
difficult to find where these settings must be made.  IDEs are great
once they're set up, but I occasionally feel they hide a lot of stuff
that developers really should be aware of...

And while we're at it, I seem to recall that you took a look at GS
when we first released it in '99, have you looked at the latest?
We've come a very long way since those days.

Regards,
Kevin
Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

Thanks for the suggestion Jimmy.  We are fully licensed with support for
Netexpress but I was hoping that one of the regulars here had tried the new
GUI tool, rather than relying on MF to help me debug a third party tool..

No, Kevin.  The pointers that John, of your office, provided were no more
than what I had read I your documentation.  They did not resolve the
problem.

Just in case here is someone lurking....

I edited the GS.CBL stub program to include the Microfocus specific code.
That was simply adding "CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS WINAPI." to SPECIAL-NAMES  and
using the following CALL statements:

        CALL WINAPI 'GS32'       USING BY REFERENCE A B C D.
        GOBACK.
        ENTRY 'GSWINAPI'         USING W A B C D E F.
        CALL WINAPI 'GSWAPI'     USING BY REFERENCE W A B C D E F.
        GOBACK.

I built a new project with default properties in order to compile GS into a
DLL.  The defaults were modified to add the word LITLINK to the project
directives under the project properties.  I added GS32.LIB to build
settings, link, advanced, link with these libs.  The project compiled and
linked without error, producing GS.DLL.

I used the sample program TESTPAN.COB renaming it to TESTPAN.CBL and
renaming the copy modules with .CPY extensions.  I built a project with all
default settings.  It compiled without error.

When I animate TESTPAN I get an error "the dynamic link library GS32.DLL
could not be found in the specified path.  This tells me that the build of
GS did not link in all the modules from GS32.LIB, but is trying to load them
at runtime.  I browsed thru the MF online documentation on LITLINK but don't
fully understand it.

Thanks,  Charlie


PS Jimmy form Cowtown: be careful with nicknames, don't you remember July
15, 1999
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help with Norcom's GUIscreenio and Netexpress

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T03:54:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bejbqvoln6eosnc7s3k8c89ltc20lfedpg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <3FA56184.F97959CA@shaw.ca> <gosaqvgbp7t96h9aa70mqaf3hgqqqgm1rh@4ax.com> <FUjpb.264566$6C4.211958@pd7tw1no>`

```
On Mon, 03 Nov 2003 03:25:25 GMT, "Charlie Goodman" wrote:

(snip)

>I built a new project with default properties in order to compile GS into a
>DLL.  The defaults were modified to add the word LITLINK to the project
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>fully understand it.
----------
Charlie,

Your LINK is fine, as is the GS.DLL that you have created.

GS.LIB is just an import library that exposes the entry points you
need in our runtime, GS32.DLL.  If the LINK had failed, you would have
gotten a linker error complaining of unresolved externals.  So,
there's nothing wrong there.

If the Animator is complaining that it can't find GS32.DLL, your
GS.DLL has been built correctly, since the system is indeed attempting
to load GS32.DLL, as it should. 

Therefore:

1.  Verify that GS32.DLL is in the same subdirectory as your
executable (or is in the PATH).  

2.  GUI ScreenIO also requires the Microsoft module GDIPlus.DLL, which
we distribute with the product.  In some cases, we've seen Windows
complain that it couldn't load GS32.DLL when, in fact, it was
GDIPlus.DLL that was missing.  So, verify that GDIPlus.DLL is also in
the same subdirectory as your executable.

LITLINK just tells Micro Focus to generate the calls to the GUI
ScreenIO runtime entriypoints GS32 and GSWINAPI as static, instead of
dynamic calls.  We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of static
and dynamic calls in the "Technical Tips" portion of the manual.

Regards,
Kevin

Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

work.

I will be in touch.

---Charlie

new documentation a little bit.

By the way, you don't need CARFSW20.DLL, that's only required for the
panel editor.

The required modules are:

- GS32.DLL
- CARCLW60,DLL
- GDIPlus.DLL (Microsoft DLL, only needed for versions of Windows
prior to XP)

Have fun.

Kevin

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help with Norcom's GUIscreenio and Netexpress

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T04:11:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hkbqv878ul3u0320je4kmmgs3d1t20u0c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <3FA56184.F97959CA@shaw.ca> <gosaqvgbp7t96h9aa70mqaf3hgqqqgm1rh@4ax.com> <FUjpb.264566$6C4.211958@pd7tw1no> <bejbqvoln6eosnc7s3k8c89ltc20lfedpg@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 03 Nov 2003 03:54:48 GMT, Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
wrote:

>LITLINK just tells Micro Focus to generate the calls to the GUI
>ScreenIO runtime entriypoints GS32 and GSWINAPI as static, instead of
>dynamic calls.  We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of static
>and dynamic calls in the "Technical Tips" portion of the manual.
--------
Guess I was a bit opaque there...  ;-)

LITLINK causes the compiler to generate the code as though the CALLed
module is linked statically.

The library GS32.LIB that you include with your LINK command isn't a
regular object library, it's an import library.  Import libraries
allow you (yes, anyone can do this) to expose multiple entry points in
a DLL.  This means that you can distribute a single DLL instead of a
whole bunch of them, which keeps things simple.  

What _really_ happens is that even though your generated code thinks
it's calling GS32 and GSWINAPI statically, in fact the import library
will link to GS32.DLL dynamically when you run your application.
That's how most of Windows actually works.   

The magic of the import library is that it will cause the system to
know, at runtime, that GSWINAPI is found in the module GS32.DLL, and
if it's not loaded, will load it.  If you simply tried to call
GSWINAPI dynamically, the compiler's runtime will go looking for
GSWINAPI.DLL (which it won't find), instead of finding the alternate
entry point in GS32.DLL.

Hope this helps a bit more..

Kevin
Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help with Norcom's GUIscreenio and Netexpress

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T20:57:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0311032057.174c3de0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <bfdfc3e8.0311030628.1075d0df@posting.google.com> <inhdqv8oc59182h20d0dqhbmdaisc83esm@4ax.com>`

```
Sure Kevin,

I perform tech support for Flexus as well as develop add on products
for sale by Flexus, conduct training classes and consult in software
development.  Besides that work, I have several other clients for whom
I do development, some of it is Java, and Oracle, and other is
Mainframe - Software Pursuits MVT/VSE and IBM OS/390.  There's even a
rumor I have a mainframe in my house here somewhere, but I won't
confirm or deny that.

What's more interesting perhaps is how I came to Flexus - my
association started as that of a customer.  I was a CA-Realia 16 bit
user (This is before Windows 95 was released) using Screenio.  I
wanted to go to OS/2 or Windows. I found Flexus on the internet (this
news group perhaps) when I had first tried the Realia presentation
manager examples - because I knew I needed to take my applications to
GUI.  I was consulting part time at the time developing my own
software.  My full time employ was with MicroFocus COBOL and Dialog
System - and I tried to get my employer to switch to Sp2 - but they
wanted to stay with a "Micro Focus" supported product - which
interestingly is no longer being enhanced, and the company has since
been absorbed and is no more.

I evaluated sp2 and purchased it and form print.  I converted (in
short order) my Screenio applications to Windows and OS/2 using Realia
COBOL.

I really liked the product - at that time GUI Screenio development had
not began (as far as I am aware).

When it came time to move to 32 bit for Windows 95, I evaluated
compilers, and settled on Fujitsu - as with Flexus products, I liked
that compiler so much I offered my services to the company and became
their J4 COBOL rep.

I am in an interesting position - because not only do I support the
Flexus tools - I USE them every day in my own software development. 
I've found nothing better - and as environments change and evolve (Dos
-> OS2/Windows -> 32 Bit Windows etc) the Flexus tools grow and
evolve.

I am proud of my association - and am pleased to share my experiences
and give a ringing endorsement to the tools.  It's just an added bonus
that I happen to do some work for them as well.

On 3 Nov 2003 20:57:27 -0800, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>Sure Kevin,
>
>I perform tech support for Flexus as well as develop add on products
>for sale by Flexus, conduct training classes and consult in software
>development.  

(snipped long response about why he uses sp2 and Fujitsu COBOL)

---------
Shucks, Thane, I have no problem with any of that.  And, you're
correct that GUI ScreenIO was just a twinkle in Norcom's eyes when you
switched to sp2, so I can't fault you for moving away from DOS
ScreenIO, though we regret losing you as a customer way back then.  

But, it's one thing to post a gratuitous plug for Flexus products
because you're a happy customer, and entirely another when you stand
to profit by it - regardless of your motives.  

If you're going to flog Flexus products, you owe it to people to be
honest about the fact that you have a direct financial interest in
their sales.

I don't object to what you say about the products you like, or how
often you say it - but I DO object to your failure to reveal your
business relationship with Flexus when you talk about how great the
products are.  

Some people might find that prejudicial, you know...  ;-)

Regards,
Kevin

http://www.ScreenIO.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: need help with Norcom's GUIscreenio and Netexpress

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T15:25:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhffqvoi2df9ti968ae1kcl6k38imfmflr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <bfdfc3e8.0311030628.1075d0df@posting.google.com> <inhdqv8oc59182h20d0dqhbmdaisc83esm@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0311032057.174c3de0@posting.google.com> <72meqvkqakiukds18sset8lcrgvs5cc8lt@4ax.com>`

```
Kevin:

I think that the COBOL newsgroup isn't the appropriate place for
"vendor wars" so if you would like to discuss this offline, please
send me your concerns via private e-mail.

I would like to say in Thane's defense, that:

1.  He did not try to hide his identity.  Intentionally hiding one's
identity would create an aura of questionable motives.

2.  Thane's message was positive and focused on products he knows very
well.  He did not say a single negative thing about any competing
product.

3.  If you re-read his original reply to Charlie, he did not represent
himself as a happy customer as you suggested.  He merely represented
himself as Thane Hubbell.

4.  Comp.lang.cobol is a public forum.

He merely suggested to Charlie that he consider evaluating the Flexus
solution.  We always encourage COBOL programmers to evaluate as many
options as possible.  This is regardless of whether they are
evaluating a compiler, programming tools or a particular architecture
for their application.  That allows the prospective customer to know
that they have made the best decision for their specific needs.

Thane was simply offering an alternative to the customer and the
customer would have immediately learned that Thane is a contractor
during the 60 day evaluation period.  This is prior to the exchange of
a single penny of compensation to anyone.

I believe that Charlie is a highly intelligent and highly professional
person who is capable of making his own mind up regarding what
constitutes questionable behavior.

I would suggest that a far more questionable posting would involve a
competitor doing the following:

1.  Intentionally hiding his or her identity.
2.  Berating their competitor's product.
3.  Not having the courtesy to respond when confronted on the matter.

See the comp.lang.cobol posting from:

FROM:     JSA (jsa@kona.penguinpowered.com)
SUBJECT:  Re: Graphical User Interface 
DATED:    2000/07/01

Read the follow-up postings.  You may have missed this.

We do not berate our competition.  That is highly unprofessional.  

This is my first and only posting on this this thread as I believe
that this is not the place to discuss these matters.

>On 3 Nov 2003 20:57:27 -0800, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane
>Hubbell) wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.ScreenIO.com


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com

- **References:** `<aF_ob.254152$6C4.176546@pd7tw1no> <bfdfc3e8.0311030628.1075d0df@posting.google.com> <inhdqv8oc59182h20d0dqhbmdaisc83esm@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0311032057.174c3de0@posting.google.com> <72meqvkqakiukds18sset8lcrgvs5cc8lt@4ax.com> <bhffqvoi2df9ti968ae1kcl6k38imfmflr@4ax.com>`

On Tue, 04 Nov 2003 15:25:52 GMT, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
wrote:

>Kevin:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>4.  Comp.lang.cobol is a public forum.

<snip>
---------

Bob, I'd be glad to discuss this off the ng if you like, though I
think much of it is germane to the group where it concerns the
objectivity of posts promoting commercial products.  

I'll also mention that the comments you objected to on our website
were removed some time ago.  You are correct that I was not aware of
that one ng posting you cited from over three years ago, and that it
was inappropriate.  Please accept my apologies.

I didn't intend to make a big issue of this.  I thought it was
appropriate to point out that, although you are correct that Thane
doesn't hide his identity, neither has he been particularly open about
the fact that he has a monetary interest in promoting your products.
I doubt that most people following the ng were aware of this.

The failure to mention this relationship implies, by omission, that he
is an independent third party - which he admitted he is not.  People
tend to view statements differently when they know they originate from
a vendor, or a vendor representative.  It seems reasonable to me that
Thane should mention this when he promotes your products so that
people are aware of it, just as they are aware of your and my
involvement with our respective products.

I think that Bill Klein's eloquent remarks on this subject are right
on target.  Vendor participation is important, vendor representatives
should identify their relationship to the product, and it's certainly
OK to mention that a product provides a potential to a problem and
could be evaluated.  It is, as we've all noted, a public forum.

If you reread my posting that started this, you'll see that I didn't
object to the posting, didn't speak critically of your products, and,
I think, was fair in what I said.  I simply observed that Thane's
posting is not necessarily originating from an independent third
party, and that he ought to mention it.

And that concludes my remarks on this subject.  

Regards,
Kevin
Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

IMproved) at www.vim.org.  I've been using vi on various Unix systems and

##### ↳ ↳ Re: copy-overlay in VI

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T07:17:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA601F5.EE2CA2B4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0310310904.e72f314@posting.google.com> <uPGdnRzoPp2u5jmiRVn-hA@comcast.com>`

```
JJ wrote:
> 
> What does a "copy-overlay" do in ISPF?

The source line is marked with a "C" in the ISPF prefix area.  The
target line is marked with an "O" in the prefix area.  A range of
target lines is marked with "OO" in the beginning and ending line.

For every matching character position in the source and target
line(s), the source character will be copied to the matching position
in the target line if the character is blank in the target line:

For example, BEFORE executing:

C..... 05  MY-DATA        PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES.
            
OO.... 05  FILLER    
...... 05  FILLER 
OO.... 05  NAME

and AFTER the copy-overlay is executed:

...... 05  MY-DATA        PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES.
            
...... 05  FILLER         PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES.    
...... 05  FILLER         PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES. 
...... 05  NAMETA         PIC X(04) VALUE SPACES.

It can be a very useful command in the right situation.

I hope that helps!
```

#### ↳ Re: copy-overlay in VI

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-11-02T23:04:03+00:00

- **References:** `<8168768c.0310310904.e72f314@posting.google.com>`

```
Looking up my Unix manual shows no equivalent for VI. You can copy 
after/before but not overlay. You might be able to achieve what you want 
using SED or AWK. Good luck.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copy-overlay in VI

- **From:** farmer@netnet.net (Mark A Framness)
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T06:49:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8168768c.0311030649.77da4325@posting.google.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<joe_zitzelberger-28D9BC.22554702112003@corp.supernews.com>...
> In article <Glh0AtAj1Yp$Ewvt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>,
>  Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> IIRC vi does support it, but I know I cannot remember how.

Overlay is placing text onto an already existing line. Indeed, VI can
block copy (e.g. yy5 & p or dd5 & p) but overlay is a different
operation.

I want to be able to lke the following:

C  foo-
OO     bar1
       bar2
OO     bar3

Hit enter and you get

   foo-bar1
   foo-bar2
   foo-bar3

ISPF does this nicely but apparently w/o using a more functional
editor I need to use sed & awk to accomplish this. Oh well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copy-overlay in VI

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T22:51:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CrKdncT99s1Q7jWiRVn-jg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0310310904.e72f314@posting.google.com> <Glh0AtAj1Yp$Ewvt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <joe_zitzelberger-28D9BC.22554702112003@corp.supernews.com> <8168768c.0311030649.77da4325@posting.google.com>`

```
It could be done without sed & awk - as I suggested before, check out Vim at
www.vim.org - it has a built-in macro language that could probably do
something like this pretty easily - and it's still "vi" (but improved!).

If you did write a sed or awk (or perl) script to do it, you can execute it
through vi without leaving vi.

Taking your example:

C  foo-
OO     bar1
       bar2
OO     bar3

If you had a shell script in your path named "coverlay" that executed a sed
command (or awk or whatever) that read stdin and wrote stdout, from within
vi, move the cursor to the first line (C foo-) and type:

4!!coverlay

where 4 = number of lines to send to stdin of coverlay.  vi will then
replace those 4 lines with the stdout of coverlay.

- **References:** `<8168768c.0310310904.e72f314@posting.google.com> <Glh0AtAj1Yp$Ewvt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <joe_zitzelberger-28D9BC.22554702112003@corp.supernews.com>`

In message <joe_zitzelberger-28D9BC.22554702112003@corp.supernews.com>, 
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> writes
>In article <Glh0AtAj1Yp$Ewvt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>,
> Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>IIRC vi does support it, but I know I cannot remember how.

email. Under VI a copy before/after is done by putting a block out to a 
buffer and copying it in again (IIRC).

<a id="t-8cbcbc783d"></a>
## Thread: Cobol IMS Update Name Over-write — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Cobol IMS Update Name Over-write

- **From:** denkenlamb <member46674@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-02T00:41:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3549913.1067751713@dbforums.com>`

```

I am working with code that is not Structrured Cobol.  I need help in
determining where new code is being overwritten by a IMS Database
update.  A code change is being undone when the database is updated.
For example, a change is made online to a name, when the database is
updated, then the change is overwritten.  Does anyone know of any tips
about IMS that would help me solve this problem?
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol IMS Update Name Over-write

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T04:55:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dlpb.4301$qh2.1306@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3549913.1067751713@dbforums.com>`

```
What type of "interactive" debugging tools do you have for IMS programs?

  Xpediter?
  BTS?
  IBM debug tool?

Depending on what tools are available, you SHOULD be able to "trace" when
such changes are taking place in your application logic (or <G> mis-logic)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol IMS Update Name Over-write

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T05:32:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PLlpb.203780$0v4.15993636@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3549913.1067751713@dbforums.com> <3dlpb.4301$qh2.1306@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

 "denkenlamb" <member46674@dbforums.com> wrote in message
 news:3549913.1067751713@dbforums.com...

> I am working with code that is not Structrured Cobol.  I need help in
> determining where new code is being overwritten by a IMS Database
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> about IMS that would help me solve this problem?

The most likely cause of this is a logic error
whereby the program to issue an unwarranted rollback call
after the update call has been executed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol IMS Update Name Over-write

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T17:41:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo6416$o64$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3549913.1067751713@dbforums.com>`

```

On  1-Nov-2003, denkenlamb <member46674@dbforums.com> wrote:

> I am working with code that is not Structrured Cobol.  I need help in
> determining where new code is being overwritten by a IMS Database
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> about IMS that would help me solve this problem?

Does IMS have a pre-compiler that sticks in code?   Does your compiler have an
option to see this listing that is normally turned off?
```

I checked the links. Free format doesn't allow continuations and is limited
to 250 bytes. The precompiler is breaking the larger SQL statements into 256
byte chunks. This causes errors as well.

So, am I going to be stuck with having to reformat all of my programs and
copybooks to ansi format instead of terminal? Is there another way? Am I
overlooking something?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler Directives?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-02T10:27:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311021027.41ee6b22@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vq1gsbe1kmk1da@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0310301006.571d8c81@posting.google.com> <vq44t48g4gr062@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0310311033.4360f94f@posting.google.com> <3FA2071D.F2424693@shaw.ca> <vq9jbro62082bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote

> I checked the links. Free format doesn't allow continuations and is limited
> to 250 bytes. The precompiler is breaking the larger SQL statements into 256
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> overlooking something?

Add $SET directives to change between source formats so that the
preprocessor is generating code in fixed format areas while other
areas are left as free format.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler Directives?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Wiggy <wignomore@btopenworld.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T08:06:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo529h$54h$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<vq9jbro62082bd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vq1gsbe1kmk1da@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0310301006.571d8c81@posting.google.com> <vq44t48g4gr062@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0310311033.4360f94f@posting.google.com> <3FA2071D.F2424693@shaw.ca> <vq9jbro62082bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> I checked the links. Free format doesn't allow continuations and is limited
> to 250 bytes. The precompiler is breaking the larger SQL statements into 256
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> overlooking something?

You should be able to use the $SET SOURCEFORMAT statement within your 
code to switch between modes dynamically at compile-time as required, 
rather than reformatting your source at all, e.g. $SET SOURCEFORMAT"free" .

When in fixed format mode, the $ should be in col 7. In freeformat, this 
should start in col 1.

Wiggy.
```

On Thu, 30 Oct 2003 01:53:52 -0600, "Dan"
<deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:

>We are converting from Compaq Cobol to MicroFocus Cobol and are having
>problems compiling.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> + procob iname=testpgm.pco format=terminal DBMS=V8 UNSAFE_NULL=YES
There is a PROCOBOL keyword that allows you to define the maximum size
a literal will have. Search for it and change it to be less than 254.
The default is 1024.
If I am not wrong this keyword will solve your problem.
snip



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler Directives?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T01:15:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vqmhkkosdts674@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vq1gsbe1kmk1da@corp.supernews.com> <3gadqvs6lt6ki9apf1o0b4l5tncr42orkj@4ax.com> <vqe3iemum2ci07@corp.supernews.com> <vqgu8f4m9dodd4@corp.supernews.com> <6akiqvshlcv28fchvq4b6k223vssto5aje@4ax.com>`

```

"Frederico Fonseca" <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote in message
news:6akiqvshlcv28fchvq4b6k223vssto5aje@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 4 Nov 2003 22:07:12 -0600, "Dan"
> <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler command line for us to see?


maxliterals=40 - compiles with errors because of the continuation

01  SQ0001 GLOBAL.
           02  FILLER PIC X(40) VALUE "select D.DECAL_NO  ,C.CUSTOMER_ID
-    "  ,C.FI".

           02  FILLER PIC X(40) VALUE "RST_NAME  ,C.MIDDLE_INITIAL  ,C.L
-    "AST_NAM".

maxliterals=32 - compiles clean

01  SQ0001 GLOBAL.
           02  FILLER PIC X(32) VALUE "select D.DECAL_NO  ,C.CUSTOMER_I"
     .

           02  FILLER PIC X(32) VALUE "D  ,C.FIRST_NAME  ,C.MIDDLE_INIT"
     .

           02  FILLER PIC X(32) VALUE "IAL  ,C.LAST_NAME  ,C.STREET  ,C"
     .

It appears that the precompiler works on 4 byte increments (ie 28, 32, 36,
40). So, 32 bytes is as big as I can get. "02  FILLER PIC X(32) VALUE" takes
up the remaining 28 bytes for a total of 60 bytes before it wraps. The
wrapping period doesn't count in the size of the statement, so it doesn't
count in the length.

Here's the precompile command:
procob iname=testpco4.pco format=terminal DBMS=V8 UNSAFE_NULL=YES \
               include=/path1/path2/path3/ \
               maxliterals=32

Here's the compile command:
cob -C nolist -vx -C sourceformat"'free'"
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Applications

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T09:08:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031103100840.692$De@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<3550517.1067789256@dbforums.com>`

```
"eric.a.davis" <member46700@dbforums.com> wrote:
>
> What are the common applications for COBOL?


Core business applications, such as tracking money, inventories, sales,
personnel, etc. For every game or common user application on the PC
there are 10,000 or more COBOL programs quietly running the real
business world. The US government probably has in the tens of millions
of individual COBOL programs. The number of COBOL programs in
businesses, local and state governments is beyond count. During the
Y2K effort, it was estimated that 60%-80% of all currently running
computer code in the world is COBOL. Many large organizations have
had dozens or hundreds of COBOL programmers busy writing code
for decades, much of it still in use.
```

## Thread: Working with a tab-delimited input file — 55 msgs

### Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T11:37:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```

I have a third-party system that is supplying me with a tab-delimited
plain text data file of client information that must be fed into a
legacy system.  The legacy system was written (poorly) in MF Workbench
and has recently been converted over to MF NetExpress 3.1



The problem I run into is that the records coming from the third party
system are variable length.  For instance if an optional field is empty
then there are two successive tab characters.  How can I seperate these
fields in MF COBOL?  Under Visual Basic or C/C++ I wouldn't have a
problem.  But these languages are not an option to me in this instance.



The file can range from 1 to 536 characters.



Thanks,





Christopher
```

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T11:30:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H_idnel00c0wDTuiU-KYgw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```
TofuTheGreat wrote:
> I have a third-party system that is supplying me with a tab-delimited
> plain text data file of client information that must be fed into a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> in this instance.

Uh...

You say the problem is the input records are variable length. Then you ask
how to deal with an optional field.

Assuming you can get a single, delimited, record, then:

MOVE SPACES TO ALL-MY-FIELDS.
UNSTRING INPUT-REC DELIMITED BY X'09' INTO
   FIELD-1
   FIELD-2
   FIELD-3
   etc.
   END-UNSTRING.

works by skipping the optional field.
```

```

Why isn't the same approach possible in COBOL?

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T17:56:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA69677.DD1F724C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```


TofuTheGreat wrote:

> I have a third-party system that is supplying me with a tab-delimited
> plain text data file of client information that must be fed into a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christopher

I've never used anything with TAB delimiteds - so that is a separate
problem. The file will have been created as variable in Workbench and the
FCD block knows this. So ignoring the TABS for the moment, something like
the following should work :-

FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 536 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(536).

I'm assuming you are reading a Sequential. If above is ISAM with the
Primekey as the first ten characters, then you would have to specify :-

    record varying from 10 to 536 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.

otherwise the compiler is going to 'groan' at you.

*>------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readNextVariable".
*>------------------------------------------------------

*> Used for VARIABLE LENGTH records

Linkage section.
01 lnk-ReturnValues.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-RecordSize                    pic 9(04).
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize  *> (your 536)
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division returning lnk-ReturnValues.

  initialize lnk-ReturnValues
  Read Data-File next record
    At End
      Set fileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move  Data-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
      to    lnk-record  (1:ws-RecordSize)
      Move  ws-RecordSize to lnk-RecordSize
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readNextVariable".
*>------------------------------------------------------

If you ANIMATE the readNext you should see the 'current record length' in
ws-RecordSize.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T13:50:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3554123.1067885417@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```

Originally posted by Jerrymouse You say the problem is the
input records are variable length. Then you ask how to deal with an
optional field.

> 



The 3rd party system supplies the file.  In that system some of the
fields are optional (and don't apply at all in my system).  When the 3rd
party system creates the record it simply puts:



field1[tab]field2[tab]field3[tab]....



if field2 is blank then I get:



field1[tab][tab]field3....



the "blank" field is what was throwing me for a loop.





Christopher
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T19:20:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oUxpb.5411$qh2.101@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3554123.1067885417@dbforums.com>`

```
What you need to do is run your file thru a "conversion" routine (NOT in
Micro Focus COBOL) first to "change" all the tabs to some other character
(that never appears in your data).  Then you can read in the file and use
UNSTRING to get all the actual fields (and those that are optional).

UNFORTUNATELY, Micro Focus (all products - as far as I know) does NOT
provide any way of reading in a file with TABS and not "converting" them.
The "T" run-time switch impacts the WRITING of files with/without tabs, but
not the reading.  See the documentation which says,

"On input, tab characters are always expanded to spaces to the next
position, regardless of the setting of the T switch."
```

This is exactly what I don't want to do.  This process will need to run
multiple times a day on demand.  My user base is not
sofisticated/smart/savvy enough to be depended on to launch a separate
process to massage the data before it gets into the system that they are
used to.  Since this involves a strong union I can't do much to change
this fact either (although it will disappear in time).



I had already thought of creating an Excel macro to convert the data.
Then I would simply tie a toolbar button to the macro.  This raised all
kinds of opposition when proposed.



So, bottom line, my users are idiots and I have to make their job
easier.  Sounds like any programmer that I know.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T21:17:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tBzpb.5622$qh2.4041@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3554123.1067885417@dbforums.com> <oUxpb.5411$qh2.101@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3554731.1067894502@dbforums.com>`

```
Any chance you can get the input changed from "tab-delimited" to "comma
delimited" (CSV)?

Alternatively, does your data include spaces? If not, you can unstring
delimited by SPACES.  Otherwise, I simply do not (personally) know of any
way that your Micro Focus COBOL program will be able to distinguish between
"valid space data" and "spaces that were originally tab delimiters"

It seems to me that there was an "enhancement request" to Micro Focus for an
OPTION to not convert TABS on *input*.  You might want to check with them to
see what the status is for such a "feature".
```

format.  The program is run by 3K+ locations so they are not willing to
futz with their export for my benefit.



None of the fields are likely to have spaces so I tried "unstring
delimited by spaces" and it simply interprets the sequential "empty"
fields incorrectly (for example if fields 4 & 5 are empty then field6
ends up starting in field8).



Maybe I should just create an algorithm to step through the incoming
record character by character testing for x"09" at each step and
building each field bit-by-bit.  Pain in the ass but that's what I'm
left with.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T23:24:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<btBpb.5789$qh2.1212@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** klaatu62@hotmail.com (TV_Junkie)
- **Date:** 2003-11-17T10:46:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5679495.0311171046.293cdf70@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3554123.1067885417@dbforums.com> <oUxpb.5411$qh2.101@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3554731.1067894502@dbforums.com> <tBzpb.5622$qh2.4041@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
This is a little tricky, but you could do the following:

Declare the file to be binary sequential, and 1 byte long...

Read the file through a loop, moving information into the first field
until you get to the x'09' then switch fields (you could use a table
to move the data, incrementing as you hit the x'09' bytes.)

Also check for a x'0A' byte that declares end of record, and then
reset yourself to the first field for the next bytes, and move the
table data into the appropriate fields for the record just processed.

This will get around the problem of expanding the X'09' to spaces, and
the problem of a variable length record being read as fixed length
causing an incomplete record at the end of the file.

I have several file workarounds that I do somewhat like this because
of issues with Line Sequential files.  Unfortunately I need record
delimited formats for some of the files I transfer from UNIX back to a
PC or other RECORD oriented environment...

Randy Boback
Quincy Mass
randy.boback (AT) verizon.net
-----------------------------------------------------------------


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<tBzpb.5622$qh2.4041@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>...
> Any chance you can get the input changed from "tab-delimited" to "comma
> delimited" (CSV)?
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi tofu,

I assume the "optional" field you describe is really a field that
sometimes contains data and sometimes doesn't and though the length of
the file is variable, the number of fields for each record is fixed.

If that's the case, Jerry Mouse's solution should work fine in all
cases. Just define the "optional" field w/a length of the largest data
string expected for the field (you'd do the same for all fields in the
record.)

When the UNSTRING stmt is executed and the "optional" field is
encountered (i.e. the ",,"), and no data is xfered from the IP to the
receiving field and it remains filled w/spaces, the initialized value.

HTH, Jack.

```
Jack Sleight wrote:
> Hi tofu,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> HTH, Jack.

Jack,

I think his I/O routine is killing the tabs and repositioning his data to
pre-defined tab positions. In other words, by the time he sees the data,
there are no tabs left. Realia COBOL had a file type like that, [T] as I
recall.

I never used it for the following reason. Consider a record with two fields,
tab delimited. Further, consider the tab stops to be every 5 characters. Now
pretend two records (+=TAB):

A+XYZ
ABCDEF+XYZ

In the first example, "XYZ" begins in byte 6. In the second example, "XYZ"
begins in byte 11. I could never figure out where the hell the data were, so
I never used the thing. As I understand it, tab-delimited fields were
designed to send data to PRINTERS that had built-in tabs, according to some
arbitrary standard dating back to the Sumerians.
```

       FD incoming-file

           record varying from 19 to 536 characters

           depending on rec-length.

       01 incoming-record pic x(536).



       working-storage section.

       01 rec-length               pic 9(3) value zeroes.



       01 switches.

           05 eof-incoming-sw                 pic x(1) value 'n'.

               88 eof-incoming                    values 'y', 'Y'.

           05 facility-number             pic x(7).

           05 medical-record-no        pic x(24).

            .

            .

            .

           open input incoming-file



           perform with test before until eof-pathlinks

               perform 1000-read-incoming-file

               perform 3000-seperate-fields



               ... other processing ...



           end-perform



            stop run returning "0".

       .



       1000-read-incoming-file.

           read incoming-file next record

               at end

                   move 'y' to eof-incoming-sw

               not at end

                   move 'n' to eof-incoming-sw

           end-read

       .



       3000-seperate-fields.

           UNSTRING incoming-record

               DELIMITED BY ws-tab

               INTO *>work-record-fields

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T17:14:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rc-dnWkFvbnQfDuiU-KYuA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3554393.1067889210@dbforums.com>`

```
TofuTheGreat wrote:
> I tried using UNSTRING delimited by x"09" but only the first field
> gets pulled into the working-record.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> and see that there is data there.

Ah ha!

The compiler is expanding the tabs to default positions and removing the tab
character! For example,
Record = AB+CDEFGH+JK...   (+=tab) results in

ABbbbCDEFGHbbbbIJKbb...

In other words, on input the TAB tells the IO routine to place this field at
the next multiple of 5 (assuming 5 is the tab default).

I think you're pretty much screwed as far as an easy way to process the
file.

We do it here using the CBL routines for reading. Here's our techinque:

1.Set the starting byte to 0.
2. Read some maximum number of bytes starting at START-BYTE
3. Decompose the record into fields, keeping track of how many bytes we've
accessed.
4. Add this number of bytes to START-BYTE
5. Go to step 2.

I can send you the code for a subprogram operating on the above principle if
you like.
```

> gets into Google ?

I don't know. I have used Google for newgroup access maybe twice - and then
only for searching.

In other words, if you use a new service for reading/posting your message
propogates around the web quite rapidly - even to Google. Once it gets to
Google, however, internal processing delays your message (and everyone
elses' for several hours).

Let me rephrase.

If neither of us is using Google, your message to this newsgroup will be
seen by me in minutes and my response to you will be seen by you within
minutes. If either of us is using Google Groups, this process will take
hours.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311041840.3b93dacf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3554393.1067889210@dbforums.com> <8a2d426e.0311032327.3bf0236a@posting.google.com> <OhycnUZK8I8QNDqiU-KYiA@giganews.com> <217e491a.0311041021.55587b56@posting.google.com> <Xa2dnVp1fdW7rTWiU-KYuA@giganews.com>`

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> > Ah yes, it may appear in Giganews quickly, but how long before it
> > gets into Google ?

> Let me rephrase.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> hours.

I would accept that if both were using, say, Giganews.  Obviously if
you post directly into that it can process it as fast as it wishes and
anyone else using the _same_ service will see it as soon as it is
posted.

However, this is not necessarily true if other news services are used,
or if the two people are using different news services - neither of
which are Google.

Google acts as a clearing house for at least some other services. 
This means that they deal with huge volumes of both incoming and
outgoing.  Typically a post would be available quickly on the service
it is posted to, but may take some hours to propagate to other
services, regardless of whether it is Google or some other.
```

First off I have to thank everyone who has posted.  I've learned
tremendously from this.  Thanks to everyone.



Jack Sleight, you described the problem perfectly:

<quote>

I assume the "optional" field you describe is really a field that
sometimes contains data and sometimes doesn't and though the length of
the file is variable, the number of fields for each record is fixed.

</quote>



Why I couldn't think of that description I don't know, oh well.



With that out of the way I started to investigate some of the avenues
presented.  First off I looked into the "EXPANDTAB" setting.  I changed
my file handler configuration file to have "EXPANDTAB=OFF".  That
didn't help.



However I did find a way to look at the hex values of the record coming
in after the read statement executes.  The tabs are indeed being
expanded to spaces to the next tab position.  For instance the first
field is up to 5-digits but only has 2 in it.  So the read is padding 6
spaces (x"20") onto the field.  The second field is up to 7 digits but
only has 4 and is being padded with 4 spaces (again x"20").  So James J.
Gavan and Jerrymouse hit it on the head there.



Jack Sleight wrote something that I'm not sure I follow:

<quote>

When the UNSTRING stmt is executed and the "optional" field is
encountered (i.e. the ",,"), and no data is xfered from the IP to the
receiving field and it remains filled w/spaces, the initialized value.

</quote>

Lost me there (forgive the blatant ignorance).



The working-storage record, that I outlined in one of my other posts in
this thread,  was the one supplied by the vendor who is supplying the
file.  Maybe I should alter it to accomodate the tabstops?



Bottom line.  I think I'm almost there.  I must be setting the EXPANDTAB
incorrectly so I'll look into that more.



Thanks again to all for the sharing of knowledge!





Christopher
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID" <"lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID">
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T10:54:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<3557602.1067958443@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3557602.1067958443@dbforums.com>`

```
TofuTheGreat wrote:
<snip>
> The working-storage record, that I outlined in one of my other posts in
> this thread,  was the one supplied by the vendor who is supplying the
> file.  Maybe I should alter it to accomodate the tabstops?

If the "missing" fields are actually filled with blanks, that should
work.  But then your records would all be the same length, and if I've
been following this correctly, they're not.  If the records as you
see them have different lengths, and the same field shows up at a
different offset in different records, no COBOL record description is
going to apply.

> Bottom line.  I think I'm almost there.  I must be setting the EXPANDTAB
> incorrectly so I'll look into that more.

If that doesn't work, you may want to write a simplified version of the
algorithm in the UNIX "unexpand" command which replaces spaces by tabs.
If you read C, a Google search for "unexpand.c" will show you various
implementations (all C is obfuscated C to some extent, and these files
are no exception).

Louis
```

TofuTheGreat wrote:

> First off I have to thank everyone who has posted.  I've learned
> tremendously from this.  Thanks to everyone.

Christopher,

It's a large number of years since I did any CSV-ing - and it was a pain in
the ass - the reason I've switched to DBMS with SQL is so that I can export
from a DBMS and the user can import into another DBMS.

Back to EXPANDTAB :-
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The EXPANDTAB parameter determines whether to expand TAB characters,
encountered in line sequential or line advancing files during a READ
operation, into the equivalent number of spaces.

EXPANDTAB={ON|OFF}

Default: ON

OFF Leave TAB characters unchanged.
ON Expand TAB characters into the equivalent number of spaces.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Haven't tried it - My thoughts are you need EXPANDTAB *ON* to get fixed
field lengths. From above on-line text looks like the Tabs 'disappear'
replaced by requisite spaces. Then unstring 'DELIMITED BY SIZE'  - initially
moving to pic x(??) fields. Then use COMPUTE FUNCTION NUMVAL or NUMVAL-C to
get the correct values for numerics into a redefined field ???? .

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T12:51:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0311041251.3db3f7b3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3557602.1067958443@dbforums.com>`

```
First, let me confess to NOT reading the intervening replies.

I would suggest not messing with the expandtab - you don't want to
expand them, you want to use them as field delimiters.

However, - you can't read the file with a "line sequential"
organization because the tabs will get expanded and you can't use them
that way.  (Though the Expandtab directive "should" cure that - I
never had much luck that way either).

So - here's what I did when I ran into this.  I preprocessed the file.
 I opened it with access SEQUENTIAL for I-O.  Then define the record
as ONE BYTE.  Then do this:

Perform until all-done
   Read input-file 
         at end 
            set all-done to true
         not at end
            perform process-record
   end-read
end-perform
.
process-record.
   If input-record = X"09"
      move X"FF" to input-record
      rewrite input-record
   end-if
   .

Then use line sequential organization to read the file and unstring
delimited by X"FF".


If you don't like using X"FF" you could use "~" provided there are not
any tildes in your input data.

…[67 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T23:20:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0311042320.480cb694@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3557602.1067958443@dbforums.com>`

```
Hi Chris,

You said:

"Lost me there (forgive the blatant ignorance)."

It would have helped if I wrote the idea in a complete sentence :).
Let me try it again (for the record):

When the UNSTRING stmt is executed and an "optional" field is
encountered (i.e. a field containing no data, notated as X'0909' in
the file), no data is xfered from the IP rec to the receiving field.
As a result it remains unchanged, i.e. filled w/spaces, the
initialized value.

Regards, Jack.

> tremendously from this.  Thanks to everyone.
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christopher

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T16:46:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3559700.1067982372@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```

Originally posted by Thane Hubbell 

> First, let me confess to NOT reading the intervening replies.

> I would suggest not messing with the expandtab - you don't want to
> expand them, you want to use them as field delimiters.

> However, - you can't read the file with a "line sequential"
> organization because the tabs will get expanded and you can't use them
> that way.  (Though the Expandtab directive "should" cure that - I
> never had much luck that way either).

> So - here's what I did when I ran into this.  I preprocessed the file.
> I opened it with access SEQUENTIAL for I-O.  Then define the record as
> ONE BYTE.  Then do this:

>

> Perform until all-done

>    Read input-file

>          at end

>             set all-done to true

>          not at end

>             perform process-record

>    end-read

> end-perform

> .

> process-record.

>    If input-record = X"09"

>       move X"FF" to input-record

>       rewrite input-record

>    end-if

>    .

>

> Then use line sequential organization to read the file and unstring
> delimited by X"FF".

> If you don't like using X"FF" you could use "~" provided there are not
> any tildes in your input data.

> 



Excellent suggestion.  I'll give this one a try!  This is similar
(though much simpler and more elegant) to something I was
contemplating doing.



[UPDATED]

DUH moment here for me.  The problem is happening when I do the actual
READ.  At the read the tab character (x"09") is being replaced by
padding spaces.  I can't use spaces as the delimiter since some of the
fields (i.e. addresses) will contain spaces.

The EXPANDTAB=OFF is supposed to suppress this behavior but isn't
working right.  If it was then my problems would be solved.  It's
looking more and more like I'm going to have to code either an
Excel/Word macro or a stand-alone VB program for the users to pre-
process the stupid file.  I can hear the bitching now.....
```

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T18:01:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3559986.1067986911@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```

Well for giggles I decided to try the solution that Thane Hubbell
suggested.  All I can say is LONG LIVE THANE HUBBELL!!!!



I added this code to my program as a file prepocessor:

<snip>

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

       FILE-CONTROL.

      ...

          select incoming-file

               assign to external incoming-file-dd

               organization is line sequential

               access is sequential.



          select one-byte-file

               assign to external incoming-file-dd

               organization is sequential

               access is sequential.

      ...



       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       ...

       FD incoming-file

           record varying from 1 to 536 characters

           depending on rec-length.

       01 incoming-record pic x(536).



       fd one-byte-file

           data record is one-byte-record.

       01 one-byte-record                      pic x.



       ...

       0000-prepocess-file.

           open i-o one-byte-file



           perform with test after until eof-incoming-file

               read one-byte-file next record

                   at end

                       move "y" to eof-incoming-sw

                   not at end

                       move "n" to eof-incoming-sw

                       if one-byte-record = x"09"

                           move "|" to one-byte-record

                           rewrite one-byte-record

                       end-if

           end-perform



           move "n" to eof-incoming-sw



           close one-byte-file

       ...

       3000-seperate-fields.

           move "|" to c-delim



           UNSTRING incoming-record

               DELIMITED BY C-DELIM

               INTO

                   *>work-record-fields

           end-unstring

       .

</snip>

I'll be damn but THIS WORKED!  My only thought (other than "FINALLY!")
is that the one-byte limit prevents the padding of spaces?  No matter
what though it worked.



The file is now properly separated into the fields like I need them to
be and the "empty" fields are recognized as empty.  I was then able to
proceed with the file processing.



I'm still leaving the problem with EXPANDTAB=OFF open at MicroFocus so
that it gets fixed and others don't have to endure this mess themselves.



Thanks for all the help from everyone.  I think this was a good thread
don't you?  :-)





Christopher
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID" <"lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID">

TofuTheGreat wrote:
<snip>
>        FD incoming-file
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>        0000-prepocess-file.
<snip>
>        ...
> 
>        3000-seperate-fields.
<snip>
> I'll be damn but THIS WORKED!  My only thought (other than "FINALLY!")
> is that the one-byte limit prevents the padding of spaces?  No matter
> what though it worked.

I'd bet it was the "sequential" vs. "line sequential" -- one leaves the
tabs alone, the other turns them into useless strings of blanks.

It was a brilliant idea, doing it this way.

If performance becomes an issue, you might want to try reading a buffer
(of up to 536 bytes?), translating all the tabs to vertical bars, and
then rewriting the buffer.  This would give you fewer read/rewrite
cycles.

While you're at it, that's "preprocess" and "separate."  Someday,
somebody's gonna notice.

Louis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T09:58:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311050958.1f4cb171@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3559986.1067986911@dbforums.com> <vqhe35fo6bh7b9@corp.supernews.com> <peudnULvWdqdYjWiU-KYhA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> > I'd bet it was the "sequential" vs. "line sequential" -- one leaves
> > the tabs alone, the other turns them into useless strings of blanks.
> 
> He doesn't have tabs after the read - that's the biggest part of the
> problem.

If it was read as sequential then he _would_ have tabs after the read,
but it would be read in fixed size blocks.  The real problem with that
is the last 'record' will give an error on the read and the record
would have to be padded and possibly appended back on the end of the
file.  In fact rewriting is probably not an option (depending on run
time used) and it may be necessary to write a converted sequential
file with the last record padded.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T18:17:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<09bqb.1617$Z25.1366@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3559986.1067986911@dbforums.com> <vqhe35fo6bh7b9@corp.supernews.com> <peudnULvWdqdYjWiU-KYhA@giganews.com> <217e491a.0311050958.1f4cb171@posting.google.com>`

```
Just as others may have missed it ...

As far as Micro Focus goes:

1) For RECORD sequential files, tabs are NEVER expanded to spaces (input or
output)

2) For Line Sequential files with EXPANDTAB=OFF, tabs are not expanded on
READs.  With it ON (the default), they are expanded.

3) The T run-time option, determines whether tabs are or are not expanded on
WRITEs (rewrites) for LINE SEQUENTIAL files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T19:19:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311051919.5976b15d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3559986.1067986911@dbforums.com> <vqhe35fo6bh7b9@corp.supernews.com> <peudnULvWdqdYjWiU-KYhA@giganews.com> <217e491a.0311050958.1f4cb171@posting.google.com> <09bqb.1617$Z25.1366@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> As far as Micro Focus goes:
> 
> 2) For Line Sequential files with EXPANDTAB=OFF, tabs are not expanded on
> READs.  With it ON (the default), they are expanded.

Perhaps you should indicate _exactly_ which versions of MF you are
commenting on here.  Object Cobol 4.1 does not have this.  It may well
be in NetExpress, but which version introduced it ?
 
> 3) The T run-time option, determines whether tabs are or are not expanded on
> WRITEs (rewrites) for LINE SEQUENTIAL files.

Not 'expanded', 'inserted'.  If the T switch is on then writing to a
line sequential file (you cannot rewrite) may have blocks of spaces
replaced by tabs by the run-time. This is by default OFF (-T) and has
been in MF since at least Level II Cobol of 1983.
```

Richard wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> been in MF since at least Level II Cobol of 1983.

EXPANDTAB is in N/E V 3.1 - whether or not it was in VISOC or N/E V 2.0 or 3.0 - I
don't know. (Yes I have the CDs - but be buggered if I'm going to load them to
confirm when !)

Just yesterday looked at the Directives, (V 3.1)  which also contain following :-
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
REWRITE-LS

Allows REWRITE statements on line sequential files.

Comments:
You must make sure that the record you write is the same size as the one it
replaces.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T18:27:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FABE399.75A4F79D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3559986.1067986911@dbforums.com> <vqhe35fo6bh7b9@corp.supernews.com> <peudnULvWdqdYjWiU-KYhA@giganews.com> <217e491a.0311050958.1f4cb171@posting.google.com> <09bqb.1617$Z25.1366@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0311051919.5976b15d@posting.google.com> <3FA9C8EE.5C582704@shaw.ca>`

```


"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Just yesterday looked at the Directives, (V 3.1)  which also contain following :-
> ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> -------------------------------------------------------------------------

Just out of curiosity, followed up the above querying why it wasn't possible with
SEQUENTIAL as well as LINE SEQUENTIAL. Got the following reply :-

"Actually, it is possible to rewrite both Line Sequential and Record
Sequential files. However, you have to be real careful when you have
variable length records (in either Sequential format) and your directive,
file handler config file, and runtime switch settings, as they relate to
data compression, expansion (or not) of tabs, trailing space
compression, etc.

The ability to rewrite Record Sequential file records is part of the
ANS85 spec. The ability to rewrite Line Sequential file records is a
Micro Focus extension and is only available when you use the MF
compiler directive or, use the REWRITE-LS compiler directive to
specifically turn on that feature when using other COBOL dialects. As
you can imagine, it is not a frequently used feature, but it is one that
can come in handy in certain situations.

For those who would like some more information on this, I have
included, below, the Help file information on the Rewrite command,
which shows the different formats and the cautions and things that
can affect the operation of the Rewrite command...............".

Above quote comes with caveats. Any M/F user who might be interested, check Answer
Exchange (Net Express) under thread "REWRITING SEQUENTIALS"  for the full detail,

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
