##### ↳ ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-10T23:28:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FB01EA0.49412C26@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com> <3562473.1068044690@dbforums.com>`

```
Chris,

Have you yet had an answer back on EXPANDTAB from Micro Focus ?

 Using 'ExpandTab' as my subject title I wrote to Answer Exchange because
somewhere in the distant past I recalled seeing a sample program using
byte-stream routines. I think my title triggered with the moderator who had
seen your query.

For what it's worth I got the following back - not trying to pre-empt  M/F,
but based on your query they may have answered you slightly differently. :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

....... You are correct that Byte-Stream I/O is one way to be able to read
a file in large chunks (so you get higher speed vs reading byte by byte) so
you
can unblock and process the data in memory. This is a standard part of
the Micro Focus compiler products.......... There should be a complete
chapter in the File Handling documentation book covering Byte-Stream I/O (in
fact, I think I referred someone to it a couple of weeks ago, if I remember
correctly). The commands are fairly straight forward and easy to use.

**** There is - if you look at the N/E V 4.0 on-line manuals ******

(then refers to your sequential reads)......

However, in testing I did several years ago, using
Unstring was not very efficient and using your own routine parsing
through the data record using Reference Modification would be much
faster. If you were to combine that with Byte-Stream I/O, it would be
faster still.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Unfortunately I didn't get an example of the program which I thought
existed. I know Thane has given you that byte-by-byte approach, which Jerry
qualified by saying it may be slow depending upon volumes, (and as I recall,
you want to do this on a daily basis), so it would make sense to use
byte-stream routines, and do "editing"  on chunks rather than bytes.

The above of course is still a two stage process - be nice if you find out
EXPANDTAB either ON/OFF works properly - then it is only one step.

On using "features" (other than that bloody VOCABULARY :-) ), I think it is
worth following up, if it gives you a edge - in your case does 'EXPANDTAB"
work - or was it something daft that you did.

In my case having created two DBs with MS Access, (1)  A.mdb (General tables
applicable to all users) and (2) B.mdb (common format but many different
users), how do I get at B1.mdb, B2.mdb, B3.mdb etc.

My first Mickey Mouse solution was to use CBL_FILE routines to change names
etc. But in the M/F literature there was an example of doing a DYNAMIC
CONNECTion. Couldn't figure it out from the examples. Then the penny dropped
when I got a sample back from M/F. I'm using the ODBC to register A.mdb and
B.mdb for which I have two entries :-

A        Merant Microsoft Access
B        Merant Microsoft Access

While I needed B to set up the tables, once I used their example with an SQL
INCLUDE for DECLGEN ( the Table-X for say B1, B2, B3 being dynamic - but in
fact a copy of Table-X in B) it worked as sweet as a nut when I used  the
following as my driver :-

MS Access Database    - Microsoft Access Driver [*.mdb]

Sure my CBL_COPYFILE, or CBL_RENAMEFILE would work - but the *real* solution
was to use their Format 6 CONNECT (dynamically).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Working with a tab-delimited input file

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T10:08:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3562478.1068044930@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3553493.1067877433@dbforums.com>`

```

Originally posted by Richard 

> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

>

> > > Ah yes, it may appear in Giganews quickly, but how long before
>     it

> > > gets into Google ?

>

> > Let me rephrase.

> > 

> > If neither of us is using Google, your message to this newsgroup
>     will be

> > seen by me in minutes and my response to you will be seen by you
>     within

> > minutes. If either of us is using Google Groups, this process
>     will take

> > hours.

>

> I would accept that if both were using, say, Giganews.  Obviously if

> you post directly into that it can process it as fast as it wishes and

> anyone else using the _same_ service will see it as soon as it is

> posted.

>

> However, this is not necessarily true if other news services are used,

> or if the two people are using different news services - neither of

> which are Google.

>

> Google acts as a clearing house for at least some other services.

> This means that they deal with huge volumes of both incoming and

> outgoing.  Typically a post would be available quickly on the service

> it is posted to, but may take some hours to propagate to other

> services, regardless of whether it is Google or some other.
> 



My two cents:



Our network admin blocked access to newsgroups quite some time ago.
When doing a search, for the solution that started this whole thread, I
ran into dbforums.com.



While it's not a direct post to the usenet it's working for me and at
least I can get to the newsgroups when I need to.  Best of all it's
free (so far).



There is a lag when posting but it doesn't seem to be too bad.  For
instance it's now 8:50am US Central time.  We'll see how long it takes
for this to make it to the newsgroup.
```

<a id="t-09cc786e9f"></a>
## Thread: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC — 5 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2003-10](2003-10.md#t-09cc786e9f)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T17:45:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo648b$p2v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```

On 31-Oct-2003, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Why do you need to add line feeds?
>
> If you use an XML viewer to view the data (Internet Explorer/XMLSpy) it
> should format the document for you.  There are a number of free tools for
> this.

I don't know.   We are sending a long string of code to a program that was
written by Pinnacle.   Unfortunately we have to do so in CoBOL II, so we can't
even write hex values nicely.   It has a display that doesn't always have line
feeds to let us read the code easily.

We are at the trying out stage right now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T14:38:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA6A0A5.E23DA159@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bo648b$p2v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On 31-Oct-2003, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> We are at the trying out stage right now.

If at all possible get to a supported version of the COBOL compiler. 
Having said that, if you are at the most current release of VS COBOL II
which is 1.4 IIRC, you have hex literals available to you.  If this is
on an IBM mainframe, it probably won't have any default ability to
produce line feeds because LINE SEQUENTIAL came in with COBOL for MVS
and VM or later.  The latest and greatest version for OS390 and z/OS has
at least some XML support.  The COBOL manuals and a large amount of
other information is available at the IBM web-site www.ibm.com with some
searching or you can try Google for a more appropriate web page.  There
may be enough information there to sell the upgrade.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T19:10:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SKxpb.5398$qh2.2771@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bo648b$p2v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3FA6A0A5.E23DA159@istar.ca>`

```
Just so it is clear,  "VS COBOL II, R 1.4" was the LAST release of VS COBOL
II, it is NOT a "currently supported" release of VS COBOL II.

Hex literals were available with all releases of VS COBOL II (as far as I
remember - certainly all from 3.0 on).  Therefore, I don't understand WHY
one would think that using them with that compiler would be a problem.  They
*might* not have been available with the CMPR2 compiler option - but I don't
think that even that was a restriction.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T16:11:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo8j3m$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bo648b$p2v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3FA6A0A5.E23DA159@istar.ca> <SKxpb.5398$qh2.2771@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

On  3-Nov-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Just so it is clear,  "VS COBOL II, R 1.4" was the LAST release of VS COBOL
> II, it is NOT a "currently supported" release of VS COBOL II.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> think that even that was a restriction.

She got a syntax error when using a hex literal.  This was solved by turning on
hex mode and typing the tests by hand.   This was the same literal that I cut
and pasted from a working CoBOL 390 source code and e-mailed her.   She tried
the usual suspects from my green card, but nothing worked), so she's temporarily
skipping this problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T19:47:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2nTpb.8308$qh2.980@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bo648b$p2v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3FA6A0A5.E23DA159@istar.ca> <SKxpb.5398$qh2.2771@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bo8j3m$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
And the "answer is"

When using the CMPR2 compiler option, one may NOT use hex literals (or lots
of OTHER things).  See:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igya1101/APPENDIX1.4.2.2

She should find out WHY her program is still using CMPR2.  If she changes to
NOCMPR2, she will be able to use hex literals - and should find future
maintenance and upgrades MUCH easier.
```

<a id="t-3e3ba660c2"></a>
## Thread: SHARE COBOL, PL/I, and LE Requirements — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [2003-10](2003-10.md#t-3e3ba660c2)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SHARE COBOL, PL/I, and LE Requirements

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-03T17:02:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA6C264.1DFA0A8@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<000b01c399e8$97262df0$9865fea9@WMK> <GP2mb.4196$wc3.1466@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bnaol7$t2k$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
In this case, the notification by William in the posting below is being
sent to these groups because SHARE, an IBM mainframe user group now
allows multiple individuals at the same installation to participate in
the process and will allocate separate ID's.  If you are using another
COBOL product on a different platform, check to see if your organization
is a member of a user group for that platform or compiler.  There may be
similar processes for requesting improvements.

Since COBOL programmers are not as likely to be SHARE attendees, it may
be helpful for them to be notified here so that if they are interested
and are at an IBM shop, they can hunt down their systems people and see
if their shop belongs to SHARE.  If so, I think that multiple
individuals from the same shop can have SHARE ids and that if more than
one person from the same installation votes on a requirement, the votes
are averaged.  
Several of these requirements may be hitting long standing sore spots
for you or your shop so it is important to vote.  This helps determine
where IBM will put its resources.  

Mark Yudkin wrote:
> 
> The page requires a logon, hence it's not really suitable for a general
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
           MULTIPLY USAGE-AB BY .06 GIVING CHARGES-AB.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can't find the problem.. (Net Express 4.0)

- **From:** Vaclav Snajdr <vsn@snajdr.de>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T09:44:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo7ofi$43q$06$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com>`

```
I think there is a switch against this error ... like about
setenv COBSW -F  (unix shell)
I use only the compiler and I can find this directive in the
documentation User Guide 11-6. Have you no docu for the high
product 4.0? 


xb00mx wrote:

> 
> When "stepping" I'm getting this error:
…[131 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can't find the problem.. (Net Express 4.0)

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@microfocus.uk.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T09:57:53
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bo7tcg$a5s$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com> <bo7ofi$43q$06$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

"Vaclav Snajdr" <vsn@snajdr.de> wrote in message
news:bo7ofi$43q$06$1@news.t-online.com...
> I think there is a switch against this error ... like about
> setenv COBSW -F  (unix shell)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Vaclav,

This suggestion prevents the error from occurring but does not fix the
problem.

xb00mx,

The problem is that the MOVE statement occurs before the value of USAGE-AB
is initialized so it will contain spaces, which are not valid in a numeric
data item. Either declare a VALUE for this in WORKING-STORAGE or INITIALIZE
the variable.

I am amazed that a teacher would not be able to spot this.

By the way, when 'stepping' did you try looking at thge value of the data
item ? That would have given you a clue as to why the value was illegal.
```

```
>     the

> > documentation User Guide 11-6. Have you no docu for the
>     high

> > product 4.0?

>

> Vaclav,

>

> This suggestion prevents the error from occurring but does not fix the

> problem.

>

> xb00mx,

>

> The problem is that the MOVE statement occurs before the value of
> USAGE-AB

> is initialized so it will contain spaces, which are not valid in
> a numeric

> data item. Either declare a VALUE for this in WORKING-STORAGE or
> INITIALIZE

> the variable.

>

> I am amazed that a teacher would not be able to spot this.

>

> By the way, when 'stepping' did you try looking at thge value of
> the data

> item ? That would have given you a clue as to why the value was
> illegal. 





Thanks Gael, sounds right, but how do I go about initializing it? Or
which would way be more efficient? I'm still new to COBOL.



This is a night class & my teacher had been teaching all day, so I'll
cut her some slack :) I'm sure she'll have an answer when I return
tomorrow night.
```

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T07:31:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3557147.1067949097@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com>`

```
use



$set SPZERO

  identification divisio.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can't find the problem.. (Net Express 4.0)

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T15:17:06+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fh9fqvcleukj26lboelqhlcroklhjf41np@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com>`

```
On Tue, 04 Nov 2003 02:00:48 -0500 xb00mx <member46895@dbforums.com> wrote:

:>When "stepping" I'm getting this error:

:>163 Illegal character in numeric field

:>A:\ANBC02.int

:>.whenever it reaches MOVE USAGE-AB TO USAGE-PR in 700-DETAIL-LINE.

Makes sense.

:>I can't find out where the problem is, nor can my teacher.

First of all, get a new teacher.

After that, ask yourself the following question:

"When is USAGE-AB initialized?"

After that, ask yourself if your program logic makes sense. COBOL will not
execute the paragraphs in numerical order - unless you program it that way.

   [ snipped ]
```

#### ↳ Re: Can't find the problem.. (Net Express 4.0)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T17:48:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA7E5F5.EAAD11C4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com>`

```


xb00mx wrote:

> When "stepping" I'm getting this error:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Go with Gael's suggestions - either put zero values in fields in Working
Storage or INITIALIZE - see the on-line help for INITIALIZE with its
options. Simple example :-
01 SomeFields.
    05 FieldA            pic 9(03).            *> alternative approach
9(03) value 0.
    05 FieldB            pic x(30).            *> ,,
,,            x(30) value spaces.

INITIALIZE SomeFields.

I would recommend against the Directive $set SPZERO - it hides the
problem. While you are learning using N/E 4.0 you might eventually be
working with an IBM, UNISYS, Fujitsu, AcuCOBOL, RM/COBOL etc.... compiler.
No guarantee that the Directive will be available - then you will wonder
what has gone wrong.

Thing to remember - those examples I've given above - if not initial
values or INITIALIZED they contain JUNK when the program is first loaded.

I liked Benyamin's suggestion - but somehow don't think that's an option
for you <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can't find the problem.. (Net Express 4.0)

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T16:11:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311041611.6c7c93a0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3556437.1067929248@dbforums.com> <3FA7E5F5.EAAD11C4@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> Go with Gael's suggestions - either put zero values in fields in Working
> Storage or INITIALIZE - 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for you <G>.

Actually, Binyamin's suggestion about 'in sequence' was correct - the
lines:

>           PERFORM 700-DETAIL-LINE.
>           PERFORM 200-CALC-RTN.

Are being done in the wrong order, the calculations should be done
before the results are printed.

No 'zero' or 'initialise' need be done, just get the existing code
executing correctly.
```

```
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<snip>

Oh Richard..(groannnnn)....... I was referring to his - "First of all, get a

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Postal Barcode /MainFrame Cobol — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Postal Barcode /MainFrame Cobol

- **From:** jdrewf <member40224@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T09:03:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3557345.1067954639@dbforums.com>`

```

Is anyone doing postal barcode in cobol ?

and if so, do you have any samples.
```

#### ↳ Re: Postal Barcode /MainFrame Cobol

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T17:05:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O%Qpb.1558$773.783@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3557345.1067954639@dbforums.com>`

```

"jdrewf" <member40224@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3557345.1067954639@dbforums.com...
>
> Is anyone doing postal barcode in cobol ?
>
> and if so, do you have any samples.
>

There is an sample program, envelope.cbl, in Liant's RM/COBOL for 32-bit
Windows that will generate POSTNET barcodes.  It uses line drawing features
that are specific to RM/COBOL to print the barcode.

For specifications of postal barcodes see chapter 4, POSTNET Barcodes in:
http://pe.usps.gov/cpim/ftp/pubs/Pub25/pub25.pdf

The basics of it is:

Postnet barcodes can represent five-(ZIP), nine-(ZIP+4), or eleven digit
(ZIP+4+Delivery Point) ZIP code information.  Each code character is made up
of five bars.  Specific combinations of two full bars and three half bars
represent digits 0 thru 9.

                 Numeric   Binary Code
                  Value       Value
                ---------  ------------
                |       |  |   74210  |
                ---------  ------------
                    1          00011        0 - half bar  1 - full bar
                    2          00101
                    3          00110
                    4          01001
                    5          01010
                    6          01100
                    7          10001
                    8          10010
                    9          10100
                    0          11000

The POSTNET bar code always begins and ends with a frame bar (Full or tall
bar).
To ensure POSTNET accuracy a correction character must included immediately
before the rightmost frame bar. The correction character is always the digit
that, when added to the sum of the other digits in the bar code, result in a
total that is a multiple of 10.
The delivery point digits in the delivery point bar code are the last two
digits in the street address.

The width of the bars must be 0.020 +/- 0.005 inches.
The height of the full bars must be 0.125 +/- 0.010 inches.
The height of the half bars must be 0.050 +/- 0.010 inches.
The space between the bars must be between 0.012 and 0.040 inches.

Five-digit   ZIP code (32 Bars)  1.245 >  < 1.625 inches
Nine-digit   ZIP code (52 Bars)  2.075 >  < 2.625 inches
Eleven-digit ZIP code (62 Bars)  2.495 >  < 3.125 inches

-Rob
```

```
wrote:
```

## Thread: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

- **From:** dcshnier <member46964@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T10:25:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com>`

```

In order to even guess at the cause of the problem, you need to include
the contents of the DCLGENS and the names of the unrecognized host
variables mentioned in the compile errors.  It would also be useful, if
you include the part of the cobol program, where the host variables are
being used.   By the way, is this a brand new program you are writing,
or are you changing an existing program which has already compiled
successfully ?  If it is an existing program, then what are the exact
changes which you have added ?

dcshnier@flash.net



Originally posted by Phrozen273C 

> I have and online DB2 Cobol program(OS390) That is not compiling(db2
> pre-compiler step). The program is calling a copybook(no problem yet),
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> step. any help would be appriciated.

>

> Example:

>

>         IBM-4341.

>     DATA DIVISION.

>

>     WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

>     COPY C9002FMT.       -  copybook

>

>    ******************************************************************

>    *   SAPIENS COMMUNICATIONS AREA                                  *

>    ******************************************************************

>     LINKAGE SECTION.

>     01  SAP-IN REDEFINES DFHCOMMAREA.

>

>

> The copybook C9002FMT contains:

>

>  ******************************************************************

>   01  FILLER                          PIC   X(27)  VALUE

>          'WORKING STORAGE STARTS HERE'.

>  ******************************************************************

>  *  THESE SWITCHES ARE USED TO DETERMINE THE END OF A CURSOR      *

>  *  SELECT. THE ROUTINES ARE PERFORMED UNTIL AN SQL CODE OF +100  *

>  *  AND THEN THE SWITCH IS SET.                                   *

>  ******************************************************************

>   01  WS-SWITCHES.

>       05  END-OF-T27030               PIC   X(01)  VALUE 'N'.

>

>

>          05 WS-PROP-EXIST                 PIC  X(01).

>          05 WS-TAG-EXIST                  PIC  X(01).

>     ******************************************************************

>     *  INCLUDE THE SQL :DCLNAME FOR FORMS ATTACHMENT TABLES          *

>     ******************************************************************

>     ****************************

>     * INTERFACE_CONFIG - 17500 *

>     ****************************

>          EXEC SQL

>              INCLUDE POB17950      - dclgen

>          END-EXEC.

>     ****************************

>     * FRM_SPECIAL_PROP - 27030 *

>     ****************************

>          EXEC SQL

>              INCLUDE PST27030      -dclgen

>          END-EXEC.

>     ****************************

>

>

>

>

> Thanks,

>

Mick
```

```
It is a lot simpler than that.

The DB2 precompiler does not handle COPY statements.

:>dcshnier@flash.net

:>> statement calls in a dclgen. The compile fails with host variables not
:>> found. I believe that is because the pre-compiler does not know what
:>> to do with the cobol statements. Besides spilting up the Cobol and SQL
:>> statements is there anyway to make this work. I also wanted to find
:>> out if there is a way to use nested includes in the db2 pre-compiler
:>> step. any help would be appriciated.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T19:23:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p0Tpb.8281$qh2.4359@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com> <3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com> <mtofqv83b56v2vhodn7rhg5i0eqfn6om4h@4ax.com>`

```
And it is even simpler than that - there was an enhancement "late" in the
life of "IBM COBOL for z/OS & VM" (and in Enterprise COBOL).  IF (and only
if) you use the SQL "coprocessor" - it is now supported to have EXEC SQL
statements in COPY members.  See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3PG10/3.2

and related sections of the manuals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T13:15:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PJ6qb.5526$jW5.127625@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com> <3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com> <mtofqv83b56v2vhodn7rhg5i0eqfn6om4h@4ax.com> <p0Tpb.8281$qh2.4359@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
We've run into a number of problems doing this.  So *CHECK* all the
Fixes/APARS and everything else.

One of the issues we ran into early on was the use of a COPY with the SQL
compile option  (use the coprocessor).  I don't remember exactly what it was
but it was something to do with a copy after an sql include.  This was
resolved by applying some patch (I don't know it's not my job - that ends at
complaining it doesn't work).

I've had another problem sent to DB2 support and they have still not been
able to help me so I still bring out my old db2 preprocessor / compile JCL.

From a support standpoint no one can tell me whether the support for the
coprocessor comes from the COBOL group or the DB2 group? Any hints?

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ mRe: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T17:55:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YPaqb.1576$Z25.464@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com> <3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com> <mtofqv83b56v2vhodn7rhg5i0eqfn6om4h@4ax.com> <p0Tpb.8281$qh2.4359@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <PJ6qb.5526$jW5.127625@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
To: comp.lang.cobol (with BCC's to some of my "Usually reliable sources")

If you are having problems with the coprocessor (from COBOL - not PL/I) then
you should definitely report this to the COBOL "support" team.  It is true,
that *THEY* may then re-queue it to DB2 (or require DB2 assistance) - but
the FIRST place (Level 1 / Level 2) that you should go is via COBOL.
ESPECIALLY if it is for something that "works" with the pre-processor.

P.S. The same for the CICS integrated compiler support.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T07:06:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qmqb.111309$RP2.8678@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com> <3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com> <mtofqv83b56v2vhodn7rhg5i0eqfn6om4h@4ax.com> <p0Tpb.8281$qh2.4359@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <PJ6qb.5526$jW5.127625@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <YPaqb.1576$Z25.464@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Given that I can recreate the problem at will with any source code module on
my system I believe that it is going to be a problem with an incompatibility
in our syslib somewhere.

If it has not been resolved yet, I will work with our system support to
discuss with the COBOL support because I got nothing but dead ends from DB2.

The problem essentially is that the DBRMLIB is created successfully, the
bind is successful but the program does not execute correctly (gets a -805
not bound).  The interesting feature is that the package name it looks for
is '  ' and not the actual package (according to our DSNTIAUL formatted
output).  The SYSIBM.SYSPACKAGE table has the everything it needs but the
SSCID is all messed up and it thinks it is Katakana package.  I've searched
high and low through all the SQL options even specificying the code page to
no avail.

I may look in the APAR list again - unfortunately I am having larger issues
with other products and the work around works for now.  Soon we will be
migrating completely to the new Enterprise compiler with the SQL option once
we get the SCLM translators worked out and we do need an answer.  They are
relying on me - go figure - to determine why it's not working as it must be
a developer thing.  Of course I'm not supposed to be coding anymore :^)

But at least someone finally gave me an authentic sounding answer :-)

Thanks

JCE
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Os390 Cobol Db2 Copybook Containing Sql And Cobol Code

- **From:** dcshnier <member46964@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T15:32:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3559397.1067977965@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3405575.1065208421@dbforums.com> <3557634.1067959503@dbforums.com> <mtofqv83b56v2vhodn7rhg5i0eqfn6om4h@4ax.com>`

```

You are correct.

In order to compile, the INCLUDES will have to be part of the main
program and not in a COPYBOOK.   However, this phenomenom is shop
specific.  At my last shop, the COPY statements were resolved 'before'
the pre-compile, in which case, the host-variables would have been
recognized and the code would have compiled.

regards

dcshnier@flash.net





Originally posted by Binyamin Dissen
```

## Thread: "ADMIN" - "vendor input" — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### "ADMIN" - "vendor input"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T19:57:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZwTpb.8334$qh2.526@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
1) comp.lang.cobol *is* an "open" (unmoderated) newsgroup, so "of course"
anyone may post whatever they want - with or without any "identification"
they want/think is appropriate.

2) I have always (privately) recommended to "vendor representatives" that
WHEN they are speaking about "their" product, that they identify their
relationship to the product.  (This includes when they either
"recommend/compare" it  or when they are providing technical
input/solutions.)

3) When a vendor representative simply MENTIONS their product provides a
solution to a specific question/problem - or as was recently "done" mentions
that such a product should be "evaluated" along with other solutions, then I
don't (personally) think they "need" to identify their relationship.  On the
other hand, it certainly never "hurts" to do so.

FINALLY,
  I really appreciate all the "vendors" who participate within
comp.lang.cobol - both as it relates to their own products and "general"
COBOL issues.  I hope that none of the current participants "worry" about
their posts and, thereby, limit their participation in the future..
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3562633.1068046166@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<ZwTpb.8334$qh2.526@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

You bring up good points Bill.  I think it's an ethical question here.
Should a vendor "mask" his relationship to promote his/her product?  I
don't think so.  I, for one, am more inclined to work with vendors who
are open and honest about who they are and what they do.





Christopher
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "ADMIN" - "vendor input"

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T06:22:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zMlqb.111142$RP2.11436@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ZwTpb.8334$qh2.526@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <3562633.1068046166@dbforums.com>`

```
"TofuTheGreat" <member46811@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3562633.1068046166@dbforums.com...

> You bring up good points Bill.  I think it's an ethical question here.
> Should a vendor "mask" his relationship to promote his/her product?  I
> don't think so.  I, for one, am more inclined to work with vendors who
> are open and honest about who they are and what they do.
> Christopher
Should accounting firms mask their relationship with whom they are
accounting?
Should Presidents release the names of those assisting with Energy Bills?
Should newspapers disclose any interests they have in a story?

Of course they should.  Do they ?

This is usenet.  People aren't masking their relationship to promote his/her
product - if they do I would suggest it fails.  If someone wanted to mask
their relationship they would lie.  It's the internet - you wouldn't know.

Bottom line- always take everything as __input___ into a decision and
nothing more.

I venture if someone has an email at ibm.com / microfocus.com  etc it is
fairly obvious that they represent the company.   These people will identify
the relationship because they have some belief in it.  I've never heard of
commission from usenet :-)

But I agree....if someone represents a product fully I am more likely to
entertain it in a serious manner.  I think it's *great* that people actually
take the time to monitor forums to assist both with and without their
company.

So I think Bills point is very accurate...you don't affiliate yourself with
a product you sell then you're just an opinion.  Disclose the relationship
AND you provide help then you are a potential resource.

If you say I am bob with product ComponentCOBOLJavaGUIconversion....then you
have credibility and people with think that you have some level of
confidence in what you provide.

So I ditto the post!

JCE
```

```
I am calling one program from another using call stmt.



Calling program

___________________



WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  BUF         PIC S9(9) BINARY VALUE 99.

 01  RESULT      PIC S9(9) BINARY VALUE -99.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 START-IT.

     CALL 'SUB-MAIN-ENTRY' USING BUF.

     DISPLAY "RESULT".

     CALL 'SECOND-ENTRY'.

 END PROGRAM RETURN-CODE-TEST.



Called Program

_____________



LINKAGE SECTION.

 01  BUF PIC S9(9).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING BUF.

 PARA-01.

     ADD 1 TO BUF GIVING RETURN-CODE.

     EXIT PROGRAM.

 PARA-02.

     ENTRY "SECOND-ENTRY".

     MOVE -5000 TO RETURN-CODE.



it is compiling fine..but when I am linking, I am getting error as



obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _SECOND-ENTRY

OBJ: error  LNK2001: unresolved external symbol _SUB-MAIN-ENTRY

.\.exe : fatal error LNK1120: 2 unresolved externals

NMAKE : fatal error U1077: '...PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x19'

Stop.



Linking files failed.

Please close the window.



*********************************************



I used Main and NOALPHAL as the compiler options.



Is there anything I have to do , to make this work?

Can anyone help please?



-manga
```

#### ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-04T20:06:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xaidnf3_uf6IxjWiU-KYuA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com>`

```
manga wrote:
> I am calling one program from another using call stmt.
>
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can anyone help please?

With the information provided here, I don't see enough to help on the
linkage question. Note that "BUF" is defined differently in the calling and
called programs. This will cause all manner of unfortunate results. In the
calling program, BUF is 4 bytes long; in the called program, BUF is 7 bytes
long.
```

```
********************************************************
```

```
> below...and again I am getting the same linking error..it says it cannot
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T16:34:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com>`

```
manga <member43127@dbforums.com> wrote 

> OK...let me simplify the code..I have changed the code and copied
> below...and again I am getting the same linking error..it says it cannot
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>      EXIT PROGRAM.

When passing parameters from one program to another they must be
defined exactly the same.  PIC S9(9) is 9 bytes long and PIC 99 is two
bytes long.  Adding 1 in the subroutine will change  000000099  to 
010000099.

You seem to want to have some sort of 'return'. FJ3 does not support
returning a value to the CALL. By default, parameters are passed by
reference you can change the contents as you are doing.

As to the failed linker, you probably need to read the manual a bit
closer.  You need to compile SM1 without a MAIN and then add SM1.LIB
to the link of RT1.  RT1 must be compiled with compiler option MAIN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T19:24:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UeKdnSjCG-WiaTeiU-KYiA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
>
> You seem to want to have some sort of 'return'. FJ3 does not support
> returning a value to the CALL. By default, parameters are passed by
> reference you can change the contents as you are doing.

Yes it does, although not by the RETURNING clause. You may set whatever
numeric value you want in the special register RETURN-CODE in the called
program. The calling program can then test RETURN-CODE (be careful you don't
do anything to screw with RETURN-CODE - such as another subroutine call -
between regaining control from the subprogram and the test).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T21:35:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acsjeKCdABr$EwP3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com>`

```
In message <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com>, Richard 
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes
>When passing parameters from one program to another they must be
>defined exactly the same.

As an adjunct to this, Richard/anyone, as a general principle does a 
program calling several programs using the same parameters need those 
parameters to be specified in the same sequence for each called program? 
I suspect IBM does not and I know that Fujitsu does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T14:31:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<boh6gt$e68$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com> <acsjeKCdABr$EwP3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair MacLean queried:

<< ... as a general principle does a program calling several programs using
the same parameters need those parameters to be specified in the same
sequence for each called program?  I suspect IBM does not and I know that
Fujitsu does.>>

Yes, I think they do.

ANSI X3.23-1985, page X-29, 5.2.4, CALL statement, General Rule 10:

"The sequence of appearance of the data-names of the USING phrase of the
CALL statement and in the corresponding USING phrase in the called program's
Procedure Division header determines the correspondence between the
data-names used by the calling and called programs.  This correspondence is
positional and not by name equivalence; the first data-name in one USING
phrase corresponds to the first data-name in the other, the second to the
second, etc."

In standard COBOL, then, if Program A calls programs B, C and D, using the
parameters X, Y and Z,  X corresponds to the first parameter, Y to the
second, Z to the third, in each of the three programs B, C and D.  It
doesn't matter what B, C or D calls those parameters.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Thane,
  If so, they were NOT conforming to any ANSI/ISO COBOL Standard that I know
of.  The order of parameters in the USING phrases of CALL / Procedure
Division is *supposed* to be the only thing that impacts "matching".

P.S.  I have never run into one that didn't work this way - but that doesn't
mean that there has never been one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T18:33:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311071833.2f2d76be@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com> <acsjeKCdABr$EwP3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote

> As an adjunct to this, Richard/anyone, as a general principle does a 
> program calling several programs using the same parameters need those 
> parameters to be specified in the same sequence for each called program? 

No.  The sequence of parameters required is determined by the called
program alone and is determined for each called program individually.

In the called program it is the 'PROCEDURE DIVISION USING ..' (or
'ENTRY USING') that specifies the order that the CALL parameters are
to be in.  There is nothing that can enforce writing one PD USING
clause to match some other program's PD USING, except arbitrary
standards and some sensible consideration of uniformity.

> I suspect IBM does not and I know that Fujitsu does.

In what way does Fujitsu 'need' the parameter sequence to be the same
for calling different programs ?

There is nothing to prevent having:

           CALL "prog1" USING A B
           CALL "prog2" USING B A

as long as these are the required order for those programs.

In fact if A and B have the same definition one may say:

            CALL "prog3" USING A B
            CALL "prog3" USING B A

depending on what prog3 may be doing with those parameters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-11-09T13:49:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z575nPBBYkr$EwY0@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com> <acsjeKCdABr$EwP3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0311071833.2f2d76be@posting.google.com>`

```
In message <217e491a.0311071833.2f2d76be@posting.google.com>, Richard 
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes
>In what way does Fujitsu 'need' the parameter sequence to be the same
>for calling different programs ?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>depending on what prog3 may be doing with those parameters.

I recently wrote a Fujitsu Cobol program which called in sequence three 
other programs passing the same parameters to each program. 
Unfortunately, I did not use the same sequence of parameters for calling 
the second and third programs as was used to call the first program. 
Although the sequences for the CALLs matched the LINKAGE SECTION in the 
called programs perfectly, the program kept abending. Only after 
changing the three calls to use the same sequence of parameters did the 
program function correctly.
```

```
not the fix that you attribute it to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unresolved external symbal error in fujitsu cobol.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-10T22:21:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tbUrb.6575$nz.5400@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3560214.1067990880@dbforums.com> <3563730.1068058752@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0311051634.7b40c9a2@posting.google.com> <acsjeKCdABr$EwP3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <217e491a.0311071833.2f2d76be@posting.google.com> <z575nPBBYkr$EwY0@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistar,
  Either you found a "bug" in the Fujitsu product, or there is something you
aren't telling us about the situation.

Certainly if EITHER the size/definitions of the CALLing parameter or the
CALLed (linkage section) parameters are "different" - then a run-time error
would be "reasonable". Another "problem" can occur when you aren't passing
ALL the parameters in the Procedure Division USING phrase every time.
Otherwise,  I just don't see this being "likely".

The following is an "example" (outline only) of where I would expect
run-time problems:

  Program-ID.  CALLing.
     ...
   01   Big-Field  Pic X(100).
   01   Big-Field2  Pic X(100).
   01  Medium-Field  Pic X(50).
   01  Small-Field   Pic X.
       ...
     Call "CALLed" using  Big-Field Big-Field2 Medium-Field Small-Field
       ...
    Display  "Next Call should work OK in any ANSI conforming compiler"
     Call "CALLed" using Big-Field2   Big-Field  Medium-Field  Small-field
       ***
    Display "Next two calls *may* fail"
    Call "CALLed" using Small-Field Big-Field Medium-Field Big-Field2
       ...
    Call "CALLed" using  Big-Field  Big-Field2  Medium-Field
       ...
  Program-ID.  CALLed
  Linkage Section.
* note that the ORDER that fields are defined in LS is not important
  01  LS-Small-Field   Pic X.
  01  LS-Medium-Field  Pic  X(50).
  01  LS-Big-Field2  Pic X(100).
  01  LS-Big-Field   Pic X(100).
 Procedure Division Using LS-Big-Field Big-Field2  LS-Medium-Field
LS-Small-Field.
*  The next statement may fail if 4 parameters aren't passed
   Move "X" to LS-Small-Field
* The next statement may fail if the 1st parameter passed isn't 100
characters long
   Move "Y" to Big-Field (99:2)
   Exit Program
     .
```

```
either way (and currently my Fujitsu v3 compiler is dead).

Judging by the response, I must have be in error, as no-one else seems 
to have suffered from the same 'feature'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: microfocus cobol under windows

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T10:26:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311061026.5bc180c8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3561860.1068034781@dbforums.com> <3566578.1068111386@dbforums.com>`

```
stak <member47121@dbforums.com> wrote 

> The problem is that: when try to running under a DOS box in windows
> ihave: error drive c   or  put cobol1.lib in drive c
> 
> The version of micro focus cobol is v4.1

MicroFocus version 4.1 is not a MS-DOS version, it is a Windows
version.  The last DOS version was 3.4.  You are probably missing some
environment variables that will tell Cobol where the various libraries
and such are, eg SET COBLIB=...

When you installed this it should have set up the environment, or did
you just copy it from somewhere ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Old MBP Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T13:24:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jYrqb.13400$8x2.6478872@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2b33cf5e.0311050616.3e246506@posting.google.com> <bodgms$t3o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Harald Cordes" <Harald.Cordes@microfocus.com> wrote in message
news:bodgms$t3o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
> Hi Dan,
>
> I've never seen a limit of 600MHz [for an application program].

Although not specifically with any brand compiler, I've actually seen a fair
amount of this kind of thing. Many "old" MS-DOS programs .. especially
games... have had problems when the CPU speed gets to numbers significantly
greater than the those on the systems available when the software was first
written.

Because "games" seem to be the lion's share of these 'CPU too fast'
problems, and many games were created by 'one-timer-programmers,'  I
attribute this to sloppy programming.. not performing a 'wait' when one
should (e.g., video refresh) ... which did not cause a problem on slower
machines which simply could not get to the next instruction fast enough to
make a difference.

My empirical evidence for this view is precisely ZERO.  I believe it
nontheless.

MCM
```

## Thread: If you knock, you should enter... — 6 msgs

### If you knock, you should enter...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T08:54:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0311050854.811f8d0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

can you please tell me what is the Difference and Advantages when
using the
old calls for subprograms, and the new calls for subprograms with the
'ENTRY' clause in the first line of the procedure division?
thanks a lot for your kind help. Kellie.

here is a cobol example.
   identification division.
   program-id. main.
   ...
   call 'function-get-time' using
                            get-time
   end-call.
   stop run.
   end program main.
   ---------------------------------------------
   identification division.
   program-id. function-get-time.
   data division.
   working-storage section.
   ...
   linkage section.
   01 get-time       comp-5 pic s9(09).
   procedure division.
   0000-main.
   entry 'function-get-time' using
                          get-time
   ...
   goback.
   end-program function-get-time.
```

#### ↳ Re: If you knock, you should enter...

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T13:07:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S5bqb.7190$fg4.268760@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41758a6b.0311050854.811f8d0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0311050854.811f8d0@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> Hello everyone,
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>    end-program function-get-time.

There is no advantage (that I know of) in the calling program.  The 
called program, however, can have multiple entry points.  The old method 
only allows one.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: If you knock, you should enter...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T18:12:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q3bqb.1609$Z25.1470@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0311050854.811f8d0@posting.google.com>`

```
A) The "entry" (not to be confused with the "ENTER") statement is not a part
of any current or recent ANSI/ISO Standard.  Therefore, it has "limited"
portability.  (Many/most IBM "compatible" compilers support it.  I don't
know about any others)

B) If the only ENTRY statement you have is "right after" the Procedure
Division header, it probably does nothing - or almost nothing "useful".
(Older - almost ancient - versions of IMS *required* an ENTRY statement, but
that hasn't been true for many releases.  You will still see it, however, in
such environments)

C) If you have MULTIPLE "ENTRY" statements in your program, this provides
some functionality.  Especially if you don't have an '85 Standard compiler
with EXTERNAL files/data and Nested Programs.  However, even there, you need
to be VERY careful if your calling program using a different number of
parameters when calling different entry points.  Addressability to
"non-passed" parameters may or may not be maintained when you call different
ENTRY points.  Compare the Micro Focus "Sticky-Linkage" and IBM
Dynamic/Static CALL restrictions).

D) In my PERSONAL opinion, if you do have an '85 Standard compiler with
EXTERNAL data/files and Nested programs, there really is NEVER a need to use
ENTRY statements in "current" (new or maintained programs).
```

```
in such a coherent logical manner.

Thanks

JCE
```

#### ↳ Re: If you knock, you should enter...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T16:14:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311051614.1aad45a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0311050854.811f8d0@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote

> can you please tell me what is the Difference and Advantages when
> using the
> old calls for subprograms, and the new calls for subprograms with the
> 'ENTRY' clause in the first line of the procedure division?
> thanks a lot for your kind help. Kellie.

Nested Programs are the 'new' subprograms ('85) while ENTRY is an
'old' non-standard mechanism that was, for example in MF Level II
Cobol which was '74 standard.
 
>    identification division.
>    program-id. function-get-time.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>    end-program function-get-time.

The 'entry' is redundant.  Put the USING clause on the procedure
division header:
 
     identification division.
     program-id. function-get-time.
     data division.
     working-storage section.
     ...
     linkage section.
     01 get-time       comp-5 pic s9(09).
     procedure division using get-time.
     function-main.
         ...
         exit program.
     end program function-get-time.

NOTEs: 
    Your example is not 'nested'.  
    The called name is provide by the program-id.
    If the program is using dynamic linkage then the call
        will try to resolve by filename, but the cancel
        may be by program-id.
    It is 'END PROGRAM name' with a space not a hyphen.
```

---

```
P.S.  I have no financial or other interest <G> in Micro Focus.  I do still
own a few shares of MERANT stock - but that has NOTHING to do with the "new"
Micro Focus!!!

P.P.S.  The first person who is actually ABLE to do an "impartial"
comparison between the Fujitsu and Micro Focus .NET products, will certainly

have "full" support for all the language supported in the older Net Express

```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Net Express® with .NET

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T17:53:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0311051753.74d3255f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Bill,

I am writing this from the viewpoint of an interested individual.  I
have not seen the product and my information is based only on what I
have read on the website.

For me - what matters more is what's under the covers.

Is the .NET implementation fully .NET?  That is - is it entirely
managed code?

For example - the Fujitsu .NET compiler uses an interface to native
code for indexed file access.  This means that if I used indexed
files, I need to distribute the Fujitsu runtime for Windows (and own
that product).

In addition - even if I don't use indexed files, I need to distribute
certain Fujitsu dll's as part of my assembly in order to ensure that
the program will run under the CLR.

Fujitsu provides helper classes for handing (or specifically not
handling) conversion of data and type validation in calls and invokes.

An additional - and very "handy" feature or inter-language use with
Fujitus's .NET product is the automatic conversion of group level
items to System.String objects.  This allows reference via SET and a
whole host of things to work very seamlessly under .NET.

I'm curious how MF handled this.

Fujitsu - at least in the initial release - made concessions on the
COBOL side in favor of .NET compliance.  (Such as lack of a Factory
object, and no multiple inheritence with COBOL).  What did MicroFocus
do?

I noticed the web page specifically mentioned console application
templates.  That leads me to believe - since it's not mentioned (that
I could find) I don't think there is COBOL integration for Winforms
and Webforms --- YES I know the site mentions that the CLASSES for
Winforms and Webforms can be used but with Fujitsu COBOL one can use
the intergrated Forms Designer and create this from COBOL.  It remains
an event driven interlaced code system (and I instead us SP2 for my UI
even under .NET).

One thing Fujitsu has not done - as of yet - is offer a direct
migration of PowerCOBOL code to .NET.  I don't see a mention of Dialog
System to .NET on the web page - though other pages tell people to ask
about Dialog System and .NET.

I'm very anxious to see what's what here and what's really going on -
how much is native code and how much is managed?  How much of the
managed are helper classes provided by MicroFocus?   How easy is it to
distribute a system written using the COBOL for .NET?


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>...
> For more information on Micro Focus' .NET COBOL product, see:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>      http://www.microfocus.com/promotion/NAWCFRFR1003/
```

```
Not an answer to your specific queries - but as a result of asking about OO drawing/painting, just days ago, unsolicited, the
following was included in the reply from M/F :-

"BTW - With the release of the .Net compiler today you can use
Winforms which has the new datagrid control. This allows editing of
data items but you need to move to .Net in order to use it. "

So much of this new fangled stuff comes along with an hurrah ! I think what should be more important to us developers is that
'enhanced' products contain backwards compatibility - how well either F/J or M/F have done that I have no idea. If you want an
analogy - if every five years we change a vehicle, we *do* expect it to be similar to what we were used to driving. If the new
'whiz bang' has the driver's seat placed in the back of the auto with a periscope to stare out - I don't think any of us is going
to be too thrilled.

My immediate concern is the emphasis on MICROSOFT just like Time Magazine ran those blurbs on the useless yuppies starting up
dot.coms until the flower wilted. No, I don't think MS is about to go belly up - but for 'shareholder' JerryMouse and his
reference to Linux cults - did he catch this piece in his local newspaper, roughly two weeks ago :-

Somewhat uncomfortable with MS "commercial power", both Japan and S.Korea are starting to plug Linux and are trying to bring the
People's Republic on board. It may never happen - BUT - if it does,  those three present a pretty significant number if you
starting counting all the zeroes in their population figures.

As for Bill's "performance query" - which nut is going to shell out the price for both compilers to find out <G>

Jimmy
```

```
Thane,

Where I talked about "Microsoft power" - I should have also added the following.

I recently had a message from somebody, (more than just competent in OO COBOL and 'Webbing'),  who must have been working on a Beta
of the product. He's already mentioned about using C++ (in preference to Java).
Get the drift ? To put it bluntly - Microsoft don't give a SHIT just so long as it has 'MS' emblazoned all over it. There will, where
advantageous, be a tendency for COBOL developers to move into VB, C++ etc...- and then from there...... ?.

Ironically the greatest enemies of COBOL programmers are - IBM and Microsoft.
MS has been advertising in Canada to outsource to them letting them 'do your thing' in MS products. For the second time in a row the
papers picked up on yet another large company, (something like 200-300 developers) who have been outsourced to IBM - the complete
staff is switched over to the IBM payroll - then of course the attrition takes place. Sorry don't remember the names but one
reference was to a big accounting house - Price Waterhouse, I think.

If this is going on in Canada, then it's a fair bet it is ten-fold in the U.S.

On a 'funny' note - from the 'International Express' - two outfits in UK, one of them being what I would have referred to as British
Rail (in my time), are contemplating outsourcing their query desks to India. So you phone for the next train from Portsmouth to
London - "My goodness gracious, I yam havin' much difficulty pleese. How is it, how do you spell Plotsmeth ?

PS. Ever tried phoning a local/state/federal department with an enquiry - where the person on the other end of the phone has a thick
Indian(East) accent ?

Jimmy
```

```
is it, how do you spell Plotsmeth ?
You are about 5 years behind.  Nowadays when you call a help desk the people
have been trained to talk just as you would expect them to.  That Chicago
accent is probably in Bangalore or Malaysia somewhere.  If they have an
accent that is indian then the company is "really" cheap....well this would
be the British government.

> PS. Ever tried phoning a local/state/federal department with an enquiry -
where the person on the other end of the phone has a thick
> Indian(East) accent ?
Whatever happened to all the Irish accents you used to hear?

JCE
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T15:00:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bfdfc3e8.0311051753.74d3255f@posting.google.com> <3FA9B0FA.E4BAE708@shaw.ca> <3FA9B9CA.647C74E3@shaw.ca> <T6mqb.111259$RP2.81894@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bodumj$149j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oGvqb.207541$0v4.16400626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:oGvqb.207541$0v4.16400626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

> My first car was an Austin 7, gifted to me by our IBM SE
> who had just indulged himself with a Jag.
>
> This isn't my car, but it looked a lot like it
> http://www.austin7.org/AYC.BMP

Nice!   I can't see the emblem, but it does look to me like it reads "Austin
Seven".  What I was referring to is that the Mini version emblem actually
read "Se7en".  I believe this was immortalized in a recurrent topic in Walt
Kelly's famous comic strip "Pogo" along the lines of "How do you spell
that?"  "With a seven."

Did the spelling "Se7en" in the emblems on the vehicle identifying it as an
Austin Seven predate the Mini version?   I thought this was an innovation
introduced with the resurrection of the famous old name for the shared
Austin/Morris product.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (OT) was Re: Micro Focus Net Express��� with .NET

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T01:04:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8iXqb.33321$Ec1.2859518@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bfdfc3e8.0311051753.74d3255f@posting.google.com> <3FA9B0FA.E4BAE708@shaw.ca> <3FA9B9CA.647C74E3@shaw.ca> <T6mqb.111259$RP2.81894@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bodumj$149j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oGvqb.207541$0v4.16400626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> that?"  "With a seven."

I'm not sure, but they may be referring to the movie?
>
> Did the spelling "Se7en" in the emblems on the vehicle identifying it as an
> Austin Seven predate the Mini version?   I thought this was an innovation
> introduced with the resurrection of the famous old name for the shared
> Austin/Morris product.

The car was introduced as the Austin Seven and the Morris Mini Minor,
if I remember correctly, as there was a very popular car called the
Morris Minor, which looked like a bloated VW bug.  I think that there
was a larger version called the Morris Major, but I'm not sure.
I do not recall ever seeing a Se7en emblem.  I think that that nomenclature
was just used in the advertising copy.

Regardless, nobody ever called the Morris or the Austin
anything but "a Mini".
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T22:40:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311072240.4857f8b9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bfdfc3e8.0311051753.74d3255f@posting.google.com> <3FA9B0FA.E4BAE708@shaw.ca> <3FA9B9CA.647C74E3@shaw.ca> <T6mqb.111259$RP2.81894@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bodumj$149j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oGvqb.207541$0v4.16400626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8iXqb.33321$Ec1.2859518@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote 

> I think that there
> was a larger version called the Morris Major, but I'm not sure.

There was, but (in time frame '50s and '60s) only in Australia.

The car was intended to replace the Minor with a 1200 cc motor, but
was only built in the UK as the Riley 1.5 and Wolsey 1500 with a 1500
cc motor.  It was built in Australia as the Morris Major and Austin
Lancer.
```

```
On Sat, 08 Nov 2003 01:04:04 GMT, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
enlightened us:

>
>Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The car was launched to the public on the 26th of August 1959 through
both Austin and Morris franchises worldwide. Badged as the Austin
Seven and the Morris Mini Minor the car was priced at ï¿½496. The first
winter of production saw a host of problems for the little car.
Leaking sills, rotting carpets, water logged distributors (later cars
were fitted with a card sheet to try to keep the electrics dry) to
name but a few. At the launch of the Mini, eighty cars were loaned to
leading figures to try to tempt the public into buying a
"technological marvel". This culminated in the Queen being taken for a
drive around Windsor Park by Alec Issigonis of BMC, the designer of
the car.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


"I once wanted to become an atheist but I gave up.
They have no holidays."
..... Henny Youngman
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (OT) was Re: Micro Focus Net Express��� with .NET

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-17T09:25:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bpb0ad$kuk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<TEaqb.1550$Z25.441@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bfdfc3e8.0311051753.74d3255f@posting.google.com> <3FA9B0FA.E4BAE708@shaw.ca> <3FA9B9CA.647C74E3@shaw.ca> <T6mqb.111259$RP2.81894@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bodumj$149j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oGvqb.207541$0v4.16400626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <boh860$f8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8iXqb.33321$Ec1.2859518@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:8iXqb.33321$Ec1.2859518@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> The car was introduced as the Austin Seven and the Morris Mini Minor,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I do not recall ever seeing a Se7en emblem.  I think that that
nomenclature
> was just used in the advertising copy.

No, that's just it.  It may have been an export-only issue, but we had a
local dealership that carried both the MiniMinor (and I remember it as
MiniMinor, not Mini Minor) and the Se7en upon their introduction to the US,
and I clearly remember the Austin's emblem *as imported here*.

This would have been sometime between September 1959 and June 1961 (and most
likely earlier than later in that period).  I visited the dealership at the
time and actually got to take one out -- probably the Morris variant -- for
a test drive!

I was out of the country (central Venezuela, of all places!) all summer in
'61, and by the following fall had my very own well-thrashed '52 MG TD
(those were the days -- my father paid the grand sum of $265 for it), so I
wasn't taking test drives on my visits to the dealership, only buying parts!
June 1961 is an absolute "terminus ad quem" for my introduction to the
MiniMinor variants; a more likely date would be not long after the release
data SkippyPB mentioned -- 26 August 1959.  It was big news back then, and
my visits to the dealership were at the height of the publicity about the
car.  Probably no later than November 1959.

I'm almost certain the Austin variant faded VERY quickly from the US market.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
are starting to plug Linux and are trying to bring the

> starting counting all the zeroes in their population figures.

A billion people is a lot.  The fact that 950,000,000 of these don't have
their own PCs probably means that the impact is not as great as one would
imagine.

It is in the interest of the pursuers of globalization to closely regulate
the level of freedom that these individuals have.  I can rest assured
knowing that the Chinese government is about to give 1 billion people the
freedom to unionize, collect and be empowered.  (As much as Friedman
suggests that this is going to happen in his eutopian global world).

Half of microsoft products in the world are ripped off anyway.

This has nothing to do with COBOL of course.

JCE
```

- **From:** annb@townofbreckenridge.com (AnnX)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cf12b640.0311051342.109bab13@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone help?
```

#### ↳ Re: Utility to Convert RM/COBOL 6.61.00

- **From:** david.engle@microxl.com (David E)
- **Date:** 2003-11-24T06:07:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96361a68.0311240607.54707595@posting.google.com>`

```
Ann, Unfortunately there is no known utility to convert data files for
RM/Cobol without having the source code that create the file itself. I
would however be interested in speaking with you about being able to
extract the data for you. I write extract programs for RM/Cobol data
files for others with needs such as your self. I believe I can provide
a low cost solution, could you provide some information about the
program?

annb@townofbreckenridge.com (AnnX) wrote in message news:<cf12b640.0311051342.109bab13@posting.google.com>...
> I need a utility to convert RM/COBOL data files to an ASCII format?
> This will be a one time conversion so I need it to be low in cost.
> Can anyone help?
```

#### ↳ Re: Utility to Convert RM/COBOL 6.61.00

- **From:** tkan@in.gr (theotyflos)
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T00:19:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3451a3f8.0311270019.148843da@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cf12b640.0311051342.109bab13@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Ann,

a) runcobol rmmapinx
b) enter the name of the file. This will show the file structure,
record length, and all the keys. This is all the info you need. Write
them down
c) write a programm and "select/fd" a dummy file with the above info
and another text file which will be your output. Read the dummy file,
move record to text file and write.

PS. If you don't have the original FD, there is no way for you to know
what fields exist in the record.

Hope it helps,
Theophilos.
```

```

This is why my current favorite extension to COBOL is one to provide
meat to the Identification Divison. Such as:

Ourput files would have a real standard label, i.e., the FD details
of the output file. Extending that might remove some of the problems
of cross product conversion or mix.

Warren Simmons

>
```

#### ↳ Re: Utility to Convert RM/COBOL 6.61.00

- **From:** tkan@in.gr (theotyflos)
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T00:38:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3451a3f8.0311270038.1522442c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cf12b640.0311051342.109bab13@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Ann,

If you don't have the original FD, there is no way to know what fields
exist in the record. You CAN however extract the records from ANY
indexed file to an ascii with the following procedure:

a) runcobol rmmapinx
b) enter filename
c) info about te file's structure, record size and keys displayed.
WRITE them down.
d) write a program that "select/fd" a "dummy indexed" file with the
above info and a text file which will be your output. Read dummy file
move record to text and write text.

Hope it helps,
Theophilos.

annb@townofbreckenridge.com (AnnX) wrote in message news:<cf12b640.0311051342.109bab13@posting.google.com>...

> This will be a one time conversion so I need it to be low in cost.
> Can anyone help?
```

## Thread: Cobol85 Problem — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** Greg Good <member46468@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T17:44:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com>`

```

I am trying to read a file that has records 300 bytes long. In some of
the records there are records containing characters that have a hex
value of '0C'. Each time my program encounters a hex '0C' it thinks that
it's reading a new record. How can I make my program ignore the hex '0C'
characters and keep the entire 300 bite record together?

Thank you for any help that you can give me,

Greg Good
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T18:03:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3564885.1068073423@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com>`

```

Originally posted by Greg Good 

> I am trying to read a file that has records 300 bytes long. In some of
> the records there are records containing characters that have a hex
> value of '0C'. Each time my program encounters a hex '0C' it thinks
> that it's reading a new record. How can I make my program ignore the
> hex '0C' characters and keep the entire 300 bite record together?

> Thank you for any help that you can give me,

> Greg Good 



What is the file format?  Is it indexed, line sequential, record
sequential?



Need more information in order to answer.





Christopher
```

- **From:** Greg Good <member46468@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T18:11:37-05:00

```
Greg Good
```

```
news:3564890.1068073897@dbforums.com...
```
