##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T15:06:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cstqb.99$WL.11195500@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com> <3564890.1068073897@dbforums.com>`

```
"Greg Good" <member46468@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3564890.1068073897@dbforums.com...
> The file was a sequential file on a mainframe and was downloaded to a
> CD.  I would call this file sequential.

Assuming you are on a Windows or UNix platform with one of the major COBOL
vendors...

In the SELECT statement in the FILE-CONTROL paragraph, try the following:

      SELECT file-name ASSIGN ...
          ORGANIZATION BINARY SEQUENTIAL

Then in the data division, make sure that the record definition is 300
characters.  One way to do this is:

      FD  file-name.
      01   a-record-name   PIC X(300).

Then in the procedure division:

     READ file-name INTO some-working-storage-area  AT END ...  END-READ.

There are two types of sequential on PC and Unix systems:
    LINE SEQUENTIAL - useful for text editors and printers and such, but
doesn't conform to semantic rules of COBOL and also does not have data
transparency (your problem), and
    BINARY SEQUENTIAL - does not work well with text editors and printers
and such, but DOES conform to the semantic rules of COBOL and DOES have data
transparency.

A further subtlety is that binary sequential files that are defined as fixed
length tend to be viewed as contiguous chunks of record-length characters,
which seems to be what you want.

Hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T11:05:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311061105.32b9bb10@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com> <3564890.1068073897@dbforums.com> <cstqb.99$WL.11195500@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com> wrote 

> Assuming you are on a Windows or UNix platform with one of the major COBOL
> vendors...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>           ORGANIZATION BINARY SEQUENTIAL

'BINARY SEQUENTIAL' is an extension for RM Cobol (only?).  Other
'major vendors' use 'RECORD SEQUENTIAL' or just 'SEQUENTIAL' when
defining a file that will be fixed length blocks with no record header
or terminator.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-05T22:10:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tmudnd-3EMUlVDSiU-KYjQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com>`

```
Greg Good wrote:
> I am trying to read a file that has records 300 bytes long. In some of
> the records there are records containing characters that have a hex
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greg Good

That's easy. You have to tell your program to read 300 bytes ignoring
control characters (X'0C' is a form-feed to those I/O routines looking for
that sort of thing).

You specified the organization of the file improperly. As to what the proper
definition is, I can't say.

Review the documentation of your compiler. Each compiler vendor has - and is
allowed - their own rules for file organizational definitions. Find the
definition that allows 300 bytes to be read irrespective of their contents
and use that. It may be "X" in

ORGANIZATION IS "X"

or in ASSIGN TO "X"

who knows? But that's what you have to discover.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** TofuTheGreat <member46811@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T09:57:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3567389.1068130656@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3564877.1068072267@dbforums.com>`

```

Originally posted by Greg Good 

> The file was a sequential file on a mainframe and was downloaded to a
> CD.  I would call this file sequential.

> Greg Good 

The reason I ask about the file organization is that I ran into a
similar problem with a CD that I got from data processing clearinghouse.



My incoming file was supposed to be record sequential but actually came
in as line sequential.  Once I put in "ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL"
the problem cleared right up.





Christopher
```

- **From:** dburrow@fds.com (David Burrow)

I'm investigating Cobol/Java and have this issue I'm hoping someone
can help with at the very basic of basic levels.

The goal: create and store a properties file in Enterprize (MVS/VM)
Cobol.

I can create a Properties object in Cobol and set properties within
it, but when I try and write to the file system, it complains that it
can't invoke the 'store' method of the object 'propConfig' (an
instance of Properties).

The following Java class works as expected... the subsequent rewrite
as
Enterprize Cobol fails with:

"IGZ0045S Unable to invoke method 'store' on line number 374 in COBOL
program TryProperties"

Line 374 refers to the Cobol statement:

Invoke propConfig "store"   using 
   by value                 objFileOutputStream
                            jstringUnicode

Any takers??

===== Java ======================================================

import java.util.Properties;
import java.io.FileOutputStream;

public class TryProperties
{
    public static void main(StringÝ¨ args)
    {
        Properties propConfig = new Properties();
        try
        {
           FileOutputStream fosSaveProperties 
              = new FileOutputStream("/tmp/new.properties");
            propConfig.setProperty("Key","Value");
            propConfig.store(fosSaveProperties,"This is a comment
header...");
            fosSaveProperties.close();
        }
        catch(Exception ex)
        {
            ex.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}

===== Cobol ======================================================

           cbl dll,thread,pgmname(longmixed),lc(0)

       Identification Division.
       Program-id. "TryProperties" recursive.

       Environment Division.
       Configuration Section.
       Repository.
           Class Base is
              "java.lang.Object"
           Class jstring is
              "jstring"
           Class JavaException is 
              "java.lang.Exception"
           Class Properties is
              "java.util.Properties"
           Class FileOutputStream is 
              "java.io.FileOutputStream".

       Data Division.
       Local-storage section.
       01  propConfig                  object reference 
                                       Properties.
       01  ex                          object reference 
                                       JavaException.
       01  objReturned                 object reference
                                       Base.
       01  objFileOutputStream         object reference 
                                       FileOutputStream.
                        
       01  rc                          pic s9(9)  value 0 comp-5.

       01  ebcdicString                pic x(500) value spaces.       
       01  propNameString              pic x(500) value spaces.
       01  propValString               pic x(500) value spaces.

       01  jstringUnicode              object reference 
                                       jstring.
       01  jstring1                    object reference 
                                       jstring.
       01  jstring2                    object reference 
                                       jstring.

       Linkage section.
       Copy JNI.

       Procedure division.

           Set address of JNIenv       to JNIEnvPtr
           Set address of JNINativeInterface to JNIenv
    
           Invoke Properties  New
           returning                   propConfig
           Perform 9999-Java-Exception-Check
           
           Move z"Key"                 to propNameString
           Move z"Value"               to propValString
           Perform 1000-Add-Property-Pair

           Move z"/tmp/new.properties" to ebcdicString
           Perform 0500-Ebcdic-Literal-To-Unicode
           
           Invoke FileOutputStream New using 
              by value                 jstringUnicode
              returning                objFileOutputStream
           Perform 9999-Java-Exception-Check
              
           Move z"This is a comment header....." 
                                       to ebcdicString
           Perform 0500-Ebcdic-Literal-To-Unicode
          
           Invoke propConfig "store"   using 
              by value                 objFileOutputStream
                                       jstringUnicode
           Perform 9999-Java-Exception-Check
                                     
           Invoke objFileOutputStream "close"
           Perform 9999-Java-Exception-Check                       
         
           Goback.

       0500-Ebcdic-Literal-To-Unicode. 

           Call "NewStringPlatform"    using
               by value                JNIEnvPtr
               address of              ebcdicString
               address of              jstringUnicode
                                       0
               returning               rc

           If rc not = zero then
              Display "Error occurred creating jstringUnicode object"
              Stop run
           End-if.
               
       1000-Add-Property-Pair.

           Move propNameString         to ebcdicString
           Perform 0500-Ebcdic-Literal-To-Unicode
           Set jstring1                to jstringUnicode            
           
           Move propValString          to ebcdicString
           Perform 0500-Ebcdic-Literal-To-Unicode
           Set jstring2                to jstringUnicode          
    
           Invoke propConfig "setProperty" using 
              by value                 jstring1
                                       jstring2
              returning                objReturned

       9999-Java-Exception-Check.
       
           Call ExceptionOccurred

               returning               ex
               
           If ex not = null then
               Call ExceptionClear using by value JNIEnvPtr
               Display "Caught an unexpected exception"
               Invoke ex "printStackTrace"
               Stop run
           End-if.          
              
       End Program "TryProperties".

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T18:29:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311061829.2634f5c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcf11c49.0311061209.5e1b3991@posting.google.com>`

```
kennyhelms@yahoo.com (Kenny) wrote 

> C:\src>htcobol testa.cob
> Input file 'testa.i' not found or not readable

It probably means that it cannot find the file 'tesa.i'

Perhaps you have a COPY of this specified in your source.
```

#### ↳ Re: TinyCobol---What is this error?

- **From:** David Essex <nospam@nobloodyspam.nospam>
- **Date:** 2003-11-06T22:52:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6GEqb.16731$uf3.6715@nntp-post.primus.ca>`
- **References:** `<dcf11c49.0311061209.5e1b3991@posting.google.com>`

```
Kenny wrote:

 > htcobol testa.cob
 > Input file 'testa.i' not found or not readable

TC has a pre-processor 'htcobolpp', which produces the intermediate '.i'
file from the COBOL source.

The pre-processor is run as a separate process.

If the intermediate file was not created probably means that the
pre-processor process did not run.

Did you set the environment variables ?

(Example)
SET TCOB_PP_PATH=D:\TinyCOBOL
SET TCOB_OPTIONS_PATH=D:\TinyCOBOL
```

## Thread: Filler keyword not mandatory? — 48 msgs

### Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** farmer@netnet.net (Mark A Framness)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T06:57:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings,

I am looking at some MF cobol code and see the following definition:

05  ws-report-fld1       pic x(10) value spaces.
05                       pic x(03) value spaces.
05  ws-report-fld2       pic x(05) value spaces.
05                       pic x(03) value spaces.
......

Apparently the filler keyword is optional. Is this a MF cobol thing or
is it widespread?

Not to start a religious war but IMO it is very poor style to not use
filler in the above example.

ttyl
```

```
Hi Mark,

RM/COBOL also allows FILLER to be optional.  Even the PICTURE clause may be
omitted for an elementary data item described with a VALUE clause that
specifies a literal.   I will not disagree with you about style.

Robert Heady
Liant Software Corp.

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T10:25:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`

```
Since the reserved word FILLER is optional in the 1985 ANSI COBOL 
Standard, why do you consider it bad form to omit it?  (If it were 
simply an extension put in by a compiler writer, I think I would feel 
differently, because it might make a program less portable to another 
compiler / platform.)

What advantage is gained by coding FILLER?  Whether FILLER is explicitly 
coded or not, the data item has no name, and cannot be referenced 
(except by reference modification).

Many programmers intentionally introduce white space into COBOL programs 
as a readability aid.  If it were your practice to align all the data 
names in a record description, the lack of anything on a line would tend 
to catch your eye and tell you "here is some FILLER".  Or, if you were 
coding a literal whose purpose was to help you find your way in a dump, 
you could get more of the literal ona single line by omitting the word 
FILLER.

Mark A Framness wrote:
> Greetings,
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> ttyl
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T05:59:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```
"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this> wrote:
> Since the reserved word FILLER is optional in the 1985 ANSI COBOL
> Standard, why do you consider it bad form to omit it?  (If it were
> simply an extension put in by a compiler writer, I think I would feel
> differently, because it might make a program less portable to another
> compiler / platform.)

The very fact that you rarely seen it done is sufficient evidence that it
is poor form. In design there is an important but often neglected rule
called "the rule of least astonishment." The rule of least astonishment
says essentially that the use of any device/feature/etc. should be as
intuitively obvious as possible. IOW, in a way that causes the least
astonishment. This rule has broad application to any type of human
interface, even that of computer listing/human eye-brain. Since you
rarely see FILLER omitted, it is clearly most intuitive that FILLER be
present for best clarity and least astonishment. Q.E.D. :-)
```

```

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T13:47:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon writes ...

[snip]

>The very fact that you rarely seen it done is sufficient evidence that it
>is poor form. In design there is an important but often neglected rule
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>present for best clarity and least astonishment. Q.E.D. :-

I can't believe you put that forward as a legitimate argument.

The reason you don't see omitted filler very much is simple:

1) it wasn't an option for many years, so everyone coded filler
2) old habits die hard

But I'm with the poster who said they never code it anymore and remove it from
existing code when they do maintenance. Once you code this way the old way
seems ugly and cumbersome.

Of course, I also force code to mixed case when I do maintenance, turn
numbering off, and if I have time I'll change 'comp' to 'binary' and 'comp-3'
to 'packed-decimal'. 

Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-10T05:56:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0MFrb.6613$O2.293722@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com>`

```
"S Comstock" <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com...
> Judson McClendon writes ...
> [snip]
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I'm with the poster who said they never code it anymore and remove it
from
> existing code when they do maintenance. Once you code this way the old way
> seems ugly and cumbersome.

I wonder what overall monetary savings companies achieve by paying people
while they replace the word FILLER which does nothing with spaces which does
nothing.

Because you don't need it doesn't make it ugly.  I know plenty of people who
like to see bellybuttons but yet the only use for it seems to be collect
fluff and to reveal.

The same argument that you use is the same one that old PL/I / REXX
programmers when they tell you that declarations are for weenies.   If you
don't need to declare it, why would you? The meaning is obvious when you use
it.

These are the same people that don't initialize variables because - well I
don't need to initialize it because I *always* set it.

Or the coders who don't code END-IF because they don't need to....

I'm one of those who believe that people do this because it coder
one-up-man-ship.

One person finds out and does it because it's "clever".  The others then
follow because obviously it's "clever"...and then it's just the local
standard.  I fail to see any benefit at all to not coding the word FILLER.
It looks and appears more sensible to me with it...you have the LEVEL NAME
TYPE - it's consistent and "least surprise" method..

So bottom line - it's a style.  In my world, maintenance doesn't include
fixing style unless that style change serves a useful purpose at which point
it is not a matter of style.

> Of course, I also force code to mixed case when I do maintenance, turn
> numbering off, and if I have time I'll change 'comp' to 'binary' and
'comp-3'
> to 'packed-decimal'.
Because this saves the company money?


> Kind regards,
> -Steve Comstock
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-10T10:50:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311101050.6e392de@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com> <0MFrb.6613$O2.293722@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote

> I wonder what overall monetary savings companies achieve by paying people
> while they replace the word FILLER which does nothing with spaces which does
> nothing.

How many milliseconds does it take to make "g/ FILLER /         /s"
into a macro that is callable later in another session ?

> Because you don't need it doesn't make it ugly. 

Some people spend half their coding life adding full stops to every
line and then removing them again when indenting the code, and adding
them back when outdenting.

> I know plenty of people who
> like to see bellybuttons but yet the only use for it seems to be collect
> fluff and to reveal.

Did you have another use for them with which I am yet unaquainted ?
 
> The same argument that you use is the same one that old PL/I / REXX
> programmers when they tell you that declarations are for weenies.   If you
> don't need to declare it, why would you? The meaning is obvious when you use
> it.

So why would you ?

Compulsory declarations (eg in Cobol) are a good thing, but if the
language has auto-declaration then using declarations is no advantage.
 
> These are the same people that don't initialize variables because - well I
> don't need to initialize it because I *always* set it.

Surely 'setting it' _is_ 'initializing'.  What's your point ?

If a variable is used before it is 'set' to a real value, then it
doesn't matter whether it has been 'initialised' to some specific
wrong value or left with some random wrong value.

In fact it is often better to have complete garbage because it is more
likely to be noticed.

In one recent thread here the programmer did the calculations _before_
the data record was read.  The 'solution' provided was to initialize
the calculation result to avoid the 163 error.  This, of course, left
the program outputting the wrong values on the report, the calculation
from the previous record.

I prefer to get a 163 error rather than a subtley wrong result.

> I'm one of those who believe that people do this because it coder
> one-up-man-ship.

Whereas your form of 'coder one-up-man-ship' is completely in the
other direction ?
 
> One person finds out and does it because it's "clever".  The others then
> follow because obviously it's "clever"...and then it's just the local
> standard.  I fail to see any benefit at all to not coding the word FILLER.

That's OK, no one is asking you to not code them.  If you can come up
with an _actual_ argument to convince anyone that they are beneficial
or useful then please do so.  Allusions and innuendo are not
convincing arguments.

> It looks and appears more sensible to me with it...you have the LEVEL NAME
> TYPE - it's consistent and "least surprise" method..

It is only 'surprising' if you are unaware that it is possible.  Do
try and keep up, at least to the 90s.

> > Of course, I also force code to mixed case when I do maintenance, turn
> > numbering off, and if I have time I'll change 'comp' to 'binary' and
>  'comp-3'
> > to 'packed-decimal'.
> Because this saves the company money?

I will change case on code, but I use 'Modula-2' style where all
keywords are upper case and user variables are mixed case.  This
_does_ save me time (and thus the company money) because it allows me
to 'parse' the code faster and to make fewer mistakes.  For example I
have syntax colouring on my editor so the keywords should show up in
different colour.  If an uppercase word has the wrong colour then it
stands out and may be a misspelt keyword.  This saves me compiles and
helps concentrate on the actual code rather than the details.

(I do have a block 'proper case' function in the editor.)

Making them lower case though, that is just a waste of time to try to
look 'modern'     ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-16T07:27:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%FFtb.181370$RP2.127081@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com> <0MFrb.6613$O2.293722@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <217e491a.0311101050.6e392de@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0311101050.6e392de@posting.google.com...
> "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote
>
> > I wonder what overall monetary savings companies achieve by paying
people
> > while they replace the word FILLER which does nothing with spaces which
does
> > nothing.
>
> How many milliseconds does it take to make "g/ FILLER /         /s"
> into a macro that is callable later in another session ?
About 10000? But then you have to remember what the macro is called...and I
don't have enough room for that much stuff ;-)

> > Because you don't need it doesn't make it ugly.
> Some people spend half their coding life adding full stops to every
> line and then removing them again when indenting the code, and adding
> them back when outdenting.
That's quite interesting.  I've noticed the same phenomenon in other
places....I've notice in COBOL that when people add an IF they add an
END-IF.  I also have known people in Java that have removed a { an } and
then later had to add a { and }somewhere else in the code....I don't know
what I would do without File->Tools->Autoformat :-).

> > I know plenty of people who
> > like to see bellybuttons but yet the only use for it seems to be collect
> > fluff and to reveal.
>
> Did you have another use for them with which I am yet unaquainted ?
No, I could only think of those two.....but apparently some people still
like to see them - and *that* was my point.

> > These are the same people that don't initialize variables because - well
I
> > don't need to initialize it because I *always* set it.
> Surely 'setting it' _is_ 'initializing'.  What's your point ?
There's a difference between a declaration and an initialization.  IMHO One
should *always* place a variable in a state that will be "expected".
A ptr should never be declared and not initialized - that's what I was
taught...and in general the only problems I've had with pointers is when I
for some reason ?! forgot to do this.  It's not that easy to find...my
program ran on 3 AIX boxes in test, and one WIN2000 machine in test and then
much later debugging.  The production WIN2K machine crapped out EACH and
EVERY time.  The problem was the non initialization of a pointer.

Most java compilers will give a 'warning' if it is used before being
initialized....and following my principle of "no unexplained"
warnings...generally I fix them.

This isn't necessarily as much of a problem in procedural code where the use
of variables is very structured and defined...however, in a multithreaded OO
environment, not initializing properly can be a major headache.

 > If a variable is used before it is 'set' to a real value, then it
> doesn't matter whether it has been 'initialised' to some specific
> wrong value or left with some random wrong value.
> In fact it is often better to have complete garbage because it is more
> likely to be noticed.
Yes, but in the case of buffer overflows this may be found in production and
take a lot of time to debug.

> In one recent thread here the programmer did the calculations _before_
> the data record was read.  The 'solution' provided was to initialize
> the calculation result to avoid the 163 error.  This, of course, left
> the program outputting the wrong values on the report, the calculation
> from the previous record.
But I would classify this as a logic problem and not a code problem - and
therefore irrelevant to the argument.

> > I'm one of those who believe that people do this because it coder
> > one-up-man-ship.
> Whereas your form of 'coder one-up-man-ship' is completely in the
> other direction ?
No. That would be one-downs-man-ship given that coding FILLER is,
apparently, bloody stupid.
I believe that coding FILLER is acceptable.  I believe not coding FILLER is
acceptable.
I believe that someone going into a shop that codes FILLER and telling them
that FILLER is obsolete and therefore the use of it is "ugly" is, in a
sense, trying to show how smart he / she is.  But that does depend on the
method of teaching.  If there is general approval very quickly then it is
just called "sharing" which I *really* approve of - if there is general
poo-pooing of the "new" idea then it is being a wisenheimer.

> > One person finds out and does it because it's "clever".  The others then
> > follow because obviously it's "clever"...and then it's just the local
> > standard.  I fail to see any benefit at all to not coding the word
FILLER.
> That's OK, no one is asking you to not code them.  If you can come up
> with an _actual_ argument to convince anyone that they are beneficial
> or useful then please do so.  Allusions and innuendo are not
> convincing arguments.
I gave you my opinion....you choose to not agree...that's your prerogative.

I like the consistency of LEVEL NAME TYPE.  I see no *real* benefit to
either leaving it in or out other than  it's used where I work right now so
why rock the boat.
I think not rocking the boat in a development team is very important when
the level of benefit of rocking said boat would be nearly zero.

> > It looks and appears more sensible to me with it...you have the LEVEL
NAME
> > TYPE - it's consistent and "least surprise" method..
> It is only 'surprising' if you are unaware that it is possible.  Do
> try and keep up, at least to the 90s.
I thought this was an 85 standard - so I should try and keep up with the 80s
I guess...I just watched We are the 80s 1985 and saw no reference to the
COBOL standard.
I did miss out on the ANSI announcement - I was probably just discovering
girls and boys are allowed to like each other.

> > > Of course, I also force code to mixed case when I do maintenance, turn
> > > numbering off, and if I have time I'll change 'comp' to 'binary' and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> helps concentrate on the actual code rather than the details.
The weird thing for me is that I code all Java/C/C++ with very specific
format  lowercaseUppercase (though I don't use _m for member variables as
some do).  All my COBOL and PL/I has been all CAPS.  The reason being that
until recently that was all I ever saw and that as I do think that as the
language is not case sensitive it is easier to be consistent if they are all
one case or the other and not mixed.
My editor colours key words and variables a different colour without
worrying about case.

I actually could care less if the company saves money at this level.  The
overhead of a few measly lines of code is usually insignificant to any
overall budget.

> (I do have a block 'proper case' function in the editor.)
> Making them lower case though, that is just a waste of time to try to
> look 'modern'     ;-)
I don't know what proper case blocking is......and it's hard to look modern
if you editor has COBOL in it :-)

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-16T10:00:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311161000.2a5502e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com> <0MFrb.6613$O2.293722@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <217e491a.0311101050.6e392de@posting.google.com> <%FFtb.181370$RP2.127081@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote 

> ....I don't know
> what I would do without File->Tools->Autoformat :-).

I tried that and didn't like it at all, too clumsy.  I have keyboard
macros that give me most keywords with 2 keystrokes.  eg F9+F ->
FILLER.
 
>  > If a variable is used before it is 'set' to a real value, then it
> > doesn't matter whether it has been 'initialised' to some specific
> > wrong value or left with some random wrong value.
> > In fact it is often better to have complete garbage because it is more
> > likely to be noticed.

> Yes, but in the case of buffer overflows this may be found in production and
> take a lot of time to debug.

You seem to be swinging between problems.  What has 'buffer overflow'
got to do with 'initializing' ?  They are quite different problems.
 
> > In one recent thread here the programmer did the calculations _before_
> > the data record was read.  The 'solution' provided was to initialize
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> therefore irrelevant to the argument.

In what way is 'printing a value before setting it' different from
using a pointer before setting it ?  In both cases 'initialising' (as
distinct from setting to a useful value) doesn't make the program
correct.

> No. That would be one-downs-man-ship given that coding FILLER is,
> apparently, bloody stupid.

There was one sarcastic remark in response to the implication that
leaving out FILLER was 'stupid'.

> I believe that coding FILLER is acceptable.  I believe not coding FILLER is
> acceptable.

That's all right then.

> I believe that someone going into a shop that codes FILLER and telling them
> that FILLER is obsolete and therefore the use of it is "ugly" is, in a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> poo-pooing of the "new" idea then it is being a wisenheimer.

I get the impression that your reaction is more based on the
personalities involved rather than the technical merits.

> I gave you my opinion....you choose to not agree...that's your prerogative.

Yes, but you gave me your opinion on how you deal with the
personalities involved, not on technical merits.

> > stands out and may be a misspelt keyword.  This saves me compiles and
> > helps concentrate on the actual code rather than the details.

> My editor colours key words and variables a different colour without
> worrying about case.

Yes, but when I see an uppercase word that is _not_ coloured as a
keyword then it is probably a misspelling.

> I don't know what proper case blocking is......

Proper Case Is Where Each Word Starts With A Capital.  It is normally
used for proper names, hense its applicablity to computer languages
where the variables have names.

> and it's hard to look modern
> if you editor has COBOL in it :-)

But is only looks old fashioned because it is all upper case.  When
Cobol is coded in the same case style as other languages it can look
just like those to a casual observer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-16T19:05:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5UPtb.24182$w66.528077@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <20031108084728.29118.00000314@mb-m23.aol.com> <0MFrb.6613$O2.293722@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <217e491a.0311101050.6e392de@posting.google.com> <%FFtb.181370$RP2.127081@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <217e491a.0311161000.2a5502e2@posting.google.com>`

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0311161000.2a5502e2@posting.google.com...
> "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote
> I get the impression that your reaction is more based on the
> personalities involved rather than the technical merits.
....
> Yes, but you gave me your opinion on how you deal with the
> personalities involved, not on technical merits.

Yes. Probably. This is why I am currently pursuing a career outside of
development.

I'm not sure that I really care about the technical merit of explicit vs
implicit FILLER.

To me, there is no business without personality.  There is no code without
creativity.  I cannot teach people to follow a thinking pattern.  People
think and process the way they have since they were young.  I'd rather
encourage each individual to work to their strengths with some "large"
boundary conditions than encourage people to to adhere to strict "narrow"
rules.
I've yet to find any situation which could not have been coded
differently...

Therefore my arguments generally will focus on the people aspect of code.
This to me is appropriate until the time when we no longer have coders...

> > and it's hard to look modern
> > if you editor has COBOL in it :-)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> just like those to a casual observer.
And once it has compiled then it *really* looks no different.

I understand and appreciate each one of your arguments.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T08:51:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031108095150.468$eG@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <yG6rb.10655$Pg1.576463@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hello Donald.  Actually, I was just trying to liven things up a bit. (see
the smiley :-). Though the rule of least astonishment is perfectly valid,
it is not the only design criterion, of course. The question is "Is there
enough benefit from omitting FILLER to make it worth while violating
the rule of least astonishment?" I'm sure opinions differ, but I lean
toward not. Change always costs something, and sometimes a lot (e.g.
Microsoft unnecessarily changing the human interface to every product
they sell, on every release, so they can sell more training). But if the
benefits outweigh the costs, go for it. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-11-14T14:13:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<usctb.235$b77.64@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20031108095150.468$eG@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <yG6rb.10655$Pg1.576463@news20.bellglobal.com> <20031108095150.468$eG@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Hello Donald.  Actually, I was just trying to liven things up a bit. (see
> the smiley :-). Though the rule of least astonishment is perfectly valid,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> benefits outweigh the costs, go for it. :-)
I believe your argument is reversed.  FILLER is an optional word 
(although it once was required).

So, the real question is:
"Is there enough benefit to coding FILLER to make it worthwhile to 
expose one's probable ignorance of the 1985 and 2002 ANSI COBOL Standard?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-11-15T13:36:19+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fb5751e_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <yG6rb.10655$Pg1.576463@news20.bellglobal.com> <20031108095150.468$eG@news.newsreader.com> <usctb.235$b77.64@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this> wrote in message
news:usctb.235$b77.64@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> > Hello Donald.  Actually, I was just trying to liven things up a bit.
(see
> > the smiley :-). Though the rule of least astonishment is perfectly
valid,
> > it is not the only design criterion, of course. The question is "Is
there
> > enough benefit from omitting FILLER to make it worth while violating
> > the rule of least astonishment?" I'm sure opinions differ, but I lean
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's a  question that is just as stupid as Judson's.

Judson's "rule of Least astonishment" is a variation on the information
theory tenet proposed by Marshall McLuhan that "data has value according to
its unexpectedness".  It has nothing to do with coding style in COBOL or any
other Language.  Richard has pointed out many times here that what we
perceive as "familiar" in code is what we consider "good". As "familarity"
must depend on each individual's experience, "good" code is a purely
subjective assessment and must come down to "style".

In fact, all of the "Religious Wars" we have seen in this forum over the
years are simply pointless. (That is not to say that they aren't fun or
entertaining...<G>). We can agree on points of style (and that is what Local
Standards attempt to achieve) but that of itself will not make an iota of
difference to whether code is "good" or "bad" when the new chum has to
maintain it. (It will help, once he goes into agreement with the site
standards, but until then it is no help. That is why we allow a "learning
curve" for new programmers. Not because they aren't trained or can't do the
job (if that were true, they wouldn't be staying very long) but because they
have to "learn" our site-specifc way of doing things... the "good" way <G>).

Being swayed only by cost/benefit analysis is another purely subjective
principle. (I see more shades of grey...cost benefit is ONE (an, admittedly,
important)  factor, but not the ONLY factor on which I would make a
descision. For example, If the cost/benefit of implementing a solution is
extremely attractive, but I can't afford that solution anyway, it really
doesn't matter HOW much cost benefit there is...).

Coding isn't necessarily JUST about benefit in "efficiency" terms (because
the "efficiency" definition varies depending on circumstances and
environments), and to assume a lack of knowledge because someone chooses to
do something a certain way is just presumptous.

I am very well aware of the status of FILLER, yet I continue to code it, and
will do so in the future.

It's because FILLER has a very particular meaning for me.

It doesn't just mean "I can't think of a name for this bit of storage and I
don't think I'm likely to reference it...".

I have never used the forms described here of:  Filler01,  rec-filler02, and
suchlike, because I don't consider that is proper use of FILLER.

Neither have I ever had a problem with INITIALIZE not moving spaces or
anything else to FILLER.

FILLER in my code means: "This is a piece of storage which I am CERTAIN I am
NEVER going to reference, but which I should be aware of, because I may
REDEFINE it or its higher levels, and I may expect it to have a certain
static value that will never change. (As in table definitions,
f'rinstance...)".

Yes, I know you can do exactly the same and just omit the dataname FILLER,
but there are many things in COBOL which give you an option and the
individual use of them constitutes one aspect of "style".

For me the "benefit" in coding FILLER is that I can immediately see what
unreferenced storage is allocated in my program (FILLER entries stick out
like sore thumbs) and, if I want to use any source code processing (and I
have several  processors that I wrote many years ago), they will work
correctly.

(When I wrote these processors I operated on the assumption that a data
definition would contain a dataname somewhere in it, normally following the
level number, but a quick reserved word check makes it definite... FILLER is
a reserved word; any other dataname is not.... I COULD go back and update
them but I have neither time nor inclination to do so. I can't help
wondering how many OTHER COBOL Source processors expect FILLER also...)

I don't think people who don't use FILLER are wrong; any more than someone
who codes ACCESS SEQUENTIAL rather than ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL is "wrong".

I DO think it is wrong to make assumptions about what people know, based on
how they code (or any other indirect evidence, actually).

And I DO think both...

" The question is "Is there enough benefit from omitting FILLER to make it
worth while violating the rule of least astonishment?"

AND

"So, the real question is: "Is there enough benefit to coding FILLER to make
it worthwhile to expose one's probable ignorance of the 1985 and 2002 ANSI
COBOL Standard?"

...are just plain silly.

People will code FILLER (or NOT) because they CHOOSE to. It has nothing to
do with imagined "cost benefits", "efficiency", "rules of least
astonishment", or perceived lack of knowledge about COBOL standards.

And whatever they decide is not "wrong".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T20:19:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sbjqqvcrh0mmlu1dpac0b0j98ksu6qmjfg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`

```
On Sat, 8 Nov 2003 05:59:15 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this> wrote:
>> Since the reserved word FILLER is optional in the 1985 ANSI COBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>is poor form. In design there is an important but often neglected rule
Don't agree with that.
So far all the places I have worked with (and that had cobol 85) had
stopped using them as soon as ONE of their programmers was made aware
of that option.

So for me most of the companies I worked with already had their filler
removed in most of the programs, but not all, as they would not change
the program just for that.

Many of the customers I worked with on Unix would do it like this.
/PROCEDURE DIVISION + [enter]
:1,.s/ FILLER /s//        /g + [enter]

which translated to about 10 seconds of work.


The one exception to this rule was a very specific customer that used
a CODE generator, and that one of their requirements was that the code
compiled with a 74 compiler!!, so even though they would like to
remove it they were not allowed to do it.

>called "the rule of least astonishment." The rule of least astonishment
>says essentially that the use of any device/feature/etc. should be as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>present for best clarity and least astonishment. Q.E.D. :-)
So since up to a certain stage on my career I (and others) did not see
correctly structured code , but only spaghetti full of GOTO's and
ALTER it is clearly most intuitive that a mixture of
spaghetti+GOTO+ALTER code is present for best clarity. Q.E.D.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-D4026C.21435210112003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`

```
In article <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>,
 "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> "Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this> wrote:
> > Since the reserved word FILLER is optional in the 1985 ANSI COBOL
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> present for best clarity and least astonishment. Q.E.D. :-)

I have to disagree with you.  I have rarely seen "FILLER" actually 
coded.  

And I would consider it just added noise that contributes nothing to the 
understanding of the program.  IMNSHO it falls into that same Cobol 
space as the ',' character -- never necessary and always a waste of a 
keystroke.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-11T15:42:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bor022$ajm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <joe_zitzelberger-D4026C.21435210112003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 10-Nov-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> I have to disagree with you.  I have rarely seen "FILLER" actually
> coded.

Interesting.   I have rarely seen it missing.   My experience has been virtually
all mainframes of various makes.   Where has the code you have maintained been?

> And I would consider it just added noise that contributes nothing to the
> understanding of the program.  IMNSHO it falls into that same Cobol
> space as the ',' character -- never necessary and always a waste of a
> keystroke.

Back when we used impact printers, I found commas to be dangerous (looked like
periods), so I switched to semi-colons.   I can type faster than I can think, so
commas and white space don't cost me anything.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-12T08:18:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-67EAC0.08182912112003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com> <joe_zitzelberger-D4026C.21435210112003@corp.supernews.com> <bor022$ajm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bor022$ajm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 10-Nov-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> been?

Various large institutions in the financial services sector, 
specifically credit reporting, credit scoring and card processing areas.  
All IBM mainframe.  Since I started life as an assembler programmer and 
only began using Cobol seriously in the early 90's, most of the Cobol 
applications I have worked on have been written using post 85 standard.  
My last 74-to-85 conversion was in 97.

I often see people using "FILLER"-like data names, e.g. MyRec-Filler-01, 
MyRec-Filler-02 ... MyRec-Filler-nn, and I've seen some places codify 
that into standard.  But that is an actual data name, and follows the 
rules as such, it is not a filler identification.  Use of the actual 
"FILLER" keyword, not very frequent.


> > And I would consider it just added noise that contributes nothing to the
> > understanding of the program.  IMNSHO it falls into that same Cobol
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> commas and white space don't cost me anything.

Move A to X; Y; Z?  Why put anything at all?
```

> > Back when we used impact printers, I found commas to be dangerous (looked

Where the cost is minimal, I prefer to add clarity.   White space and semicolons
have minimal cost, so I make it easy to read and to make it obvious what my
intentions are.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this>
- **Date:** 2003-11-14T14:09:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7octb.234$b77.55@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <KsRqb.168$b77.218@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com> <20031108065939.352$af@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net_remove_this> wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> present for best clarity and least astonishment. Q.E.D. :-)

The very fact that many COBOL programmers began writing code before the 
1985 ANSI COBOL Standard was adopted, and that FILLER was not an 
optional word in the 1968 or 1974 ANSI COBOL Standard would explain why 
it is rarely seen.  It is also possible to speculate that many COBOL 
programmers have not actually read a Language Reference manual to learn 
whether their coding style could be modified.

In other words, if people are astonished to see a data item described 
without the word FILLER, it may well be because they are ignorant. 
Isn't that astonishing?!
```

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T11:49:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311071149.1ad1d09e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`

```
farmer@netnet.net (Mark A Framness) wrote 

> Apparently the filler keyword is optional. Is this a MF cobol thing or
> is it widespread?

The keyword FILLER is optional.  This was introduced in the ANS'85
standard.  With MF (and some others) it is usually easy to distinguish
between standard code and extensions because the extensions are
highlighted in the syntax diagrams and marked (MF), (OSVS), etc.  Of
course those marked (ANS85) are part of that standard.
 
> Not to start a religious war but IMO it is very poor style to not use
> filler in the above example.

All styles that one is not used to are 'poor' until one gets used to
them.
```

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T21:55:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<boh4d6$ehr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`

```

On  7-Nov-2003, farmer@netnet.net (Mark A Framness) wrote:

> Not to start a religious war but IMO it is very poor style to not use
> filler in the above example.

Some people have recommended using it in the following case:

05                   PIC X.
88   SW-EOF              VALUE "E".
88   SW-NOT-EOF       VALUE 'N".


This means they can assume that the switch is not set by a MOVE statement.   
However, those using a debugger note that this also means that you have no field
name to break on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T16:29:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Mark A Framness wrote:
> Greetings,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> filler in the above example.

There are those who avoid "FILLER" altogether. Instead they code, as per
your example,

05  ws-report-spare1  pic x(03) value space
...
05  ws-report-spare2 pic x(05) value space.

As you know, INITIALIZE skips fields defined by FILLER. The above construct
causes INITIALIZE to place spaces in the field.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T14:37:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<boh6s6$e88$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <5Ladnf-__6I0gTGiU-KYiw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:5Ladnf-__6I0gTGiU-KYiw@giganews.com...

> As you know, INITIALIZE skips fields defined by FILLER. ...

Not unconditionally in 2002 COBOL.  The syntax for the INITIALIZE statement
has been enhanced to allow initializing FILLER clauses, and also to allow
the re-application of VALUE clauses (on FILLERs or otherwise).

Some have suggested this be added to existing '85 implementations, as many
users seem to have found the omission of FILLER items from the fields that
are initialized in '85 COBOL, as well as the inability to reestablish VALUE
clauses through INTIALIZE in that dialect, counterintuitive.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T23:21:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZNVqb.1785$nz.1606@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <5Ladnf-__6I0gTGiU-KYiw@giganews.com> <boh6s6$e88$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
For those readers of CLC who are ALSO part of SHARE (IBM user groups) and
who are interested in the enhancements to the INITIALIZE statement to allow
INITIALIZE of FILLER items (and/or initialize to VALUE), please make certain
that you (or someone in your organization) votes on the current SHARE
requirement:

  SSLNGC0313594  2002 ISO COBOL - Enhanced INITIALIZE (To VALUE & FILLER)


If your organization belongs to SHARE (and you know your installation code)
but you don't currently have a user id for voting, please go to:


http://www.share.org/share/website/shareweb.nsf/1d99cd65f2badf1e86256b78006ccb15/d84e1cb707541ed686256bf90066b343?OpenDocument

and follow the "links" from the "register" option.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

```

On  7-Nov-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> There are those who avoid "FILLER" altogether. Instead they code, as per
> your example,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

I've always seen that as FILLER01, FILLER02, FILLER03, etc., or simply FILLER1.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-17T08:50:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bpau96$jge$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <5Ladnf-__6I0gTGiU-KYiw@giganews.com> <booj48$i62$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:booj48$i62$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> >
> > As you know, INITIALIZE skips fields defined by FILLER. The above
construct
> > causes INITIALIZE to place spaces in the field.

One of the new features of the 2002 standard is INITIALIZE ... WITH FILLER.
My strong suspicion is that the INITIALIZE enhancements in that standard
will make their way into many an '85-compliant compiler as extensions.  Many
people have complained to me that the failure of INITIALIZE to initialize
FILLERs as well as the failure of INITIALIZE to use the VALUE clauses
associated with either FILLERs or data-names is at best counterintuitive.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

```
Mark A Framness wrote:
> Greetings,

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> is it widespread?

It's pretty widespread, since it was part of the '85 standard.

> Not to start a religious war but IMO it is very poor style to not use
> filler in the above example.

I disagree.  Were your eyes not immediately drawn to the "actual" data

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Andy Decepida <andydecepida@-No-sPam-sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T10:34:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<483qqv01knits8dp36v279o3lku3pumdu0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <vqomlipl207se6@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Fri, 07 Nov 2003 20:53:38 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Mark A Framness wrote:
>> Greetings,
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>simply for that reason.

It's true.  It's simpler, easier to do and elegant in being minimalist
and without detracting from function.

How many times have stray mispellings of FILLER cluttered up your XREF
listing when hunting for either unused declarations or whatever?  Not
using FILLER speaks for itself.  It is unreferenced and not directly
addressable so why should it be given a token or name at all.

FILLER is clutter... just like commas.  They're just detritus to the
compiler since the parser phase will just have more to deal with when
they're seen.
```

#### ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us>
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T08:42:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FC5B8CA.732C39BC@paul.washington.dc.us>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com>`

```
Mark A Framness wrote:

> Greetings,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> filler in the above example.

Most assembly languages allow unnamed data fields as a means of defining
an unused place holder.  With the exception of COBOL, most high-level
languages do not offer that capability.  FORTRAN does provide 'blank
common' but the variables within COMMON must be declared.  C has the
STRUCT construct but doesn't allow unnamed fields.  Pascal has the RECORD
construct for the same thing but it doesn't allow unnamed fields.  Some
versions of BASIC have the FIELD construct but it doesn't allow unnamed
fields either.  RPG II does, I think.

Personally, I would think not giving a name to a field that is not being
used would be more appropriate than giving it a fictitious name of
'FILLER' to 60 or 70 (or 1,000 if the program is big enough) different
fields, all with the same name.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Filler keyword not mandatory?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T07:03:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031127080415.804$20@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<8168768c.0311070657.b72c91b@posting.google.com> <3FC5B8CA.732C39BC@paul.washington.dc.us>`

```
"Paul Robinson" <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us> wrote:
>
> Personally, I would think not giving a name to a field that is not being
> used would be more appropriate than giving it a fictitious name of
> 'FILLER' to 60 or 70 (or 1,000 if the program is big enough) different
> fields, all with the same name.

On the other hand, anyone who knows COBOL knows what FILLER means.
And further, humans like consistency at the base perceptual level (whether
they realize it or not), and the pattern

    <level> <name> ...

is, if always repeated, very consistent. :-)

I know I keep trumpeting this, but most programmers seem oblivious that
the human eye and brain work in certain ways that are not always
appreciated. For example, printing a long alphanumeric field to the left
of a long zero suppressed numeric field will *ALWAYS* be harder for
the human eye to connect than the reverse. This is easily demonstrable,
yet some programmers rarely take this into consideration when designing
reports. Similar principles hold for source formatting, because compilers
generally don't care about coding style, but human brains and human eyes
do. Believe me, personal preference can, and often does, run contrary to
eye/brain characteristics. But what is hard wired into a human outweighs
what may be pleasing, when it comes to function. Like most things in life,
there are often competing, even conflicting, priorities and such things are
usually a compromise among multiple priorities. But in general, if you
use human eye/brain characteristics to your advantage, it will pay big
dividends in readability and understandability.
```

<a id="t-a9ed48663c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Use Among HP NonStop Systems Users — 1 msgs

### COBOL Use Among HP NonStop Systems Users

- **From:** jeff.dot.lanam@compaq.com.not.dot.net (Jeff Lanam)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T21:06:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fac07ee.79802770@news.compaq.com>`

```
ITUG, the International Tandem Users Group, recently ran a poll on
their website asking about present and future use of COBOL.  I thought
the results would be of general interest on CLC.

http://www.itug.org/pollarchive.cfm?QuestionID=96

The question was:

What is the primary use of COBOL in your shop?

16.0% 	  None of our applications are written in COBOL 
 34.3%     Our COBOL applications run our company, and new
applications are being written in COBOL 
 32.0%    Our COBOL applications substantially run our company, but
new applications are being written in other languages 
 14.4%    Our COBOL programs run only old applications, but we keep
them maintained 
 3.3%     We are converting our COBOL applications to use other
languages 

Total Votes: 181  
Poll Dates: 10/16/2003 - 10/30/2003
```

<a id="t-262981830d"></a>
## Thread: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2 — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T13:41:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com>`

```
I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
defined functions. In one of them, someone mentions being able to do
this usinf MF COBOL. Essentially I have two questions:

1 - Is this truly possible?

2 - Is it possible without using the OO features. I'm hoping that it
is.


I would like to incorporate the use of user-defined functions if
possible, as it is much nicer to write something like:

IF  CHECK-PART-NO(ws-Part-No)
    *> The part number is valid
ELSE
    *> The part number is NOT valid
END-IF

As opposed to the existing:

CALL "CHECK-PART-NO"
    USING BY VALUE ws-Part-No
END-CALL

IF  RETURN-CODE  =  TRUE
    *> The part number is valid
ELSE
    *> The part number is NOT valid
END-IF
```

#### ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T14:18:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<boh5nq$ddf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com>`

```
Can't speak to any specific product or vendor, but I can assert that the
ability to declare and access user-defined functions is definitely part of
the current (2002) standard for COBOL.  And I for one don't think of this
ability as an "OO feature".

Depending on how the function is declared in the program, you may or may not
be able to omit the keyword "FUNCTION" before CHECK-PART-NO in your example,
but otherwise, I'd say yeah, that's the general idea.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com...
> I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
> defined functions. In one of them, someone mentions being able to do
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF
```

#### ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T01:26:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FAC45DF.B17F102E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com>`

```


Chris wrote:

> I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
> defined functions. In one of them, someone mentions being able to do
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> is.

As an OO user I don't have a problem with this. Check out the N/E V 4.0
on-line manuals Language Reference Part 1 and from the index :-

Local-storage section (non-OO) and Function - prototype, state,
F-id-paragraph, F-identifier, F-prototype-name.

Can't see that's going to help you though - unless your compiler already
has these features. (I guess you already have 'Local-Storage section'
?)

...... Hold the Fort ! Can you believe it - I've just had two of three
spotlights go kaput - yeah I know the RAF trained me as a touch typist -
but I ain't so great with braille on a computer keyboard. So a quick
trip to the hardware store !.........

.... As I was about to write, before the above unwarranted
interruption......

If you are stumped on the above, there's a possible workaround,
providing your query wasn't just idle curiosity. If you have a whole
bunch of functions, you *could* use an OO class to hold your various
functions - rather like the old DOS TSRs, (Terminate and Stay Resident).
The first invoke of an OO class, (to the best of my knowledge), only
stores a reference to the class as a result of instantiation. Methods
only occupy memory as they get invoked - and M/F caches them to
'actively' have only the most current used - I guess with some sort of
algorithm..

And surprise, surprise, I didn't know until I tried it, but a Procedural
program or an OO class can do a Standard CALL by reference - where the
reference is the OO object reference.

I wont pursue the detail unless you tell me you are interested.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T01:58:11+00:00

```
Chris wrote:
> I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
> defined functions. In one of them, someone mentions being able to do
> this usinf MF COBOL.

Chris, I'm into this stream half-way through, so apologies in advance if I'm
off base...

...I'm just wondering if you're thinking of "Vocabularies"? This is a
feature of Micro Focus OO COBOL that enables you to define new COBOL verbs
and functions for objects you create.

Gary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T06:45:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FAC9093.90C227A5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com> <3FAC45DF.B17F102E@shaw.ca> <T4Yqb.141072$Tr4.369686@attbi_s03>`

```


Gary wrote:

> Chris wrote:
> > I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gary.

You are right Gary. He could use Vocabulary to create a User Defined Function.
Unfortunately Chris has said he doesn't want to particularly get into OO. He
really wants to do it in-line :-

compute interest = .06 * balance (thisBankAccount)

with no CALL or RETURN.

However when I read the N/E 4.0 on-line Manual for OO - it appears that at the
present time this is specifically an OO feature because you have to create an
OO  external class, containing the vocabulary syntax in a file with the suffix
"xxxxx.if".
I checked the Language Reference Manuals - but the only reference I found to
UDFs is in the OO book. If he has Vocabulary with his M/F OC 4.2  he probably
could create a UDF - but as it reads at the moment the UDF is wrapped in a
method - so you still have a CALL(Invoke) and then a RETURN.

For Chris - a repeat of something I posted recently from the OO Manual :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
User-defined Functions

You can also create new functions with user vocabularies. The new functions look
like COBOL intrinsic functions when used in a program.

For example, you could code:

compute interest = .06 * function balance (thisBankAccount)

You can omit the keyword function, so you could also write:

compute interest = .06 * balance (thisBankAccount)

The definition of function balance looks like this:

 method-id. "getBalance".
 linkage section.
 01 lsAmount pic S9(10)v99.

 procedure division returning lsAmount
    INVOKED AS FUNCTION == Balance ( <self> ) ==.
 end method "getbalance".

Using Vocabulary-defined Verbs and
Functions

To use vocabulary defined verbs or functions:

   1.Include the external class defining the new syntax in your program as a
copyfile,
     using a COPY statement. By convention these are stored in .if files.

     If you are using several objects with their own vocabulary definitions, you
must
     include the external class for each one. Put external classes at the top of
the
     source code, before the Identification Division.

   2.Use the vocabulary defined for the object in place of an INVOKE verb and
     method name when you want to send the object a message.

The Micro Focus class libraries supplied with your COBOL system include external

classes with vocabularies for the following types of manipulation:.

 chararray.if
                String manipulation verbs
 symbtab.if
                SymbolTable manipulation verbs
 Net Express:
 guibase.if
                Event mapping verbs
 Net Express:
 sdiframe.if
                GUI application framework verbs


One advantage that vocabularies give you is that data values are cast into the
format
expected by the target method. For example, you can code:

credit anAccount with 5

The interface definition at the start of the program enables the Compiler to
convert the
number 5 into the numeric format expected by the method. The equivalent using
standard OO COBOL requires two statements:

move 5 to anAmount
    invoke anAccount "credit" using anAmount

For an example that uses requirements-based vocabulary, see the chapter
Requirements-based Vocabulary Tutorial.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T18:45:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311071845.2e74b85f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote 

> I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
> defined functions.

There have been some 'contributors' who have made statements about
'user defined functions' without understanding what these actually
are, or would be when they are possible.  They (well one mainly) were
actually talking about called programs that could use the RETURNING
clause, but still could only be accessed with a CALL.
 
> IF  CHECK-PART-NO(ws-Part-No)

Yes, that would be a 'user defined function'.
```

#### ↳ Re: User-Defined Functions w/ MicroFocus OC v4.2

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T18:56:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FAD3BEC.51239962@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0311071341.377ee2f0@posting.google.com>`

```


Chris wrote:

> I've read a number of threads concerning the incoporation of user
> defined functions. In one of them, someone mentions being able to do
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> possible, as it is much nicer to write something like:

The simple question is "Is it worth the HASSLE ?",  just  to save a few
keystrokes - and to my mind finish up writing  'cryptic syntax' in your
actual source.

Here's my feeble attempt to try it. If Gary has used Vocabulary, perhaps
he can give you some pointers. Looks like the Vocabulary is a fairly
'old' feature because the program VOCABGEN.EXE puts out prompt dialogs
which look like they've been designed using Dialog System (bold black
ugly text). Initially I copied an existing xxx.if to give me jaygees.if.
Got syntax errors on that and you can see I've commented lines and put
in fresh ones. Then I ran VOCABGEN.EXE -  WITH the CORRECT path/fielname
which generated testudf.if - the TOP part which I've commented out. Then
I included in that the method text from jaygees.if. As you can see from
the source, from the IDE I can display testudf.if - but the compiler
says "copyfile testudf.if  not found" - go figure !

Enough is ENOUGH - I quit !

Apart from which if you don't have VOCABGEN.EXE with your compiler -
it's just an academic exercise.

*>---------------------------------------------------------
$set MF-OO
 copy "testudf.if".
*>------------------ testudf.if -----------------------
$set hidemessage(731)
 *>program-id. TestUDF is external.
 *>procedure division
*> vocabulary
 *>INVOKED as
 *>== TestUDF
 *>==.

 *>   **** The lines above are what VOCABGEN.EXE generated for the
 *>  Procedural program TestUDF.cbl - because it didn't find any methods

 *>  and below I've pasted the methods I had in jaygees.if

method-id. credit is method.
  linkage section.
  01 lnkAmount        pic s9(06)v99.

  procedure division using lnkAmount
 *> INVOKED AS ==Credit <self> [with] <lnkAmount> ==
  INVOKED AS ==Credit [with] <lnkAmount> ==
 *>      OR AS ==deposit <lnkAmount> [in] <self> ==
       OR AS ==deposit <lnkAmount> [in]  ==
  end method credit.
 end program TestUDF.
 *>-------------------------------------------------
 copy "jaygees.if".
 *>----------------- jaygees.if ----------------------

 method-id. credit is method.
  linkage section.
  01 lnkAmount        pic s9(06)v99.

  procedure division using lnkAmount
 *> INVOKED AS ==Credit <self> [with] <lnkAmount> ==
  INVOKED AS ==Credit [with] <lnkAmount> ==
 *>      OR AS ==deposit <lnkAmount> [in] <self> ==
       OR AS ==deposit <lnkAmount> [in]  ==
  .
  end method credit.
 *>---------------------------------------------------

 Program-id. TestUDF.

 Working-Storage section.

 *>------- part stuff

 01 ws-Part-No               pic 9(06) value 123.
 01 FirstNumber              pic 9(06) value 123.
 01 SecondNumber         pic 9(06) value 238.
 01 AnyNumber              pic 9(06).

 *>----- account stuff

 01 AnAccount                pic s9(06).99 value 1045.
 01 anAmount                 pic s9(06).99.

 Procedure Division.

 move -123.45 to LnkAmount
 credit anAccount with anAmount
 display anAccount
 move3 66.78 to LnkAmount
 deposit anAmount in anAccount
 display anAccount

 move FirstNumber to AnyNumber
 perform CHECK-IT
 move SecondNumber to AnyNumber
 perform CHECK-IT
 STOP RUN.

 CHECK-IT.

 if AnyNumber(ws-Part-No)
    display "Number is OK"

 else display "Number doesn't match"
 End-if.

 *>---------------------------------------------------
```

## Thread: Validating e-mail !! — 4 msgs

### Validating e-mail !!

- **From:** "Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-08T12:13:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pGVqb.5568$ws.537659@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
i am writing code in COBOL II to run on OS/390 to validate an e-mail
address. Any ideas how this can be done in the best way. I have an environ
of CICS/COBOL/DB2.

As of now I have an Inspect statement to count for @ character. If the count
is one then I am accepting the e-mail id.

Any body who can enlighten me more on this topic.

Jagster
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating e-mail !!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-07T18:48:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311071848.7aac23d8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pGVqb.5568$ws.537659@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
"Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote 

> As of now I have an Inspect statement to count for @ character. If the count
> is one then I am accepting the e-mail id.

valid EMail addresses may be for example: "My name <user@domain>".

Checking for one and only one @ sign and at least one '.' following
that (in the domain name) is about all you do unless you access to a
DNS server to do a domain name lookup.
 
> Any body who can enlighten me more on this topic.

Use Google and do a search for, say, "email address validation".
```

Though they get rarer each year, it is still a valid way to describe a 
path.

                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express 3.0 help

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
