```
In article <3FC7FA04.5070200@optonline.net>,
Warren Simmons  <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Perhaps 100 miles north of Indianapolis is South Bend, home of the 
>Studebaker Wagon Manufacturing Co. which turned into an Automobile 
>factory and the home of Notre Dame.

'Notre Dame' being pronounced by the locals not as 'NO-truh Dahm' but more 
along the lines of 'NO-der Dayme'... I spent some time in South Bend on 
contract and i found it to be a sad town.  In the Neighborhood Local I 
frequented (The Ewing Cafe... what a dive!) folks would suck down some 
suds and reminisce about the Goode Olde Dayse, when all the businesses 
were running... Studebaker, South Bend Tool & Die, the mighty names of 
yore... back in the mid 1950's and it has been downhill ever since.

(This was my standard for Sad City Stories until I spent time on a 
contract in Wilkes-Barre, PA... where folks would reminisce about the 
Goode Olde Dayse when all the coal-mines were running... back in the mid 
1920's.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States - Indiana

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-29T19:56:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HR6yb.11323$iU5.4027758@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bq961a$4f4$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com> <3FC7FA04.5070200@optonline.net> <bq961a$4f4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
My father had a nephew who was invlved with the assembly line at 
Studabaker. They were close enough in age (dad and his nephew)
that we ever so often would travel to S.B. to visit. His nephew
was involved on the assembly line maintenace in some capacity,
and at his late 50's began to take flying lessons. Dad, his nephew,
and my younger brother enjoyed talking about this hobby. As an
employee of S.B. he would get a new car delivered without any prep
and take it to a gas station, and put the car on the rack to tighen
all the bolts.

My cousin who lives in OR once had a company that built house trailers
and the pickup truck versions thereof. They (with permission of S.B.)
used the brand name "CONESTOGA" , the name used by the S.B. people for
their wagons.

Driving from Gary tgo S.Bend in those days seemed endless. Now, the
turnpike gets you there in a mile a minute (or less).

By the by, have you read the site called "freshmeat". I believe some of
the readers here would find some of the material available and listed
daily of much interest. In particular, the OO people. Some of the 
material is cross platform, but much is Linux related. Today I found
a text, etc. for JP..

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States was: OT: Hemlines

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-28T19:49:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vsfup5croun392@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> Anyway, I'd like to complete the collection and get just one salient fact
> about each of the 50 States. I wonder if posters here could help me out with
> a comment about their home State?

Well, I can give you my home state and my current state of residence.

Tennessee (home state) - There's something in each of the 4 largest 
cities.  Tennessee's state capital, Nashville, is -the- capital of 
country music, and Memphis is rich in the jazz tradition, along with 
being the birthplace of Elvis.  Chattanooga is famous for its role in 
railroads (and the Glenn Miller song "Chattanooga Choo-Choo").  And, in 
the northeast corner of the state, you have the Great Smoky Mountains, 
with Pigeon Forge (one of the prime shopping destinations for miles 
around) and Gatlinburg (a German-themed city up in the mountains). 
(And, of course, the best college football team, the Tennessee Vols, is 
from Knoxville.  ;> )

Alabama (where I am now) - "Alabama the Beautiful".  It's one of the few 
states where you can go to the mountains, lakes, rivers, and beaches 
while staying in the same state.  Along with that, it's got a long 
history in the civil rights movement - Montgomery is home to MLK Jr.'s 
church, and is the city where Rosa Parks refused to move on a bus after 
the seat in which she was sitting was moved from the colored section to 
the white; also, it was the destination on the Selma to Montgomery 
march.  Tuskegee was the home of George Washington Carver, who came up 
with over 300 uses for peanuts through the course of his career.

I could probably write 3x as much about both states...  :)  But maybe 
that'll give you some conversation topics...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT 50 States - Indiana

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-29T01:49:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FC7FA50.2070407@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3fc50d0e_9@news.athenanews.com> <20031126230150.08433.00000897@mb-m24.aol.com> <3fc5dd71_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Well, Peter, there are a few more things about Indiana that you might 
want to file away.  No order of significants is implied. But first
even the Indiana University has not been able to pin down exactly
what a Housier is, but the one given sounds good.

I'm from Gary, IN. Named after Judge Gary who was a part of the USSteel
organization. On the net you can view many views of the land it was 
build upon, and gather many other details from USS archives. The sand 
dunes around the tip of Lake Michigan had been used as a location for 
movies.

An electric train joins Gary to Chicago and South Bend. Major RR systems
run through Lake County even prior to the building of Gary.

Gary, in addition to the steel mill facilities has or had a factory that 
produced windshield wiper blades!  It was also the home of The Gary 
Screw and Bolt, or known locally by some a the "rape and run".

Except for natives, everyone who lived there moved there from somewhere 
else often using USS built homes and RR Sleeper Cars to house
people as they moved there. American Bridge also had a plant there, and
so did National Tube, both part of USS. I worked at the steel mill in
the foundry on the open hearth, and in the maintenance shops as a crane 
operator. After IU, I worked at the Gary Works of the National Tube 
Division as a college recruit. From there I was transfered to Pittsburgh 
for the Univac I project of the Division which later became a corporate 
center.

Gary School district was based upon the Wm. Wirt system and was known as 
the work, study, play system.

My folks came to Gary from the other end of the state (Orange and Dubois
Counties). In the western migration many families lived in these 
counties before some moved on. Orange County has a town called French 
Lick. There is a great old hotel there and artesian well that spews 
sulfur water marketed as "Pluto Water".

Near the Ohio River is Santa Clause, IN. They do a big business in 
stamping mail this time of year. Much lumbering, and wooden products are 
made including  Pianos and Furniture Factories. At one time French Lick 
area was the winter home of the circus that became Barnum and Baily.

Just 20 or so miles north of French Lick is Bedford where Indiana 
Limestone is mined. Twenty miles north of Bedford is Bloomington, IN
home of Indiana University where this GI got some more learning and
Hoggy Carmcal wrote Stardust.

David Letterman is from Indianapolis which is the home of Eli Lilly and
other notable companies.

Perhaps 100 miles north of Indianapolis is South Bend, home of the 
Studebaker Wagon Manufacturing Co. which turned into an Automobile 
factory and the home of Notre Dame.

Over on the Mississippi boundary an early French Trading Post existed.
Today it is called Vincennes?

Oh, and don't forget the Wabash River, subject of song and flood.
This excludes many other sites and attractions.

Hope this is entertaining off subject material from one very early
COBOL er!

Warren Simmons
```

```
On Sat, 29 Nov 2003 01:49:59 GMT, Warren Simmons
<wsimmons5@optonline.net> enlightened us:

>Well, Peter, there are a few more things about Indiana that you might 
>want to file away.  No order of significants is implied. But first

know if there is a connection between Screw and Bolt and the Jackson
family or not.


>Except for natives, everyone who lived there moved there from somewhere 
>else often using USS built homes and RR Sleeper Cars to house
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Aaah, IU, the poor step-child of the big 3 colleges in Indiana.  As
this Purdue alum knows, IU pales in comparison to Purdue which is
among the top 10 engineering schools in the world and has graduated
more astronauts than any other U.S. school.  

>David Letterman is from Indianapolis which is the home of Eli Lilly and
>other notable companies.
>

Letterman is from Indianapolis but graduated from Ball State
University in Muncie, IN.  

>Perhaps 100 miles north of Indianapolis is South Bend, home of the 
>Studebaker Wagon Manufacturing Co. which turned into an Automobile 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Vincennes is on the Illinois border.  No Mississippi river or state
anywhere near it.

>Oh, and don't forget the Wabash River, subject of song and flood.
>This excludes many other sites and attractions.
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
>> 

Regards,

Harry Edsel Smith of Albany, New York:
Born 1903-Died 1942
Looked up the elevator shaft to see if the car was on the way down.
It was.

Steve
```

```
> >Date: 11/26/03 3:28 PM Eastern Standard Time
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> mother whosyer father'.

Not quite in the same vein as your hoosier definition, but close. When we first
came here in '75 there was a young couple living some 6 houses away, immigrants
from Eastern Europe. Forgotten their family name but her first name was Anya.

In conversation with my wife, she told her how polite Canadians were, even to the
extent of using your first name. She was shopping in a supermarket, was at the
check-out and tendered notes and coins. The pleasant girl,  shuffling through the
change in her register, and to complete the transaction, said :-

"Have yer got a penny an' yer ?"

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Thanks Bob,

I read it.

Interesting and informative, but lacking the insight one often gets from
personal observation.

I'd still like to have a personal comment from you regarding your home
State. Is everybody proud to be Hoosier (like it says in the article)?

Travelling all over the World I frequently receive the identifier "Kiwi". (A
friend of mine in L.A. has threatened to make a movie called "Kiwi Pete"
based on my early life... (before I became a respectable computer
consultant..<G>)

Although I am proud of my heritage, my world view prevents me from indulging
in fervent patriotism (except when we play Australia or the French <G>...at
anything...). (My theory is we need to be Human Beings first, before we
start putting on the labels...).

While I have no problem being identified with a fragile, flightless, almost
blind little bird that snuffles around for worms and insects, at least it is
identifiable. I'm not sure how I'd feel if nobody could explain what it
meant or how it originated...<G>

And nobody can see a Kiwi (bird) walk or run without smiling or laughing
(they have a delightful bouncing roll that is very amusing).

Pete.
```

```
I take it you never saw the movie, "Hoosiers" staring Gene Hackman
either.  Hoosier is a term given to those who are born in the state of
Indiana.  It's origins are not completely known as there are 2 or 3
theories floating about.  But it's been in use since before Indiana
even became a state.


>No, wait... sorry, that's 'hoser'.  Do 'those of us who weren't raised in
```

```
In message 
<bJwwb.107774$Ec1.4818219@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>, Hugh 
Candlin <no@spam.com> writes
>
>Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No. I did know one place which had tried to use Indian call centres and 
gave the staff elocution lessons (some go so far as to give them 
Scouser, Geordie or Brummie accents) and regular soap story lines so 
that they could talk knowledgeably about last nights Eastenders. It was 
deemed a dismal failure so they stopped it. However, the IT boss had 
just returned from a mission to India, so they haven't given up yet.

The one thing that tickles me about the call centres is that the staff 
give themselves English names, ostensibly so that you have a familiar 
name to latch on to. I bet it is really a deliberate attempt to lull you 
into thinking that they are sited in the UK and not Asia.

Has anyone started a 'boycott foreign call centre company' list?
```

```
I don't have any bias against anyone of any religion, race, creed
or national origin.  It wasn't my intent to imply that.

I do have a bias against short-sighted CEO's who cannot see
the long-term impact that they are having on the intellectual capital
of their company with this flood of domestic layoffs and offshore
job transfers, and on the domestic economy, short and long term.

I realize that I am saying that I am correct and that all of these
captains of industry are wrong, and that it isn't in my best interests
to do so.  So be it.  I have to be true to myself.
>
> The one thing that tickles me about the call centres is that the staff
> give themselves English names, ostensibly so that you have a familiar
> name to latch on to. I bet it is really a deliberate attempt to lull you
> into thinking that they are sited in the UK and not Asia.

I worked in one company with a very-highly rated IT staff.
We had people from all over the world.  It was a great team.
We all got along really well.  We did have one Indian chap,
called himself Sam.  No big deal.

> Has anyone started a 'boycott foreign call centre company' list?

Only unofficially.  The millions of unemployed who find it impossible
to take "advantage" of the entreaties of all of these same companies
to "come buy our products".  With what?  Their severance pay?

What time does the depression start?
```

```
unable to purchase the latest styles.

in fashon without capital expenditure. The mfgrs can't have that, so
the hemlines go down forcing the ladies into a "must buy" situation.
```

```
goes up must come down".

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-30b6d95607"></a>
## Thread: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing — 7 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [2003-10](2003-10.md#t-30b6d95607)._

#### ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-23T13:12:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca>`

```

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca...
| As a z/OS (IBM mainframe) COBOL programmer, I am trying to get a handle
| on the uses for more than 2 gigabytes of addressability for an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
|

TABLES.

Lately, I've been doing some new development, (extensions for an existing
system).
The main program loads data into tables, and processes them.
The program is a "pig" (it isn't optimized, uses exchange sorts.)
The process runs for under a minute wall time.

The current, production, system relies heavily on VSAM random access, and
has very heavy I/O traffic, some EXCP counts go to 1,000K.
The Wall Clock Time is 1.5-2 hours, the CPU utilization is low compared to
the wall.
This system is mission critical ,and the JOB completion time is monitored by
upper management.

I'm looking at replacing some of the random access processes with sequential
processing, and using internal tables.
My GUESS is that I can get the system to run in well under 30 minutes.

The amount of spaces you can use in a single application program is limited
by the compiler.  But, you can write a modular system.

The main advantage to such a large address space is storing data in main
storage, rather than on DASD.
If you can store all operational data in main storage, you eliminate I/O
waits.

Think about how much faster your applications would be if you eliminate I/O
waits.
```

```
Although I haven't done any mainframe work in about two years, I seem to
recall one of the (then) 'fairly new things'  I had wanted to check out was
the virtual I-O available thru JCL.

Might using VIO be an alternative to supporting >2 Gb addressability? Or an
option to improve performance when I-O is the bottleneck?

(I'm more asking than suggesting).


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <usrr@mac.kat>
- **Date:** 2003-11-23T23:41:47+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<231120032341470909%usrr@mac.kat>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca> <Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <JH2wb.5000$aw2.1494709@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Actually, Data in Virtual is a better approach.
This lets you map the full address range of n TeraBytes into a given
virtual storage range.

The benefit is that the system does not have to provide page space for
your address space as you can use Linear datasets to do it for you.

The additional benefit is that page swaps to your private linear
dataset are done by the system using system optimised IO.

DO NOT TRY using Main Storage as a replacement for IO until you have
talked it over with your systems programmers. You might find that the
system workload manager thinks you are an abnormal user of system
resources and blithly swaps you out for the duration.

In article <JH2wb.5000$aw2.1494709@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>, Michael
Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
> news:Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
| recall one of the (then) 'fairly new things'  I had wanted to check out
was
| the virtual I-O available thru JCL.

I haven't seen VIO used in this shop, (and it's been around for quite some
time, I wouldn't call it new).
Neither have I seen Hyperspace used.
I don't even know if Hyperspace is still available.

|
| Might using VIO be an alternative to supporting >2 Gb addressability? Or
an
| option to improve performance when I-O is the bottleneck?

VIO wouldn't really fit in to what I'm doing, unless I want to Open and
Close the files a couple of thousand times.

|
| (I'm more asking than suggesting).

There are alternatives that will speed up I/O, but they usually involve
PAPERWORK up the ying-yang.

As far as going over the 2 gig limit, I'm not even close yet, but in the
future it may be a method we all use. It depends on the Compiler Limits.
I can remember when we conserved storage, especially in CICS.
Now I don't think twice about allocating an internal table that is 8 or even
16 meg.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-11-24T14:21:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fc15d54_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca> <Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Dennis,

before you get too excited (and without wishing to rain on your parade)
there are some serious factors you need to consider.

See below...

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> This system is mission critical ,and the JOB completion time is monitored
by
> upper management.
>
> I'm looking at replacing some of the random access processes with
sequential
> processing, and using internal tables.
> My GUESS is that I can get the system to run in well under 30 minutes.
>
> The amount of spaces you can use in a single application program is
limited
> by the compiler.  But, you can write a modular system.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You also deny resources to other applications that may be competing for
it...

> Think about how much faster your applications would be if you eliminate
I/O
> waits.
>
Think about how much data you will lose if the system crashes or your job
abends <G>...

If this is a purely Batch process and you are experiencing I/O problems,
wouldn't the first line of attack be to try and improve the VSAM
performance?  Talk to your DBAs and Systems people before you re-design this
application.

I don't know whether your system is supporting on-line access concurrently
with the batch (most mainframes are) but it is worth considering the
fundamentals:

1. On-line performance depends on 3 factors: load time, queue wait time, and
capture time. Without a great discourse on this, what it means is that
"SMALL is beautiful" and memory MUST be available for on-line modules to be
loaded. (The more memory you have, the less they need to be swapped.)

2. Batch processes should utilise as much memory as possible (which is
exactly what you are proposing) PROVIDED they do not do so at the expense of
the on-line system. So, if this is an overnight Batch run, no problem. ("BIG
is beautiful", for Batch)

But hang on a minute... Even if your process is overnight Batch and there is
no danger of it degrading other key processes, won't it require significant
changes to convert what are currently I/O areas into (VERY LARGE) tables?
Then you must provide a mechanism for loading these tables (and storing
them), change all existing access to be subscripted/indexed, and regression
test.

All of this can be done of course, but what I am saying is don't undertake
to do it overnight...<G>

If the problem is really that the VSAM random access is slowing everything
down, you should certainly discuss it with your DBA (he/she might be glad of
something interesting to do for a change...<G>). If this is the case, and
everyone agrees that the I/O has been optimised as far as it can, then you
could try progressively replacing the most frequent random accesses. (In
other words, I would approach this on a phased basis.)

Before you decide to go and use the entire address space, you MUST discuss
it with your system people. There are likely to be repercussions for JCL
(Job Classes), system paging, load balancing, scheduling, etc. etc...

Yes, your job will be allocated a Virtual space as big as you desire, but
that Virtual space still has to be mapped to real memory. It would be a pity
if you simply exchange an I/O problem for a thrashing one...

Talk to DBA, talk to systems, and Good Luck!

Pete.



>
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T13:54:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bmnxb.119057$Ec1.5109884@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca> <Tm2wb.315845$0v4.18414718@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3fc15d54_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Pete,

Not to worry.

The application volume is very low, in mainframe standards.
The program doesn't consume enormous amounts of storage, just more than it
did before.

The main problem with the current system is that the people who wrote it
were VSAM Random Access freaks. The update process is incremental, and they
didn't incorporate adequate backups because it would further degrade the
system.

What I am proposing is a variation of the Extract, Sort, Print model.
Extract the data, pass it through the process, and Update the VSAM files at
the end.
(The VSAM files are not available on-line during the process).
It will run faster, and be easier to test.
As it is now, you need about 8 VSAM files, and they have to be "in-sync", to
test any part of the system flow. Unit testing is a horror.


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3fc15d54_6@news.athenanews.com...
| Dennis,
|
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
| > | As a z/OS (IBM mainframe) COBOL programmer, I am trying to get a
handle
| > | on the uses for more than 2 gigabytes of addressability for an
| > | application.  Since both the i-series/AS400 and many Unix systems have
| > | 64 bit addressing, I was wondering if there were any applications on
| > | these systems that take advantage of the large address range that have
| > | COBOL main programs and/or subroutines.  If so, I would appreciate it
if
| > | the driver for the large addressing range could be discussed and how
use
| > | of such a range made the application development easier.
| > |
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| > Lately, I've been doing some new development, (extensions for an
existing
| > system).
| > The main program loads data into tables, and processes them.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > The current, production, system relies heavily on VSAM random access,
and
| > has very heavy I/O traffic, some EXCP counts go to 1,000K.
| > The Wall Clock Time is 1.5-2 hours, the CPU utilization is low compared
to
| > the wall.
| > This system is mission critical ,and the JOB completion time is
monitored
| by
| > upper management.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
| it...

That's true, but the run time is about a minute, and the Operating System
grabs control so I don't hog the machine.
At the time of day that this would run in production, the CPU is at about
60-80% utilization.

|
| > Think about how much faster your applications would be if you eliminate
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| abends <G>...

It runs in under a minute<G>.
Just re-run.
With the current update archecture, they have to restore the VSAM files, and
then rerun.
They also have to back-up the files in case the next update prograsm fails.
|
| If this is a purely Batch process and you are experiencing I/O problems,
| wouldn't the first line of attack be to try and improve the VSAM
| performance?  Talk to your DBAs and Systems people before you re-design
this
| application.

The system was looked at by our "in-house VSAM experts", they found nothing.

What I found out is this:
1. The VSAM files are ALL defined with one or more Alternate Indexes.
2. The Alternate Indexes are all defined as NONUNIQUEKEY, (duplicate
Alternate Key values are acceptable) even though there is no valid reason
for allowing Duplicates.
3. The Update process is totally random.

4. The I/O counts are reduced significantly by:
a. Defining the Alternate Indexes with UNIQUEKEY (no duplicate alternate
Index Keys)
b. Updating in Primary Key, Secondary Key sequence in Sequential, rather
than Random mode.

There are other alternatives that I'm aware of, but that involves getting
support from the Systems area. It would only take me about 6 months to fill
out the necessary paperwork <G>.
.
|
| I don't know whether your system is supporting on-line access concurrently
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| 1. On-line performance depends on 3 factors: load time, queue wait time,
and
| capture time. Without a great discourse on this, what it means is that
| "SMALL is beautiful" and memory MUST be available for on-line modules to
be
| loaded. (The more memory you have, the less they need to be swapped.)
|
| 2. Batch processes should utilise as much memory as possible (which is
| exactly what you are proposing) PROVIDED they do not do so at the expense
of
| the on-line system. So, if this is an overnight Batch run, no problem.
("BIG
| is beautiful", for Batch)
|
| But hang on a minute... Even if your process is overnight Batch and there
is
| no danger of it degrading other key processes, won't it require
significant
| changes to convert what are currently I/O areas into (VERY LARGE) tables?

EVERYTHING is tables.
Sequential files are tables, you just get one element at a time.

The Operating System will probably save these batch files in memory until
they aren't needed in a subsequent step. The programmer won't even be aware
of it.

| Then you must provide a mechanism for loading these tables (and storing
| them), change all existing access to be subscripted/indexed, and
regression
| test.

Not a big problem.
I have application programs that tell me what has changed, and I have
regression testing already set-up.

|
| All of this can be done of course, but what I am saying is don't undertake
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| down, you should certainly discuss it with your DBA (he/she might be glad
of
| something interesting to do for a change...<G>). If this is the case, and
| everyone agrees that the I/O has been optimised as far as it can, then you
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
| that Virtual space still has to be mapped to real memory. It would be a
pity
| if you simply exchange an I/O problem for a thrashing one...
|
| Talk to DBA, talk to systems, and Good Luck!
|

I may need the luck!
```

```
Am 23.11.03, 13:12:19, schrieb "Harley" 
<dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> zum Thema Re: Applications using 
over 2 gigabytes of addressing:

Hello

> TABLES.

> Lately, I've been doing some new development, (extensions for an 
existing
> system).
> The main program loads data into tables, and processes them.
> The program is a "pig" (it isn't optimized, uses exchange sorts.)
> The process runs for under a minute wall time.

> The current, production, system relies heavily on VSAM random access, 
and
> has very heavy I/O traffic, some EXCP counts go to 1,000K.
> The Wall Clock Time is 1.5-2 hours, the CPU utilization is low 
compared to
> the wall.
> This system is mission critical ,and the JOB completion time is 
monitored by
> upper management.

> I'm looking at replacing some of the random access processes with 
sequential
> processing, and using internal tables.
> My GUESS is that I can get the system to run in well under 30 minutes.

> The amount of spaces you can use in a single application program is 
limited
> by the compiler.  But, you can write a modular system.

> The main advantage to such a large address space is storing data in 
main
> storage, rather than on DASD.
> If you can store all operational data in main storage, you eliminate 
I/O
> waits.

> Think about how much faster your applications would be if you 
eliminate I/O
> waits.



Some years ago - i think 1986 - !candle has a newsletter for optimal 
I/O-performance of VSAM-files.
First you have a look to IMBED and REPLICATE
	best performance is NOIMBED and NOREPLICATE
second have a look about VSAM-buffer for indexprocessing
	listcat shows the level of indices, you must at least one buffer more 
than indexlevels are listes
third you mus have a good assignment of freespace CI is more efficient 
than CA
and whats about I/O-cache (must / may / never) i dont know if this is 
an option on modern DASD??

and on MVS/ESA there is a subsystem named BLSR (Batch Local Shared 
Resources):

   MVS/ESA

   Application Development Guide:
   Batch Local Shared Resources Subsystem

   MVS/ESA System Product:
   JES2 Version 4
   JES3 Version 4

   Document Number GC28-1672-0

   Program Number
   5695-047
   5695-048

   File Number S370-34

this is an old title there can be newer one but the principles are 
unchanged!

if you are interested of a german translation for optimal 
VSAM-processing i can send it

another - very interesting idea - for VSAM-KSDS you can use IMS 
database SHISAM for updateprocessing. At abendtime you can do a 
batch-backout to rollback your changes :-)

have a nice day
Andreas Lerch
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
ciao Massimo, benvenuto, spostati su it.comp.lang e segui
il Treadh
```

<a id="t-2d5f375af8"></a>
## Thread: Whe I send to a printer a file line sequetial lose all jumps — 2 msgs

### Whe I send to a printer a file line sequetial lose all jumps

- **From:** "Massimo Morgia" <areasoftware@inwind.it>
- **Date:** 2003-11-24T19:47:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nftwb.143504$e6.5207493@twister2.libero.it>`

```
I have produced a file that contains a press in this format:

     select Stampa-i assign To "c:\tmp\gapspl.tmp"
            organization is line sequential
            access mode is sequential.

 data division.
 file section.
 fd  Stampa-i.
 01  Rk-STAMPA Pic X(230).

The press is produced well, and if I visualize it is everything OK as format
and jumps, but to the moment that I reread the file of press and I send it
to a printer
I lose all the jumps (Page and Line)

do you have a suggestion to give me?

Thanks, Massimo

ps: I Use the FJ powercobol v 5
```

#### ↳ Re: Whe I send to a printer a file line sequetial lose all jumps

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-24T15:55:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311241555.9f459b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<nftwb.143504$e6.5207493@twister2.libero.it>`

```
"Massimo Morgia" <areasoftware@inwind.it> wrote 

> 
>      select Stampa-i assign To "c:\tmp\gapspl.tmp"
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> ps: I Use the FJ powercobol v 5

When you read in a line sequential file the control characters will be
stripped before the record is put into the record buffer.

This is covered in the manual.

The 'best' solution is to not read the file when you want to send it
to the printer but to use a utility like COPY to do it for you.
```

---

<a id="t-c62f8f01c6"></a>
## Thread: Believe me, you WANT to read this! — 45 this month / 50 total

### Believe me, you WANT to read this!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-25T04:56:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com>`

```
An associate just sent me the email below.
----------------------------------------------------------------
    I was looking at some of the case studies on Microsoft's web site and ran across the following one.  Note that they abandoned
Java and went back to salvaging their COBOL.  I hope the industry soon realizes that this is the most economical upgrade solution
before everyone jumps off the cliff blindly following the leader.  I found this under the Public and Nonprofit Sector link on the
following web page: http://msdn.microsoft.com/vstudio/productinfo/casestudies/industry/

Superior Court Stanislaus County System
Superior Court of California, Stanislaus County System Court Saves over $700,000 Annually by Migrating Its Legacy System to a Modern
Computing Environment
The Superior Court Stanislaus County System needed to update its legacy Case Management system. Nine months into efforts to
implement a Java-based solution, they halted the project because of scalability problems and high development costs. The Court
adopted a strategy that included use of Microsoft� Visual Studio� .NET and the Microsoft .NET Framework to migrate its legacy system
off the mainframe and into a modern environment. The new system runs on Microsoft Windows� 2000 Advanced Server operating system and
offers a lower cost of ownership, greater extensibility and improved performance. Working with Fujitsu Software Corporation's
NetCOBOL for .NET (language add-in software for the .NET Framework), the new application required just two and a half months to
build, test and implement.
----------------------------------------------------------------
Can we spell "C-O-B-O-L i-s b-e-t-t-e-r, d-u-m-m-i-e-s?" :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-25T10:17:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

>     I was looking at some of the case studies on Microsoft's web site and ran across the following one.  Note that they abandoned
> Java 

No. Microsoft did not 'abandon' Java.  They decided they could not
control it, as they have with other languages they supported, and so
they want to destroy it and replace it with their own version of it
C#.

> and went back to salvaging their COBOL.  

No, they haven't salvaged 'their Cobol'.  While MS did write a Cobol
back in the CP/M days which they converted to MS-DOS, they used a
version of MF Cobol while it suited them and now promote Fujitsu Cobol
while it suits them.

> I hope the industry soon realizes that this is the most economical upgrade solution
> before everyone jumps off the cliff blindly following the leader.  

All businesses will have highly selective 'news' items that are
slanted as much as possible to one point of view - ie buy _my_
solution.  Do you really believe marketing hype ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-25T19:12:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> solution.  Do you really believe marketing hype ?

Judson,

Richard is right on the button. Some see his comments as somewhat cynical, but they ARE factual.

I don't know whether you ever used the Microsoft COBOL - wasn't bad. Although before Internet days not too easy to get Tech support,
although it existed. At one stage Micro Focus was cozily in bed with MS - the last two versions of MS COBOL were the re-badged MF COBOL
(DOS text) that you are familiar with. The only difference being that the compiler was wrapped in their own Programmer's Workbench for
compiling, linking and help files, coupled with the Editor which I still use. (Richard thinks I'm a masochist for still using that
<G>).

There was one significant difference as I switched - the MS manuals were simply worded; switch to M/F and the manuals were a knock-off
of the legalese in the Standard.

I don't think the Merant fiasco helped any. To get the .Net to work, MS had to show that they supported ALL languages.  Pure guess, but
perhaps perceiving M/F as a 'weak partner' they looked towards Fujitsu - big conglomerate, and 'enticed' them to get into .Net.
Meanwhile of course, to compete, M/F as an add-on to Net Express V 4.0 (if you upgrade - $$$$$), offers features allowing you to use
.Net.

Continuously upgrading for small/single developers is a no go unless you are fortunate enough "to be in the right place and the right
time" - i.e., a healthy customer base you can solicit. Meanwhile medium sized software houses can see the advantage of switching to
.Net, (they take a deep breath and pay up), and in the process, in one case using N/E, somebody has already mentioned using C#
features, rather than COBOL. M/S will of course be delighted - they've hung up the sticky fly paper, and are just waiting for the flies
to get attached.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-25T15:52:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com> <3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca>`

```
Sorry, but you fellows are misreading the post. It DOES NOT say that
Microsoft is 'abandoning Java' or 'salvaging their COBOL'. It says
that about the Superior Court of California, the 'they' in the post. My
friend simply found the info on the Microsoft web site. Read it again
*carefully* this time, and you should see this clearly. Richard likes
to occasionally jump down people's throats without bothering to check
what was really said, then refuses to admit the mistake. Makes him
look stupid, like now (surprise me Richard, by admitting it this time :).
I'm surprised you did not catch that, Jimmy.

The post was not about Microsoft, or advertising hype about Fujitsu
COBOL or .NET. The point is that there are people out there who, after
wasting tons of money on VB, Java, et al, finally realize that COBOL
is highly efficient and productive, and return to it. I have newspaper
articles on my desk that report other similar incidents. I should think
you COBOL people would be pleased about that. I am. I'm tired of
watching clients blow millions that might as well have been piled up
and set on fire. Pressure is building right now in several departments
of one of my major clients to confront upper management with the
fact that they have spent over 4 years and over $20 million and have
yet to replace ONE SINGLE mainframe application, and they're not
even remotely close to doing so. More than that, the organization has
yet to receive a single benefit from all that money. If that's not failure,
I wouldn't know how to define failure.

VB, Java, etc. have their place, but building large business applications
is not it. Parts of one sure, but not the whole thing. Too unstable, too
fragile, too proprietary (VB), too expensive, too time consuming, and
too much hassle. Why do you think there is a 45%+ failure rate for new
systems these days? Barely more than half of the new systems attempted
actually come up! That factual figure is simply staggering. Who of you
old timers ever saw such a thing with COBOL based systems? I'll bet
you there won't be a single current non-trivial VB or Java application
that will still be in active use in 20 years. Even 10 years is a very big
stretch. I have a client/server COBOL inventory system that's been
running 16 years, and it has a far better chance of still being in use 20
years from now than the VB/Java stuff being written today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-11-26T14:16:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fc3ff33_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com> <3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca> <20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com>`

```
Sorry this is fairly long.

Judson raised some interesting points that required some detailed argument.

See Below...


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com...
> Sorry, but you fellows are misreading the post. It DOES NOT say that
> Microsoft is 'abandoning Java' or 'salvaging their COBOL'. It says
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> look stupid, like now (surprise me Richard, by admitting it this time :).

I'm not sure that is fair Judson. Richard is a valued contributor here. My
experience has been that he usually gets it right. While he has a pronounced
anti-MS bias, that is his prerogative, and what he said here seems fair to
me.

> I'm surprised you did not catch that, Jimmy.
>

No comment.

> The post was not about Microsoft, or advertising hype about Fujitsu
> COBOL or .NET. The point is that there are people out there who, after
> wasting tons of money on VB, Java, et al, finally realize that COBOL
> is highly efficient and productive, and return to it.

This is a very interesting point.

Certainly there are companies that ARE wasting tons of money on IT. But that
has ALWAYS been the case.

There are indeed some installations that are reviewing the use of COBOL. (I
know of 2...). "Review" does not mean "Change" in these cases...

The problem is not with the COBOL language per se, it is with the whole
"COBOL think" approach to system development.

(I'm excluding mainframe installations from the following discussion. The
mainframe installations, for the most part, are locked in to what they have.
Only as mainframe vendors see their customer base being eroded by faster,
cheaper, client/server networks is anything likly to change. IBM are betting
on Linux and Java, and they are a major influence on mainframe
installations).

The traditional Waterfall approach to system development, as practised on
COBOL installations for many years, is simply not adequate for modern
client/server, network, and distributed computing requirements.

There was a time when it was enough to open files, read files, process
files, close files, stop run.

Those days (except for the continuing requirement to handle Batch
processing, primarily for volume reporting) are gone.

(It should be noted in passing that the more efficient the updating and
maintenance of the corporate data assets in real time, the less requirement
there is for Batch processing. Finally, Batch processing becomes "Running
high volume reports" and this can be done by utilities without the need for
one-off COBOL programs. For example, the only reason you would need to sort
data, in an ideal world, would be to ensure the reporting sequence.  "Three
way split" type batch updates are obviated by efficient online processing.
The fact that we have seen so much discussion about these ancient (1956!)
techniques here, indicates that there is still some distance to go in some
quarters before the goal of efficient online maintenance is achieved...<G>).

So we should be looking at what will deliver the best online performance.

COBOL is, for the most part, not good at this.

OO COBOL is ideal, because it allows objects to be encapsulated and
distributed around the Network, just like any other OO Language.
Encapsulated objects (components) can be written in OO COBOL (which is well
suited to the task of commercial data processing) and used just like Java
Beans or VB components and deployed instantly on the Web or the desktop.

But, sadly, the COBOL establishment,  (for the most part) not understanding
the needs of the Network or the Internet, has clearly rejected OO COBOL.
(This is changing, as Fortress COBOL is being dragged kicking and screaming
into the 21st Century, simply by the demands of the Market Place, and there
are encouraging signs of people starting to cotton on to why it is important
to change working practices and adapt to the new environment, but in my
opinion, it is too late for COBOL.)

In the meantime, the new breed of programmers have been raised on C++ and
Java. It is easy to poke fun at their lack of knowledge regarding
traditional data processing, that is the bread and butter of every COBOL
programmer, but these guys are smart and hungry AND they understand the
Network. They grew up with it. Object Orientation is deeply ingrained in
them. The languages and scripts they are using are designed around that
concept. Their difficulty is in understanding procedural COBOL.

> I have newspaper
> articles on my desk that report other similar incidents. I should think
> you COBOL people would be pleased about that. I am.

Like many people over the last 15 years, Judson, I have expanded my skill
set. The term "COBOL people" does not apply to me (and many others like me),
any more than "Java people" or "C# people" would apply. I consider myself an
IT professional. I have a full armoury of tools and techniques and I use
whichever is most appropriate to the required task. If that means Java
instead of COBOL, or JavaScript instead of VBScript,  or COBOL instead of C,
then I have no problem with that.

(What I DON'T do is look at every problem from the point of view of a COBOL
solution.)

Said it before (many times <G>), say it again: "The BEST tools for the job."

Sometimes COBOL IS the best tool; sometimes it ISN'T. If I didn't expand my
horizons I wouldn't know, and I'd still be sitting wondering if COBOL was
going to "catch on" again and hoping I could find a job with it.

(I have not made a living from COBOL for many years now, but, ironically, I
am working on a distributed Internet project at the moment, where I am
writing much of the code in COBOL. This is because the major processing
involves DB Access and "data processing" which COBOL excels at. Needless to
say, it is all OO COBOL and utilises DCOM+ and MTS to distribute Client and
Server Components around the Network (in this case, the Internet, via a
portal I wrote in ASP...)).

I LOVE COBOL, have used it most of my working life in IT (38 years coming
up), but I see no point in being a "one language wonder" (whether that
Language is COBOL, C, Java, C#, Perl, Python, or whatever...). The
phrase:"Everything I want to do, I can do in COBOL..." irritates me
intensely. It simply indicates low aspirations and short sightedness.

It staggers me that people who are (for the most part) otherwise very bright
(I'm talking about COBOL Programmers...), just are not prepared to invest a
few extra hours of their own time in furthering their education.

(I put this down to emotional attachment to COBOL; learning something else
is like betraying your lover...<G>)

It isn't difficult. There is an abundance of excellent books available, most
people have access to a computer (and the Network), but it is easier to
insist that COBOL should be used (whether it is appropriate or not), because
that's what we know...)

> I'm tired of
> watching clients blow millions that might as well have been piled up
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> yet to receive a single benefit from all that money. If that's not
failure,
> I wouldn't know how to define failure.
>

I agree that certainly sounds like failure. But you would need to analyse
WHY it has failed. It isn't enough to say: "Well they used these new fangled
languages and they don't understand corporate data processing; if they'd
done it in COBOL they'd be home free..."

The misuse of a $20 million budget is not just down to programmers (in
whatever language). It sounds very much like some severe mis-management,
lack of experience, design, planning, and control. For that kind of money,
they could have bought some proper MANAGEMENT (not just technical)
experience.

> VB, Java, etc. have their place, but building large business applications
> is not it.

Right. That's why they go to Siebel, SAP, and similar corporate solutions.
They already had their fingers burned with COBOL.

> Parts of one sure, but not the whole thing. Too unstable, too
> fragile,

Hmmm...the largest Money processing Internet system in Australasia (backed
by half a dozen major banks and owned by a large American "household name"
corporation), uses Java for its payment Client and Server processing across
secure socket layers and including credit card validation and authorization,
and transferring to merchant accounts. It has been running  in its present
form for 3 years and has 99.997% up time. There has never been a discrepancy
with it. Unstable? Fragile? (I know they use Java because I am currently
interfacing my COBOL to it <G>)


> too proprietary (VB),

It is intended as a Quick Build GUI front ending tool. As such, VB is very
useful. COM components developed in VB are even more useful and I use a
number of them every day. Third party components are a very attractive
option and many of them are written in VB.

> too expensive,

$US100 for something that would've taken at least 6 weeks for ME (not a
junior programmer) to write in COBOL? I don't think so...

> too time consuming,

I have managed several non-COBOL projects. They take no longer than anything
else. In some cases they are much faster than traditional COBOL because the
tools are much more powerful. SQL Server has a visually interfaced tool that
lets you develop processes by dragging and dropping icons. It takes minutes
to do what would take days in COBOL. The first time I had this demoed to me
I was stunned. Couldn't believe it would work <G>. It did.

>and
> too much hassle.

Developing computer systems is ALWAYS a "hassle" for somebody. COBOL is no
exception. (Tried getting DBA approval to add a couple of tables to the
Corporate DB <G>?)


> Why do you think there is a 45%+ failure rate for new
> systems these days? Barely more than half of the new systems attempted
> actually come up! That factual figure is simply staggering.

That "factual figure" needs to have a source, please. Can the same source
show that this is different to what has always been the case? (IT in general
does NOT have a great success rate when it comes to getting major projects
in on time and on budget. One of the reasons I can still make a living is
because my personal track record in this area is way above the norm. I have
been pretty lucky...<G>)

> Who of you
> old timers ever saw such a thing with COBOL based systems?
 Please Sir...Me sir... I saw COBOL projects fail...often. In fact, it was
EXPECTED to be late. The waterfall level  where the overrun occurred was
simply pushed out a bit to accommodate it, then they could claim it came in
on time. (It did, AFTER the time was re-estimated...)

There are a number of factors here that you are conveniently overlooking.

1. The complexity of the COBOL projects in "Oldene Tymes" was nothing like
the complexity of modern Network based systems. Comparing Batch processing
systems to distributed Client Server systems is like comparing army boots to
rollerblades. Where there is a more complex environment, there is more scope
for failure.

Case in point: I recently spent 2 years (initially in my spare time, but
full time since I arived home in April) designing and building a certain Web
Based application. Hundreds of hours testing it, satisfied it worked as
intended. What should have been a 4 day job to install it on an Internet
exposed LAN, took thirty days. (these are "Dashwood Days"...I tend to go to
bed at 3:00am when I am obsessed by something that doesn't work...<G>, and
am on deck again at 8:00...) The frustrating thing was not my code (it all
worked perfectly); it was grappling with the environment which is built on
the shifting sands of MicroSoft, where every machine requires a different
configuration, and machines that purport to have the same version of the OS
and the same installed software, don't, or if they do, they STILL require
specialised attention. I had to burn the midnight oil because nobody could
support us. (To be fair to MS, everything you ever wanted to know is on MSDN
(and a lot more that you DIDN'T want to know <G>)). We finally got it
working but I learned some very hard lessons.

How many "COBOL programmers" had to learn the intimate details of Component
Services, DCOM+, MTS, and TCP/IP? No, in the "Oldene Tymes" mainframe
installations had (and still have) system specialists to shield application
programmers from the eerie interface with system software. It was a softer,
simpler world.

2. The "Good old days" weren't so good. COBOL development had its problems
too.  Even leaving aside the technical problems and the flaws inherent in
the Waterfall development Methodology, there was the friction between the
Business and the IT people, because the business didn't understand IT, and
the IT people viewed the Business as Luddite Troglodytes.

Noticed how, as "computer literacy" has increased we see less and less of
this now?

(In fact, it's swinging a bit too far the other way, with Users very happy
to bypass IT and implement their OWN solutions using spreadsheets and
Databases...IT are desperate to retain control, and so they implement
practices and procedures that simply slow the whole Corporate system down
and make it less useful.

Last year I had people from my team coming to me on a daily basis
complaining about how certain user departments were eroding IT credibility
by producing their own reports and demanding feeds from IT in order to do
so. It was a delicate situation (I'm not real good with those...<G>) which
we finally resolved by sitting round a table and reviewing what was actually
required by the Business, rather than what they had traditionally been
given. I then ensured that my people got  to work directly with them and in
the end, everyone was happy. The initial response from my own guys had been
"Stop them!".)

> I'll bet
> you there won't be a single current non-trivial VB or Java application
> that will still be in active use in 20 years.

I'll bet you there won't be a single current non-trivial COBOL application
that will still be in active use in 15 years...

>Even 10 years is a very big
> stretch. I have a client/server COBOL inventory system that's been
> running 16 years, and it has a far better chance of still being in use 20
> years from now than the VB/Java stuff being written today.

"Past history is no guarantee of future performance. Consult your investment
advisor."

SUMMARISING :

1. If you use statistics you must quote the Source. (If you quote statistics
that are blatantly ridiculous, don't be surprised if your Source comes in
for some stick <G>)

2. Advocating the use of COBOL as a panacaea for Corporate IT is simply
short sighted and unprofessional. It worked (up to a point) in the past,
when the World was a simpler place. That isn't true anymore. Get over it.
(There IS still a place for traditional COBOL in Batch Processing, but it is
arguable that there will ALWAYS be a requirement for this.)

3. OO COBOL has a future, especially insofar as it can be used to create
components. The opportunity here was recognised by the major COBOL vendors
who have done a brilliant job in providing compilers to support it. It was
lost on the general COBOL community who saw/(see?) it as unnecessary and
complex. (It isn't...either of those...). There is a question mark over how
long the vendors will support a product that hasn't had the uptake by the
marketplace that it deserves. If we lose it, it will be our own fault, and
will mark the death of COBOL.

3. If you intend to be a career IT programmer, expand your skill set. COBOL
alone will simply not cut it. This is a limited career path anyway. If you
are under 30, start studying Accountancy or Corporate Law. (Know programming
and IT but don't rely on it to feed you and yours. The boom is over.
"Computer programming was a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th
Century...").

Pete.




> -- 
> Judson McClendon      judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca...

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

sized software houses can see the advantage of switching
> to
> > .Net, (they take a deep breath and pay up), and in the process, in one
case using N/E, somebody has already mentioned using C#
> > features, rather than COBOL. M/S will of course be delighted - they've
hung up the sticky fly paper, and are just waiting for the
> flies
> > to get attached.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-25T22:20:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Weidnb08Sde2t1miU-KYiQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com> <3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca> <20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com> <3fc3ff33_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>>
> The problem is not with the COBOL language per se, it is with the
> whole "COBOL think" approach to system development.

[...]
>
> Sometimes COBOL IS the best tool; sometimes it ISN'T. If I didn't
> expand my horizons I wouldn't know, and I'd still be sitting
> wondering if COBOL was going to "catch on" again and hoping I could
> find a job with it.
[...]

>
> It staggers me that people who are (for the most part) otherwise very
> bright (I'm talking about COBOL Programmers...), just are not
> prepared to invest a few extra hours of their own time in furthering
> their education.

You're right, of course. Sometimes COBOL is NOT the best tool for the job.
It's not the best tool for changing spark plugs. It's not the best tool for
removing an appendix. It's not the best tool for finding truffles. But it is
the best tool for the kind of jobs I want to do.

I teach computing science at the University level. While not an expert (or
even economically competent), I do have a passing knowledge of other
languages. Most, I simply don't like.

If someone wants to be equally at home selling newspaper subscriptions
door-to-door and smoothing concrete and driving a bus, they should live
their dream. Me, I like writing programs in COBOL. If I can't write it in
COBOL, I'll hire someone else to do it. I don't want to become an expert in
another language or technique. I'm happy and hidebound.

There are those who look askance at folks like me; "Don't want to learn
anything new!" they cry. But, hey, I'm doing what I enjoy and get paid bags
of money to do it. As for those who insist on being able to do everything
(with the right tool, of course), I'm still waiting for them to pull their
own teeth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-11-26T20:23:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fc4552d_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com> <3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca> <20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com> <3fc3ff33_1@news.athenanews.com> <Weidnb08Sde2t1miU-KYiQ@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Weidnb08Sde2t1miU-KYiQ@giganews.com...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> >>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's not the best tool for changing spark plugs. It's not the best tool
for
> removing an appendix. It's not the best tool for finding truffles. But it
is
> the best tool for the kind of jobs I want to do.

Hahaha!
And you know that because?..that's what you know.
To a man with a hammer, everything is a nail...

I would not discount the fact that what YOU want to do, may indeed be best
served by COBOL <G>.

That does not make it wrong for other people to broaden their horizons.

>
> I teach computing science at the University level. While not an expert (or
> even economically competent),

Huh???!!!!!  NOW I start to understand why the industry is in decline <G>...

> I do have a passing knowledge of other
> languages. Most, I simply don't like.
>

Would you pass this personal prejudice on to your students?

> If someone wants to be equally at home selling newspaper subscriptions
> door-to-door and smoothing concrete and driving a bus, they should live
> their dream. Me, I like writing programs in COBOL.

Sadly, that won't get the paper delivered, the bus from A to B, or the
driveway levelled...

> If I can't write it in
> COBOL, I'll hire someone else to do it. I don't want to become an expert
in
> another language or technique. I'm happy and hidebound.
>

Good. I have no problem with that.

> There are those who look askance at folks like me; "Don't want to learn
> anything new!" they cry.

Not me. What you describe is fine for you. You are not trying to make a
career in computer programming. (No need to if you are sitting on large
quantities of MS shares...<G>. Well, not this year anyway...<G>)

> But, hey, I'm doing what I enjoy and get paid bags
> of money to do it. As for those who insist on being able to do everything
> (with the right tool, of course), I'm still waiting for them to pull their
> own teeth.
>

For the same reason my dentist doesn't just use a pair of pliers (he has an
exceptional range of tools and State-of-the Art computer technology), I try
to ensure that I have the range of tools to do the best possible job within
my range of expertise. That certainly includes COBOL (for the moment).
However, the world moves on. The use of computers moves on. The WAY in which
we interact with and use computers moves on...

My dentist is as far removed from Doc Halliday, as I am from the inventor of
the abacus.

And I enjoy what I do, just the same way you do.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-26T08:28:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-AC9CE7.08285826112003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0311251017.7237e99@posting.google.com> <3FC3A985.334ECE5E@shaw.ca> <20031125170024.908$KG@news.newsreader.com> <3fc3ff33_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3fc3ff33_1@news.athenanews.com>,
 "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:


> But, sadly, the COBOL establishment,  (for the most part) not understanding
> the needs of the Network or the Internet, has clearly rejected OO COBOL.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> concept. Their difficulty is in understanding procedural COBOL.

Hmmm, I'm not sure I can agree with you here.  I have seen plenty of 
Java and C++ types that refused to actually learn new and different 
things.  I have come to the conclusion that some large majority (+90% 
maybe) of technical people don't really care to understand why or how 
new things operate -- they are happy with what they learned in school 
and will never expand that knowledge unless forced.  

Many of the Java and C++ folks who learned OO in school will find 
themselves in a similar position in coming decades as some Cobol 
programmers do today -- obsolete by their own lack of self-training.

(And I'm not complaining -- there will always be a job for someone, like 
me, that doesn't mind reading the fine manual and explaining it to 
others.)


> 3. If you intend to be a career IT programmer, expand your skill set. COBOL
> alone will simply not cut it. This is a limited career path anyway. If you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Century...").

That is just depressing.  Give up pragramming for law school?  Why can't 
we make lawyers obsolete instead...

I hope your prediction is incorrect.
```

```
[snip]

>I have come to the conclusion that some large majority (+90% 
>maybe) of technical people don't really care to understand why or how 
>new things operate -- they are happy with what they learned in school 
>and will never expand that knowledge unless forced.  

Mr Zitzelberger, this is a variant of Sturgeon's Law (something along the 
lines of '90% of everything is crap') and similar to a conclusion to which 
I came decades back - based, of course, on the limited experience I had 
then - of 'In *any* profession, doing *any* job, maybe 10% of the 
practioners have 'The Touch'... everyone else is just kind of getting by 
and hoping not to get caught.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-11-26T20:44:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031126154404.08047.00000620@mb-m03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bq2mok$7g5$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
>In article <20031126115931.08047.00000602@mb-m03.aol.com>,
>S Comstock <scomstock@aol.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>We agree?  

Not uncommon, I believe.

>One of us must be wrong, he babbled, Wildely...

Ah, I see. Being your own straight man to set up your pun. Cute, too.


> Mr Comstock, it 
>is, admittedly, a function of our experiences and interpretations, but... 
>'sad'?  It might be said to be no more 'sad' than other observed 
>constants, like 9.8m/sec/sec or 100C or 2.7182818284

Well, I understand what you're saying, but I think you know what I was saying,
too (but I have learned to not be totally assured about reading other's minds).



I teach a lot of classes and see a lot of programmers in different companies,
and there does seem to be a trend towards less interest in working hard (or
even working smart, or even working) than in days of yore. I still find this
business fascinating, and I am continually learning new and amazing things from
you and Pete and Jimmy and many others on this and other newsgroups. So there
are exceptions. But there does seem to be a decline; to me that's sad to see
because I think those with no interest in continual learning are missing a
sense of fun and wonder. But, of course, that's not my problem. 

What's new here in Denver?

My colleague and I have purchased a zPad. That's an IBM Thinkpad laptop
computer running z/OS 1.4 [for the non-mainframers in the group, z/OS is IBM's
64-bit mainframe operating system]. Of necessity, then, I've become a little
bit of a systems programmer.

We host a little intranet and private website on the z/OS software. I'm
finishing up a new course about the latest IBM COBOL compiler, using Unicode
and parsing XML and interoperating with Java. Yup. It's not your father's COBOL
any more.

But my personal favorite language is mainframe Assembler. And the new machines
have all kinds of cool hardware instructions. So fat city.

Happy holidaze to you, too.

Kind regards,


-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
'sad'; I question how 'sad' can be applied to observed constants and then 
give some as examples.  If those examples are not to be labelled as 'sad' 
then I wonder how it is that another constant merits it.

'Fun and wonder' are, it seems, in the mind of the beholder... blessed are 
those who find treasures in pebbles as they will always be surrounded by 
riches.

Most kind of you.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Believe me, you WANT to read this! (Er... perhaps not....)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T11:10:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bq57lp$ge9$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<20031125055735.696$o8@news.newsreader.com> <joe_zitzelberger-AC9CE7.08285826112003@corp.supernews.com> <bq2jqo$m32$1@panix1.panix.com> <3fc50a60_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3fc50a60_4@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bq2jqo$m32$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Say, Doc, are there any caviars...sorry, caveats to that Law <G>?

None that I know of, Mr Dashwood... perhaps that's why it had such a whale 
of an acceptance.

>
>>(something along the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
><G>), this accords with my own experience also (and not just in IT...).

As with Mr Comstock, Mr Dashwood... what is it with this 'sadness' (his 
word) or 'hatred/anti-optimism'?  Do you 'hate to admit' that 
186,282mi/sec is (outside of high-gravity environs) a 'fairly constant' 
thing?  Is it 'against a generally optimistic nature' to say that every 
spring and every autumn there's a day - at about the same time of year - 
that has, more-or-less, 12 hours of darkness and twelve hours of light?

Honestly, I find this a source of amusement and wonder... one observed
phenomenon ('at sea level unpressurised, uncontaminated water boils at
100C') is considered 'a matter of nature', there's no sense in appplying
any emotion to it because... well, because if it ain't 'just Is' then it
is close enough, based on experience, to be considered 'just Is'... 'at
sea level unpressurise, uncontaminated water boils at 100C, this 
phenomenon, for whatever reason... just Is'.

Another observed phenomenon - 'In *any* profession, doing *any* job, maybe 
10% of the practioners have 'The Touch'... everyone else is just kind of 
getting by and hoping not to get caught' - is seen to be almost as 
universal as this boiling-water stuff... but *this* one makes people 'sad' 
and they 'hate to admit it'.

What might be the reasons for this discrepancy in interpretation?

(Thanks, all for this joyful bit of Holidazed Puzzlement!)

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
