#### ↳ Re: Mainframer needs help on Power Cobol

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-27T16:30:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q8CdnVFHUOho51uiU-KYkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<PEmxb.341930$0v4.18881380@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:
> I'm currently working on the design of a work-flow system, and I am
> suggesting that we create a prototype using Power-COBOL. The system
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciated.

PowerCobol (PC) project files contain everything and are not (really
searchable). You can GREP a PC Project file (PPJ) to find out whether a
string exists inside it, then start up the project and use the project's
search routine to find a bit of code.

You could also store your source in an ordinary text file and include the
name of the external file in the PPJ where it will be fetched at compile
time (think COPY), thereby making the file searchable by external routines.

We use GEM (a sort-of Source Safe) provided by FJ. Because PPJ formats are
unknown to GEM(!), GEM treats PPJ files as miscellaneous binary files - no
versioning, no roll-back, no deltas, etc.

In sum, PC stores its source files in PPJ format which is unknown to anybody
and cannot be manipulated by anything other than PC.

So far, its not be a big problem for us.
```

## Thread: Experienced mainframer seeks position — 1 msgs

### Experienced mainframer seeks position

- **From:** "Dan Connolly" <DCONNOLLY@kc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-11-28T17:28:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MALxb.69104$Vu6.8612@twister.rdc-kc.rr.com>`

```
Any interested parties:

Please review my skills and experience in light of any open positions.  I am willing to relocate at my own expense and can start at any time.

I apologize if this is not the right forum to post a resume to.  Kansas City is not a good IT job market, especially with Sprint laying off more workers this holiday season.

6545 County Line Road

Shawnee, KS 66216

Phone 913/375-1538

E-mail dancon212@yahoo.com

Daniel F. Connolly

 

Objective

To obtain a challenging IT programmer/analyst position at a level compatible with my skills and with an opportunity for advancement. 

 

Education

 

Missouri Southern State University  1994 - 1997

BS/Information Technology.   3.6 GPA in Major with a business minor.

Instruction in Pascal, C, C++, RPG, COBOL, Composer Case Tool, Visual Basic, Database Management, AS/400, and System Analysis and Design

 

Experience

United States Marine Corps  1971-1982

 

Aviation Supply Data Processing Non-Commissioned Officer and Officer.  Developed COBOL programs, laison between the Supply Department and Computer Operations, NCOIC of day-to-day operations of a 15 person computer department.  Maintained Secret clearance.  Attended college classes at night.  Discharged with the rank of Chief Warrant Officer 2.

 

American Multi-Cinema, Inc.  920 Main St., Kansas City, MO (May 1997 - January 2003)

 

COBOL programmer/analyst on an open DEC Alpha VMS system.

As primary IT contact for the Advertising, Booking, Film Trailer, and Payables systems, developed and modified programs, supported the user community, and defined scope and requirements for submitted projects.

 

Six month project developing and installing an interface between a Delphi Booking system (Oracle) and the legacy COBOL Payables system (VMS). Performed maintenance on the Delphi applications within the Booking system.

 

Maintained and monitored Enterprise Project Management system for IT.  Utilized Modular SQL, Delphi, DCL, CMS, Cognos Powerhouse database maintenance and report package in support of the above activities.  Attended AMC sponsored classes for Delphi 5 and Modular SQL instruction.  While at AMC I attended a Java class at William Jewel College, took an internet Java class at Ziff-Davis Univ., and attended the Borland 11th annual conference in 2000.  Self instructed on JBuilder 3.5, Access 2000, Microsoft Front Page, and HTML.

 

In January, 2003 I left AMC to start a business of my own.
```

<a id="t-2ad8db9c27"></a>
## Thread: Accept Date YYYYMMDD — 3 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Accept Date YYYYMMDD

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-11-30T00:02:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FC930F5.AD4E9475@shaw.ca>`

```
When in doubt, ask the chair at J4 :-

"The YYYYMMDD phrase on the ACCEPT statement was not part of the 1985
standard.  It was first standardized in the 2002 ISO standard.  However,

long before that, J4 agreed to "freeze" this new feature that was
developed
for the 2002 so that compiler writer's could implement it before the
year
2000 and users could make use of this feature, knowing that it would be
portable among implementations.  ".

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept Date YYYYMMDD

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-11-30T10:10:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0311301010.a8f3b0e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3FC930F5.AD4E9475@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote

> "The YYYYMMDD phrase on the ACCEPT statement was not part of the 1985
> standard.  It was first standardized in the 2002 ISO standard.  However,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> portable among implementations.  ".

But how many compiler writers did actually implement it ?  MF did,
though it is not documented in OCDS 4.1.  Fujitsu did not even in 7.
```
